neither by the traditions of antiquity, nor by the religious hierarchy which fought
against temporal power, nor by Scholastic philosophy, nor by feudalism and its
chivalry, nor by Protestantism, nor finally by anything of that which worked
heavily upon the minds of the West - well, can such a nation be constructed
differently from that of people who lived, grew up and still live under the effect
of all the other things? Some men of genius, some elite minds, in order to rise
up among us, have undoubtedly only to make something of this heritage of
thought and of feelings. But it is not any less regrettable that the nation en masse
has been without any real heritage from all these antecedents by the historical
arrangement of the world. No doubt, the moral action of Christianity was
powerfully exerted upon us, but as regards its logical action it is impossible not
to recognize that it was almost nil in our country. Let us add that that is one of
the most interesting questions which the philosophy of our history will have to
occupy itself the day when that philosophy will come into the world.
   One forgets too often that the Saviour did not come into the world -to
propose enigmas for it, but to give the world the very word "enigma".
    There are people who tell you that the Christian religion is not meant to get
mixed up in the social order, that Christianity has done nothing for society, that
Christianity should do nothing for society, that Christianity addresses itself only
to the individual, and that the goods which it promises refer only to future life.
In effect, what has Christianity taught men that could have some relationship to
their welfare in this world? Nothing at an:   -it taught men only that they were
brothers, that's all.
   One must surely say it, there is such a way of loving one's country that even
the most unworldly is capable of:  witness M. V.12 Above all, one owes the
truth to one's country as to one's friends.B
   What should be the name of this child?
   Do you admit many kinds of civilizations?14
   Do you believe that a country, which at the very moment when it is called
upon to seize the future rightfully belonging to it, instead goes astray to the point
of completely allowing this future to be lost by its own clumsy hands, is truly
worthy of this future?15
   Do you believe that the old Orient such as history has made it and the very
nature of the human spirit could be replaced by a new Christian Orient?
   Are you really certain that this child could not have been a monster?
    Scarcely less than a half a century ago the sovereigns of Russia ceased to
distribute by the thousands the cultivators of the state lands to their courtesans. 16
But, I ask you, how could the most simple notions of justice, law, or of any sort
of legality take root under a regime which from one day to the next transformed
whole populations of free men into slaves! Thanks to the liberal sovereign17
whom we saw among us, to the generous conqueror whom we embraced with
our favors, this odious application of absolute power in that which is the most
malevolent for the people, the perversion of their social reason, is not practiced
any more in Russia.  But alone the presence of the servitude such as  it is

--- PDF page 122 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                                119


constituted here continues to hold, soil and debase everything in this country.
No one escapes its fatal influence, and perhaps least of all the sovereign.
Surrounded since the cradle by men, possessors of their fellow men whose
fathers were serfs themselves the breath of slavery penetrates his being through
all his pores and influences his intelligence even more actively as he believes
himself sheltered from its ·action.18 For example, it would be a grave error to
imagine that its action is only exercised upon this unfortunate part19 which
suffers the oppressive weight.  It is completely the opposite, namely, one must
study its influence upon the classes which profit from it, not upon those which
suffer from  it.20  Thanks to his eminently ascetic beliefs, thanks to the
temperament of his race, little concerned for an uncertain improvement, and
finally thanks to the spaces which often separate him from his master, the
Russian serf, one must admit, has not as much to complain about as one might
believe. Moreover, his current situation is only the natural result of his previous
situation.  It is not violence which reduced him to servitude, it is the logical
march of things unfolding from the basis of his intimate life, from his religious
feelings, from his nature. Do you want proof of it? Look at the free man in
Russia! No apparent difference between him and the serf. In fact, I find in the
resigned air of the latter something more dignified, more at ease than in the
vague and worried look of the former.  The fact is that there is nothing in
common between Russian slavery and that which has existed or which still
exists in other countries of the world.21 Constituted22 as it was in antiquity as it
is in our day in the United States of America it had no other .effect than that
which naturally results from this detestable institution, misery of the slave,
corruption of the master; but the effect of slavery in Russia is very different in a
vast way.                                                                    /
  We recently observed, even though he is a slave in the full sense of the
word, the Russian serf does not bear the mark on his person.  He is not
separated from the other classes of society either by customs, public opinion, or
racial difference; in the home of his master he divides the work with the
freeman23, in the countryside he lives mixed together with the peasants from the
free communes, everywhere he is found inter-mingled with the free subjects of
the empire without any apparent distinction. Everything in Russia bears the
stamp of servitude, customs, tendencies, instruction and even liberty itself - in
so far as any liberty can even be said to exist in this milieu.24
   The more one thinks about  it, the more one becomes persuaded that
something very extraordinary is happening now in our midst. This thing which
has not even yet come to the state of a simple idea, since it has not yet any well-
drafted expression, contains, however, a social question of high import.   It
concerns nothing less than to know whether a nation - once having recognized
that it had taken the wrong route for a century - can one fine day by the simple
act of a reflective will-power return upon its footpath, rip it up and recommence
its career, rebind the broken thread of its life at the place where the nation left it
one day - one does not know too much about how. But one must admit we are

--- PDF page 123 / 323 ---

120                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


on the eve if not of a revolution of this singular problem then at least of a
tentative solution, to experiment with a social operation which the most ardent
utopists have not even dared to dream about in their most insolent dreams.  It
would undoubtedly be very hazardous to assign the probable epoch of this
event, for, as popular emotions do not obey the dynamic laws of the universe at
 all, the event is not based on the power of the usual forces. On the contrary, it is
rather due to the powerlessness of society that social movement is produced
most often.   It is permissible to believe that the moment of a brilliant
manifestation of national feeling, at least from the literate fraction of the nation,
is quite near. Perhaps you are going to believe that it is a revolution like those of
Western Europe which menace us - be reassured: we are not there at all, thank
God. The Western world and we have parted from too different points to ever
end up in identical results.  Moreover, there is something immobile in the
Russian people, something necessarily inalterable and that is its indifference
towards the nature of the power which rules it. No people in the world has
better comprehended than we this famous scriptural text: all power comes from
God.25 Established power is always sacred for us.26 It is well-known that it is
the family which is the basis of our social system:  the Russian people thus are
able to see in power nothing else but paternal authority exercised with more or
less rigor, that's all.  Every prince, no matter who he is, is for the people, a
father. For example, we do not say, I have the right to do this, we say, such a
thing is permitted: something else is not.  In our ideas it is not the law which
punished the culpable citizen, it is a father chastizing the intractable child. Our
taste for the domestic regime is such that we squander with happiness the rights
of paternity upon all those on whom we depend.27 The idea of legitimacy, the
idea of law, for the Russian people, is nonsense, for example, observe this mess
of bizarre successions which followed the reign of Peter the Great, and
especially the appalling episode of the inter-regnuIl1.  It is clear that, if it had
been in the nature of the nation to understand these ideas, she would have
understood that the prince for whom she shed her blood had no right to the
throne, and from then on the first usurper, as well as all the others, could not
have found these multitudes of adherents, whose ravages caused even the
foreign bands to tremble in their turn.28 No force in the world could ever make
us depart from the order of ideas which has made all our history, which still
makes all the poetry of our existence, which admits only a granted law and
rejects every kind of natural law . Thus, although something of it could come to
the superior levels of society, the people en bloc will never take part in it: hands
crossed, a favorite attitude of the man of pure Russian blood, he will see his new
masters made and will -salute them,according to his custom, with the title of
fathers; for one must not be mistaken, it will still be masters whom one must
give him: any other regime he will reject with disdain or hatred.
   What does the new school want?29 To rediscover, to restore the national
principle which the nation in a moment of distraction, it is said, allowed it once
to be filched by Peter the Great, a principle without which in any case no true

--- PDF page 124 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                                  121


progress is possible for any people whomsoever.  It is certain - and we are the
first to agree - that nations like individuals30 could not advance a foot in the
career of the progress or of the development which they are destined to pursue
without a profound sense of their individuality without the awareness of what
they are, even more nations could not even exist deprived of this sense, of this
awareness: but there is precisely what proves the error of your teaching, for a
people never lost its nationality without ceasing at the same time to exist; but, if I
am not mistaken, we exist a bit.31
   The more one reflects about the influence which Christianity exercised on
society, the more one remains persuaded that in the general plan of providence
the Western church was principally instituted in view of the social development
of humanity, and that her history is only a logical deduction of the organizing
principal deposed in its breast.32 One can condemn the means which the
Western church used to achieve its goal, but one should recognize that these
means were not only the most efficacious but even the sole practical ones for the
different epochs which it had traversed.  Faithful to its mission, the Western
church never detoured from the impulse which was imprinted on  it.  An
admirable instinct concerning its role guided it constantly across the centuries of
battles, disasters, and unheard-of triumphs.  Finally, if one is incapable of
denying that the Western church was ambitious and intolerant and that it did not
disdain the goods of the earth, one must agree also that it is upon these
conditions alone that God's design was accomplished.
     Isn't the realization of the idea confided in one what counts above all else?
Thus, all should be naturally subordinated to this supreme rule; is it not absurd
to reproach the Western church for having been consequential, ardent,
passionate in its task? Where would the world be if the Western church had only
tossed back and forth between the different heresies which arose from the first
days of its establishment and which did not cease to surge around it all the way
to the time when the heresies all ended absorbed in this great heresy of the
sixteenth century33 which claimed to establish the non-existence of its impalpable
church in the place of the reality of the absolute church? If you ask me whether a
perfect awareness of the work being accomplished around it was in the heart of
each of its members, I would say to you that I don't think so, given the fact that
this awareness will not be possible in God's Church until it will have run the
entire cycle of human reason, until after having exhausted the entire cycle of the
finite spirit, it will enter finally, never to leave, into the order of the infinite
spirit. But one could not, however, doubt that the Western church always had a
profound feeling, a feeling which was concentrated in some elite souls, in some
spirits crowned with the sacred halo, who enlightened the entire sphere of
intelligences composing the Christian community and penetrated each of them
with intense energy, with all the truth of an intimate and personal feeling.
   The eastern church was evidently destined for something completely
different.  It should consequently march in other ways.  Its particular role was to
make visible the power of Christianity reduced to its sole forces; it perfectly

--- PDF page 125 / 323 ---

122                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


filled this sublime mission. Come into the world on the winds of the desert,
then transported into another desert where, living amid the solitude which
barbarism had made for it, it had naturally to become ascetic and contemplative
above all else.34 Thus every kind of ambition was forbidden to it by the very
nature of its origin.  Also, one must admit, the eastern church pushed
submission to excess, also it was pleased to efface itself in all imaginable ways,
to kneel before all the princes whoever they were, faithful or unfaithful,
orthodox or schismatic, Mongol or Seljuk, and when the oppression became
excessive, or when the foreign yoke came to beat upon the eastern church, rarely
did it know how to do anything but inundate their temple courtyards with tears,
or, prostrate in the dust to invoke the aid of heaven in mental prayer. All that is
perfectly true, but it is also true that the eastern church could not do otherwise,
that it would have lost its destiny had it tried to assume another character. Only
one time in the midst of the glories of the Russian patriarch, it dared to be
ambitious, and we know what this whim of pride against nature cost the eastern
church.35 No matter what it is to this church so humble, so resigned, so
prostrate that our country owes not only the most beautiful pages of its history
but even its preservation. And here then is the lesson which the eastern church
has been called to give to the world: a great nation completely engendered by
Christ's religion, such is the interesting spectacle which we offer to the
meditation of serious spirits!36
  We have before us the work of a young French philosopher, a man of much
spirit in many ways, profoundly detaching himself from the philosophical band
of his country. But unfortunately he is also completely ignorant concerning that
which is going on beyond the Rhine and judges philosophy, in general, based
on that which is professed in the College de France. But, one must admit, the
country which saw the father of modern philosophy born has not sheltered its
own heritage:   it is neither to Mr. Cousin37 nor to Mr. Jouffroy38 that this
beautiful succession will become awarded in our day.
   Our author, for example, cries out against the claim of a well-known school
in France which would wish to abolish personal feeling in order to replace it
with social feeling. In that he is perfectly correct, but he is wrong to confound
this puerile doctrine with the philosophical doctrines of Germany. We take the
liberty of assuring him that there is nothing in common between the humanitarian
theories of France and a serious philosophy which  is preoccupied with
completely different things outside of private questions of society, in which
logical and political anarchies have come to meet. What this philosophy seeks is
to determine the link which exists between the external world and the internal
world, to reconcile the idea with reality, questions which the human spirit has
posed since time immemorial, questions which Christianity resolved in its own
way, with the instru;nents of faith but which have not yet been resolved with
those of reason.  Thus, one can say that its work is the work of Christianity,
transported or contillUed on the terrain of pure thought, so one can see that this is
far cry from Fourierism, from human living in barracks in phalansters.  Social

--- PDF page 126 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                                123


questions are found at the end of that, true, seeing that all the solutions to the
problem of life are contained therein, but in the philosophic order the solutions
should enfold from themselves, once the spirit wiHbedefinitively constituted,
the idea achieved, the logical fact accomplished:  until then philosophy has
absolutely nothing to say about these matters.
     I believe that there is enough general agreement on this point, that the moral
individual, engendered by Christianity, is made differently from the way the
moral individual of paganism was made: Very well, it is the logical individual
that philosophy is occupied at reconstructing today. Thus, who speaks to you
about annihilating the me, to substitute in its place some abstract awareness or
other of humanity en masse?  It all comes down only to rebuilding it on a more
secure basis, that's all.  If you wish to know what is the process by which
philosophy counts on coming to this desired result, here it is: Philosophy first
looks for something around itself in which the great synthetic factor which it
seeks to realize could be found already realized or rather something in which the
separation of the two elementary principles of the world and their apparent
antagonism never took place, and it is in the idea of absoLute existence that
philosophy finds its unknown. For absolute existence belol1gs only to absolute
spirit: hence there is the type which it is henceforth going to study, meditate
upon, and analyze. Philosophy gathers in its manifestations everywhere where
it will find them, it will return to them one by one, it will pass through all the
centuries,  it will arrive  at the actual  fact,  the limmense resume of the
accomplished time, it will try finally to discover the fact which is not yet realized
but which already hovers in the nebulous future; then it will hand over all this
work to human thought and will invite it to continue it, following the methods
which it will have furnished to it.  Thus, far from destroying the subject first
after having become perfectly penetrated with the awareness of its nature, it is
upon the subject,on the contrary, that philosophy imposes all its task, whereas,
on the other hand, in giving us the clear perception of the object, philosophy
releases them one by one from their vague abstraction, introduces them to
reality, and in this manner works out this accord between them which it should
fmd only at the end of its labor in a certain way from that very moment on. Such
is the philosophy of our days.

   Among4o the causes which thwart our intellectual development and impose
upon ita particular character one must observe two things, the lack of these
centers, of these hearths where the living forces of the country are accumulated,
where ideas are expanded, from which fertile principles lighten up the surface of
the soil; and the lack of these standards around which are grouped masses of
imposing and compact intellects. An idea arrives from I don't know where,
borne by some misguided breeze, breaks through somehow all sorts of barriers,
starts to filter in an unperceived moment across our minds, and then one day it
evaporates and goes off to become engulfed in some obscure comer of our

--- PDF page 127 / 323 ---

124                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


national intelligence, never to reappear again: such is the march of ideas in our
country.41 Each people carries in its breast a particular element which imprints
its seal upon its social being, which determines its pilgrimage across the
centuries and marks its place in humanity.42 This constituted element in our case
is the geographical element, there -that is what people don't want to understand.
Our entire history is produced by the nature of the immense topography which
has fallen as our 10t.43  It is the nature of the immense topography which causes
us to diverge in countless directions and scatters us in space from the very first
days of our existence; it also imposes on us a blind submission to the force of
things, to every power which proclaimed itself our master. Thus in a milieu
made in such a way there is no regular, daily contact of intellects.  In this
complete isolation of individual reason there is no logical development of reason,
no spontaneous elan of the mind towards a possible amelioration, none of these
general common feelings among souls which might reunite them in unison, in
enormous blocs before which all the material powers must necessarily bow. In a
word, we are nothing but a geological product of the vast latitudes in which
some unknown centrifical force launched us. We are but a curious page in the
physical geography of the earth. Moreover, as long as our material importance
is enormous in the world, so long our moral importance is null. We are a very
great political fact and the most minimal of moral facts.  Nevertheless, this
physiology of the country, which harbors such great inconveniences no doubt in
the present, can offer great advantages in the future and in deceiving oneself
about the first, one risks to deprive oneself of the second.44
     a. Cause and effect are not different; the principle and the product are the
same thing. One could not exist without the other, for the principle isn't such
except in so far as it produces such an effect or engenders such a thing.  In the
individual often predominance of one ·orof the ·other .occurs:  it is ·only in the

    In this first part of his system or rather in the first epoch of his philosophical
career Fichte claimed that there was nothing real in the world but knowledge,
and as all knowledge is necessarily contained in the me, there was really nothing
existing but the me.  Opposed to this doctrine is the fact that knowledge
supposes naturally a known object, and consequently there was still something
else other than the me and knowledge. Later Fichte abandoned this teaching,
without however acknowledging it openly, without perhaps even acknowledging
it to himself, since he never ceased to consider his new point of view as a

--- PDF page 128 / 323 ---

corollary of the first. No matter what, if one wishes to evaluate the teachings of
Fichte well, one must only adhere to that which we willingly call his second way
and consider the first part of the system only as a preliminary study, a powerful
analysis of the nature of the me or of the subject, a study which belongs to
history.
   Do you believe that it would be useful for Europe as also for Russia herself
that this latter were the arbitrator of the world? Another question. Between the

   What is this war? A family quarrel between true believers.  [Translator's

      I am of the opinion of Mgr. Innocent47; I do not doubt that the scepter of the

     It is very permissible, I believe, that every good Russian who seriously
loves his country ought to owe a grudge, in this supreme moment, against those
people who pushed it by their direct -or indirect influence, into this disastrous
war, who misunderstood her moral and material resources, who took their
systems for the true politics of the country, their incomplete studies for the true
national feelings, finally in intoning their premature triumphal songs, misled
public opinion when there was still time to stop it on its rapid pathway where it
was swept along by giddiness or by blundering.
     It is permissible, I believe, in the presence of our disasters not to share the
tendencies of an extravagant patriotism which has brought the country to the
brink of the precipice which the country believes it can get out of by persisting in
its dreams and refusing to lose hope in the situation created for the country by
it.48
     It is permissible to think, I believe, that when providence calls a people to
great destinies, she furnishes at the same time the means to accomplish those
great destinies, she causes powerful intellects to arise from within the people in
order to direct them, she illuminates the entire nation with brilliant lights and
does not deliver her future to the incapacity of her chiefs, to the infatuation of
some lazy heads, nourished by success in the salons and among coteries.
      It is very permissible, I believe, for every good Russian, who prefers the
good of his country over the triumph of some fashionable ideas, to remark that
in the universe there exist only two countries both afflicted by a national party
and that one of these countries is at the point of disappearing from the world
scene thanks to this very patriotism and that the other js menaced by seeing itself
stripped of the title of power of the first rank, the fruit of a century of generous
and perservering efforts, of wisdom and of courage.

   Thank God, I have not aided my country in going astray either in prose or in

     It is thought that we have something to do to France, or to England. Stupid.
It is to civilization, to the entire civilization that we have something to do and not
only to the results of this civilization but to this civilization itself, as instrument,
as belief, exerted, worked out, perfected in a thousand years of laborious effort.
There is what we have something to do, we who date from yesterday, among

memory.50
     It is neither the character of the English nation nor its wisdom which
constitutes it; it is its history. But once constituted, it is natural that the English
people are wiser than other people. The Anglo-Saxon race is not a privileged
race51; it is England which is a privileged country whose history is a bit of good
luck from one end to the other.  It is not the English people who bestowed upon
itself its constitution, it is the Norman barons who tore it from their Norman
kings. The old institutions of Alfred52 would have served for nothing without
the sword of the feudal baron.  Previously one said that it was the institutions
which made the people, today one says that it is the people who make the
institutions.
   One and the other is true. But one must appreciate the factual chronology.
First history makes the institution; then the educated people continue the work of
history by their institutions, they achieve or debase it in so far as they are more
or less gifted .. Such is the natural march of the social movement. To make
people into privileged races or cursed races is absurd .

    There is no right except that of prescription. In the moral order-as in nature,
nothing is done today which was not done yesterday. The link: which joins
together moral things among themselves is the same which joins physical things:
continuity, succession. Something new, never.
   Whether that is the right of my spirit, whether it is that of the world - that is
what I don't know and it does not matter; what I know is that outside of this
circle, I cannot imagine anything by myself. How could I have a right at this
hour which I did not have a moment before? From where would it have come to
me?  I can acquire a new right undoubtedly, but this is not the kind of right
referred to, it is from this then that this right is acquired, from the primitive right,
from the right which gives the right. Thus, the metaphysics of right does not
consist at all in demonstrating to whom such and such a right belongs, but to
make it very clear where such and such a right resides .by the v.ery nature .of
things.
   For example, if a man came into the world with evident superiority over all
his fellow humans, a natural right of superiority would revert to him. But, as all
men are born with the same capacities, there is no natural superiority in any
sense at all. However, since man is made in such a way that he can be put in
possession of a real superiority over other men in the course of his life, the right
of authority necessarily resides in the hands of some among us: one cannot
create it, one can, however, constitute it, but that is all that it is possible to do.
And this right does not belong to the individual who fmds himself by chance
invested with it; it resides in the power of nature of which the person is in
possession. To call this right there into question, wquldbe·the ·sarneas, calling
into question the force which weighs down bodies, which retains the sun, which
makes the world turn. Such is the principle which bases all power in society;
not certainly, as has been imagined, some sort of tacit or articulated pact which
never could have taken place rationally since society was already all made.
    Material possession has no other foundation either. What does one do when
one disputes the ownership of a thing with someone? One proves that he did not
possess it the moment before, that's all.
     If one could reach to the first link: in the chain which Jupiter holds in his
hand, one would come to the beginning of everything, of right as of everything
else. Meanwhile let us recognize that in this world, the past makes the present;
thus, one must seek principle and cause of everything in time: nothing outside
of time.
    Finally right can only accord liberty to act in conformity with the universal
law; that is why the first of all is that of its own conservation. For, general law
is  all comprised in continuity, in the persistence of beings: mechanical
continuity, vital continuity, intellectual continuity, moral.continuity, .all.the
modes of universal existence. In the clear idea of this existence is found the rule
of every moral fact, as the real law of the physical world would be found in the
clear idea of physical life.

--- PDF page 131 / 323 ---

128                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS

    In human reason there is something so necessary that, if one were to remove
it, there would no longer be reason.   It is with that that reason begins to
experiment, all its subsequent action is only a result of this first act.
    This something is certain notions which are like the organs of intelligence.
One calls them faculties of the soul. But what are these faculties of the soul? Is
there anything in the human spirit but ideas, again ideas and always ideas? Is the
human spirit anything else but the ensemble of ideas? And how is an jdea
produced if not by flowing from another idea? A strange fantasy! To want to
amount to the origin of our ideas is question of experience, of empiricism. Since
we do not preserve the memory of our first years, then how can you expect to
pursue human reason up to its birth? No way.  Since we ourselves are not
witnesses of that which happens in us from the beginning then who could be?
As for the speculative philosopher observing the child, in order for him to
conceive of that which occurs in this little brain, he would have to have been
philosopher and child at the same time, or he would have had to retain the
memory of that which occured in his own brain at this time. And still, what
good would it do? Thus study the embryo in the mother's womb, for it is then
that life begins, not by the light of day.
    People ask when intelligence came to the human being.  Well, what do I
know about it? What I know is that man would not have any of this intelligence
at any age of his life any more than at the age of the embryo, unless in one way
or another, intelligence was poured into him from outside.
    Every system be it psychological, ideological, anthropological etc., dealt
only with isolated man, individual man, alone among his fellow creatures:  that
is physiology, natural history, but that is not philosophy.  Philosophy only
knows man made by man in the course of time.
    Vanity makes the drunkard, pride the naughty.  The same individual is
stupid or ferocious, accordingly as he is filled by vanity or by pride. Wait, the
moment of the excess has passed? Well then he becomes reasonable and good.
   Most men appear to us not as they really are, but such as we make them.
That is because we always put a stake in their vanity or their pride. But there is:a
motive to hold a grudge only against ourselves, which is what angers us in our
relationships with our fellow humans.
   As for ourselves, well, one finds that one has been either foolish in
misunderstanding oneself or else wicked in detesting oneself, and sometimes
good and nice in bowing before oneself. And all that for what?  It's because one
has been either vain or arrogant or else neither one nor the other. Perhaps one is
never vain and proud at the same time, I don't know, but as one was for a
moment, one could be both drunk and cruel at the same time: and having come
to that, to return to one's true disposition, one could not, for nothing would
remain in the soul to enlighten it, neither heart nor reason.
   Such a man is in such a situation that his vanity is continually excited, his
pride continually offended. That person is never what he effectively is, not even
in his dreams; in every moment of his life he is what the deadly situation in
which he is thrown makes of him.  It is from these situations that inaccessible

--- PDF page 132 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                                129

characters, so tiring to see, so unfortunate to themselves, are made. Frequently
in society one meets often good people, even sometimes people of merit, but
spoiled by their evil star and by their inability to know -how to extract themselves
from circumstances. They are hated; they should be pitied.
   The most favorable position in my opinion, for the calm of passions for the
complete utilization of our faculties is a well-thought-outsubmission, willingly
deferred to a perfectly legitimate authority. For a crowd of people, for example,
the most desirable thing would be to be under the influence of a man whose
judgment and character they have learned to respect, where in order to be happy
they need only know how to submit to live under the shadow of a reason which
is not theirs.  They refrain, more miserable than submissive.  Such is the
majority of men.
   Must one consider inspirationS3 as a phenomenon so supernatural that it
annihilates the ordinary course of nature? Not at all.  It is enough to regard it as
a consequence of the direct influence of an unknown principle upon the forces of
moral nature, by means of which they acquire an intensity infinitely more
considerable than they would have had in their given state.
    Should one understand that this exaltation of the intelligent power does not
come from the creature but from the creator, that it is co-ordinated with a general
plan, that it does not end with an individual effect but that it relates to a universal
effect, as everything immediately emanated from God, -then one would be
perfectly orthodox, and then one would have even more of an advantage over the
excessive dogma in that better than it, one would comprehend the object of faith.
Likewise, revealed knowledge is nothing else but knowledge superior to the
knowledge acquired by the common ways of reason:   in no way  is  it
supernatural knowledge.
    In manifesting itself to man's spirit, God does not communicate Himself
completely: no one has seen the/ace o/the Father. Hence there is no reversal of
the given order:  prodigious augmentation of natural powers, that's all. The
impulse which has been given in primitive times to it is renewed a second time
by the same hand which gave it the impulse in the first place. Where is the
miracle?                                                                                                           I
    Moreover, does one know well all the means of knowledge which the soul
possesses? Does one know all the combinations, all the possible effects of its
faculties? Why, in certain epochs could not new forces, new capacities be
developed by the concourse of particular circumstances reborn again in human
nature? In other times could they not be extinguished for lack of nourishment or
of exercise? Another time could they not appear agahl and ever so according to a
determined plan of providence? What would there still be to marvel.at about
that?  Finally, if there is anything in man such as a free will, it should have
certainly some analogue, some identity with the supreme will, nothing but power
of liberty also, no more, no less. And then how to know that which this human
will can obtain of power, energy, and expanse when it comes to meet with the
other will, to become fused with it or to become lost in it?

130                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


    Finding himself in England without preamble, without any warning, a
foreigner feels himself singularly offended by all the mechanisms of this
industrial machine which forms the exterior life of the English. No thought to
communicate with; an enormous movement, that is all that he finds; moreover
nothing with which to sympathize.  The fact is that only active thought is
produced externally in England, reflective thought, tranquil thought is found
there relegated to the most intimate sections of the domestic interior or of the
soul; it is there that one must go to seek it. And then having arrived at this
intimate section, the singular mix of reserve and of eccentricity which
characterizes the English spirit, so well made to derail the newcomer, will push
you away undoubtedly still more. But also once installed in the hearth of Old
Eng land, a crowd of happiness and sweet sympathies will come to surround you
and compensate for the ennui of the first encounter. And once the day comes
when you pronounce the word home - at the heart of an English family in the
lovely country house with the green lawn, with beech trees and oak trees - as the
native Englishman pronounces it, then I don't doubt that you will let go without
regret from your memory the remembrance of your country, even if this country
were dear Russia.54
    In Germany one sails along incessantly on the ocean of abstraction; the
German is there more at home, more at ease than on earth; that is why there one
carries intemperance of thought to the extreme. A very simple thing; why should
pure thought without application, without body be restrained in its sailing?
Where is the danger? When pure thought wishes to enter in life, when it
becomes practical, when from the lofty region in which is soars, it comes to
confront positive reality, then it must necessarily become moderated.  But
without that all the infinite space of nature would not besufficient.for.it. _Flaring
up above all realities, it is always going to flare up: no reason for it to stop ever.
   However, one must admit, there is prodigious happiness in these endless
voyages of the soul. And I believe that it is only by this forgetting, by this
negligence of the real that pure thought can acquire all the elan of which it is
capable and achieve as a final result the most lofty knowledge that it is given to
achieve in the portion of its life that it is forced to pass here below.
   The Verb! Then what is the Verb? See the pilot maneuvering a boat amid
the rocks, turning it and returning it, as he would a piece of wood floating on the
water:  there are some words that he -prefers from moment to moment which
make that. That is the Verb. See on the battlefield a hundred battalions set in
motion at once and falling on the enemy:  it is a sign, a gesture of the general
which has made that.  There again is the Verb.  Think about this otherwise
powerful word which, more distinctly than any human voice is able to make
itself heard in limited space, resounding in all the infinity of nature. And that
there is the perfect Verb. Thus, the Verb is the acting word, the creating word.
   One imagines that the Biblical prophecies are only simple predictions,
announcements concerning the future, nothing more. A grave error. They are

--- PDF page 134 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                                  131

teachings, teaching relating to all times; essential parts of the doctrine as much as
the rest
   When the Holy Spirit spoke through the mouth of the prophets He did not
remake human nature.  For, man's heart is once made in such a way that it can
presage the future only by deduction from the present and from a given past.
Unless the human heart were to cease being what it is, a reasonable nature acting
by its own power, the human heart could not do otherwise. Thus it is this
rigorous liaison of the future with the past and the present, ·hidden from the
majority of men which was given to the seers of Israel to perceive clearly, more
clearly I would say than to the rest of mankind. And as this liaison was
constant, necessary, absolute, it is naturally the same today, even tomorrow,
even always.  Circumstances, perfectly similar situations lead at all times to
perfectly similar results. Thus, the prophet's lesson is meant for all time and for
all places, provided one knows how to apply it suitably.
    Undoubtedly this rigorous resemblance of epochs is not an easy thing to
grasp. A profound feeling, an intimate awareness of the w.ays .of God, coming
from an infinite submisssion to the manifestations of his will can alone U1iveil it.
The same superior principle that causes the gift of prophecy also gives the
intelligence. of the prophecy. The prophet and his interpreter are placed on the
same line in the intellectual heirarchy. He isa prophet who understands
perfectly the prophet.55
    For example, people have wished to trace the lofty predictions of the
Apocalypse to the epochs of particular times: simpleminded foolishness. The
thought of the Apocalypse is only an immense lesson, absolutely being applied
to each moment of infinite duration and to that which one sees every day
happening around .oneself. These frightening v:oices rev:erberate from there, .one
hears them every day. These horrible monsters which are shown there to us,
one sees them every day. This cracking .of .the w.orld'smachine which is made
there, we are perpetually witnesses of it. In a word, everyday drama, eternal for
the world, there that is the superb poem of St. John.56 And, the denouement of
this drama, not as in those products of our imagination, but according to the law
of the infinite, is perpetual and begins from the opening of the scene.
   The dreamers who have interpreted the Apocalypse had an obligatory
mission. All the foolishness to which the sacred book gave place, have never
been vain, there was a reason for everything. For example, the millenarians
wer~ absolutely necessary; without millenarians, no crusades. But, crusades of
all.sorts haye been indispensable.  First, the new society could not be formed
without them. Then, without this religious prodigy, the example of the greatest
possible exaltation of religious feeling would have been lacking to the human
spirit and of this great mover of things here below one could not have had the
true measure.  Finally, an immense indispensable memorable event, all full of
instruction and of singularly fruitful thought without it would not have been
handed down to future generations.57

132                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS

    "Sensible man", you say, "but like everyone he runs after happiness:  that is
why he agitates the way he does".  For happiness, let's see what is needed
before all else? Must it not be to be content with oneself and everything in the
world.  I ask you, can that be so for anyone who is not an imbecile? Excellent
means, I find, to dissuade certain people of the ardent desire for happiness by
demonstrating t9 them that drunkards can be happy.
     It is certain that happiness, that which the majority of men desire, is an
impossible thing without a stupid contentment with oneself and with everything.
To achieve this happiness one seeks riches, honor, glory.  But once having
obtained all that, must one not be the most wise and most perfect of men and-to
have nothing more to say about all that which happens around you? Otherwise,
what good is it? The way to be happy without that? No matter how one
imagines felicity down here, one must always suppose at the end of this felicity a
foolish satisfaction in oneself, still even more foolish indifference towards all
that surrounds us. The ancients knew that very well. More frank, more naive
than we, they had no other morality than that. What is their sage? An arrogant
drunkard, ravished with himself, insensitive to all that which is going on before
him. In that there is no difference between Epicurus58 and Zeno.59 Such is the
most perfect ideal that man could make himself from supreme wisdom. What an
interval between this apathetic, immobile, disenchanted philosophy and this
other which says to us: Seek ye first the kingdom of heaven and all things will
be given unto you!60 And nevertheless what is simpler than the lesson contained
in this beautiful word of the Saviour?  Don't seek, it says to you, good for
yourself, seek it for others and it will come unerringly to you youself without
you even thinking about it:  as for your particular happiness, does it not lie in
general happiness?
   No personality, no egoism. That is what kills happiness. To live for others
is to live for oneself. Good will, infinite love for one's fellow man right there, I
believe, is true felicity: there is no other.
   Would not every material movement be, by change, the product of the
sonorous and harmonic vibration of the aerial fluid or of some other similar
fluid, even more subtle, more ethereal which would penetrate into the most solid
compact things and would directly act on the .elementary molecules whic.h
compose them?
    For us sound is something which affects our auditive organ. But why could
it not be by the effect of its harmonic action, principle or cause of the infinity of
mutations and transportations in matter, whose cause and principles we do not
know today?
    Vibration of the air is that which is sound in reality.  But, how can the air
surrounding the body vibrate without affecting the body in any way whatsoever?
The contrary, moreover, is proved.
      It is certain that the air is in a state of perpetual agitation. As for some of the
inexplicable phenomena of organic nature which take place in the interior of
beings, such as the ascending movement of sap in vegetables, the circulation of

--- PDF page 136 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                               133


blood in animals, etc. which more or less contradict the known natural laws,
notably that of universal gravity, could these phenomena be products of that
continual movement of aerial nature?  For example, I don't see how certain
consonances could not have taken place by the sequence of this movement
between medullar, fibral and other substances, finding themselves in certain
harmonic relationships among themselves in a same being in or in different
beings. And why these consonances would not then lead to a thousand effects
which surprise us?  If an undulation of air can unsettle an extended chord in
accordance with another chord, then why, I ask you, the nerve would not be
unsettled in the same way by a similar cause?
    Undoubtedly nothing but questions still, but do you acknowledge, that if
there were a response to these questions, what vast applications computation
would have obtained one day and how much thenceforth the domain of
mathematical certitude would be expanded?
     If there is such a thing as an infinity of space, I don't know it, but I know
that there is an infinity of time and that this infinity, this incommensurable
duration, this succession of things without end, it is nothing but life itself or
perfect existence.
   And first of all the finite is multiple, the infmite not. The idea of multiplicity
is mixed up in my mind with the idea of annihilation, the idea of unity with that
of eternity. Thus annihilation is for me  evil, eternity good. Evil tends only to
destroy, good to make continue. Thus eternity, well, life - it is all the same
thing.
     I call that the two terminal ideas of the human spirit, given the fact that they
are at the two ends of the line which give its measure. All other human ideas are
contained in them or relating to those there in such a way that the spirit cannot
conceive of anything except one or the other of these ideas; the idea of
annihilation or that of duration are necessarily connected.  Basis of all our
judgments, and not only of all our judgments but even of all our feelings, this
environment in which the entire movement of intelligence takes place forms the
law or all our thought without us doubting it at all. Remark that even numerical
ideas, however foreign they seem to be to these notions, are not exempt, since
they are only ideas of division and of addition:  to divide is to annihilate, to add
is to produce.
    Especially moral thought, in my opinion, could not have any other principle
than that one there.  All ideas of perfection, beauty, harmony, virtue, and love
are onlyrnqdifications of the idea of duration.  All ideas of imperfection,
ugliness, (iiscord, vice and hatred are only modifications of the idea of
annihilation. We could not imagine anything either of goodness or of beauty
unless we~ttribute to them at the same time durability, continuity, persistance.
We could not imagine anything evil or ugly unless we attached the idea of
precariousness, cessation, annihilation. Thus our mind is constantly between
the thought of life and that of death, and it is really only governed by these two
thoughts.

134                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


   One must observe that the feeling of our own preservation does not enter at
all in these elementary notions of reason. The principle of our preservation is
only that of animal nature, completely different from the moral principle. But
one must reunite them in order to produce the complete life:  it is exactly that
which Christian philosophy does in offering to the human spirit the idea of
eternal salvation as being the idea of the absolute good.
     I ask:  when one will have explained the historical phenomenon of the
establishment of Christianity in the most natural way possible, for example, by a
sort of development of the system of Moses produced by its mixing with oriental
philosophy and that of Greece ... what would one have thereby proved against
the fact of revelation? Where has one seen that the Saviour has said that the
Father was discovered by Him in an extraordinary and instantaneous revelation,
as He Himself was later revealed, for example, to Saint Paul? Thus don't we
know iliat He listened and questioned the doctors? And again, that the little child
grew and was fortified in spirit?61
    There is historical truth based on fact, there is historical truth based on
reason. There is analysis in history but there is also synthesis:  I would almost
say that there is in history the apriori as well as the aposteriori.
    Certainly what history recounts is not what conveys more of its truth; it is
what history thinks, what it imagines, what it invents.  In this sense, there are
such poetic conceptions in history which are perhaps truer than such and such a
scrupulous narration, just as pure reflection is often truer than experience. The
chain of facts can only be imagined; it is not to be found in the chronicle.
Deductions of simple reason serve no purpose there, for, strictly speaking, they
cannot take hold of the past, the proper domain of imagination and of poetry.
Moreover, the eyewitness very often does not see the thing such as it is, perhaps
never. And it happens that after many centuries have gone .by, the profound
thinker imagines the fact more correctly then the contemporary who touched it
with his own fingers. For that, what will the thinker have to do? He will have
to set out from a principle. There is the invention and also the loftiest historical
truth. One can say that this truth is not properly in history itself, but that it is
communicated to history by thought.  Such is the truth which one finds in
Niebuhr,62 Guizot,63 etc.
   When one has related much, it is time for conjecture. Then the mass of
events, the chaos of facts, disappears, reasoning comes; in place of the recitation
you have an argument, a syllogism, and finally the moral law of the times.
   The history of our country, for example, has still not been related enough,
however, that does not mean that one cannot conjecture about it. A stronger,
more prophetic thinking than that of Karamzin64 will do it one day. The Russian
people will then know what it is and especially what it is not. At this hour the
Russian people take themselves to be a people like any other; I have the belief
that it will then be frightened by its moral nothingness.  It will learn that
providence only had it live, in order to have a certain dynamic power in the

--- PDF page 138 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                                135


world and not in order to have it figure intellectually in the world. Then we will
know that we have only weighed down on the earth and not yet acted on it.
    Just as the nations which formed the new society were at first called on the
scene only as material powers and did not take their places in the intellectual
order until they submitted to the yoke of its law, we too, at this hour, are still
only a physical force; we will become a moral force only when we shall have
done the same as they. But when?
   The disbeliever resembles not badly, I find, a very clumsy juggler who with
one foot holds onto the rope and clumsily holds onto his equilibrium with the
other. But the bad juggler of the stage makes his tours de force pay, as for this
other bad juggler, what does he get for his pain? As far as I know nothing but
fatigue. When one sees him balancing thus on his rope, one is afraid for him,
one would like to lend him a hand and say to him "Get down now, my friend,
you are going to break your neck."
   "Many are the poets that have never penn'd,"65 said Byron. But no matter
what he says about it, thought alone does not make the poet, he still must know
how to recite.  It is a fact that poetic thinking is not complete so long as it has not
been clothed in words. Poetic inspiration, in my opinion, is as much that of the
words as that of the idea. Thought in silence is a thought without body, but
poetry is all body, even today when it does nothing but reason. One is never a
poet in prose. All the great prose writers whom one treats like poets, Ft~nelon,
Buffon, Rousseau did not possess, according to me, absolutely anything of
poetic power. The material element of this power is its integral element.  I want
the poet to shake my nerves as well as my soul.
    Poetry is given to reunite the physical world to the intellectual world, and to
fool the mind by this confusion. Music has the same mission and it is proceeded
by poetry, but music all by itself could not be sufficient to produce this effect:
the mind would resist it.  But, it was urgent that the mind should have been
fooled: consequently it has fallen to poetry to do it.
   One does well to base speculation upon the principle of contradiction:  it is
undoubtedly a great law, but in relationship to God this principle is no more
viable than any other principle of confined human reason. What.exactlymakes
up God's being is that all contradiction disappears in his All-Powerfulness and
that all that is impossible for us becomes possible in Him.
   When reason tries to know God all by itself, it makes a God with its own
hands; it does not attempt to conceive of God as He is. That is why, had God
not revealed Himself, there would have never been a God on earth except the
God made by human hands.
   But ins said: hence He made Himself completely visible to man? Not at all.
He only showed to man as much as He had to, so that man could seek Him in
this life and find Him in the other; that's all.
   What are angels?   Intellects superior to the human intellect, natures
inaccessible to our organs. Why should it not be so? Here is not, I find, a case
of doubt but a case of ignorance.

136                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


    Genesis does not speak about the creation of angels.  The point is that
Genesis does not deal with everything but only with human things. And that is,
in my opinion, one of the characteristics which distinguished it best from all the
known cosmologies; there is one of the great proofs, I find, concerning the
origin attributed to Genesis. Genesis only says exactly that which must be said.
Does that not remind one of the physical principle of the least action? Angels are
not missing from the rest of the Bible; the language of the times. God could
only speak to man in man's language. Let us not be surprised that He would
wish to be heard by man, when He deigns to address words to man. But that
should not be the basis for the belief in angels.  Otherwise each word of the
Sacred Book would be dogma.
   Hence is the doctrine of angels an article of faith?  Assuredly not. Even
more, can man, made in the image of God, legitimately recognize natures
superior to himself?  I doubt it.  Jesus was not an angel, but He was God and
man at the same time. Thus it is permissible to doubt, I believe, that the interval
between the intelligent nature of man and that of God be f11led by an intennediate
nature. What is very true is that at all times multitudes, as well as the most
profound minds, were inclined to admit natures more perfect than our own
natures.  It is quite possible not to attach importance to this belief, but it would
be an even more gross superstition to reject it as a gross superstition, I find.
    Swedenborg66 was a man of great profundity. Only he was wrong to fonn
an esoteric doctrine which has only served to diminish the effect which his
works would have produced without that.  But as for his familiarity with
celestial powers, there's nothing to be surprised about in that.  I would have
been very much more surprised if, given a mind made the way his was, he
would not have been as familiar with the celestial powers as he was.
   You have often heard say that sleep is the figure of death:  it is not that at all.
As for me, I find that it is rather sleep which is a real death, and, that which is
called death, perhaps there is life there. In sleep there is an interruption of the
me, after death that does not happen, for if there were not persistance of the me
there would be annihilation. From the tomb one no longer returns, but one
returns from sleep and one takes up the me again. But, say, does one live, when
one has no doubts that one lives, even if it were only a sole instant?
   The fact is that true death is essential in life. Dead we are, perfectly dead a
portion of our lives, not hyperbolically, not figuratively, hut literally, .seriously
dead. A thousand times a 'daya reflective return back toourselveswil11et you
see that the moment before you were not living, no more than before being born.
You had no awareness of that which you did, not only the feeling of your
existence. And then, hence where was life? The life of a tree, of a living thing
more or less, not even the life of an animated being, and assuredly not life of an
intelligent being.
    Life ceaselessly escapes us, then one finds it again, so much nearly that we
might live perpetually. Intelligent life is interrupted each time that awareness is
lost.

--- PDF page 140 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                               137


   The more there are of these moments, the less there is of intellectua1life.
And if there are only those moments there, that is, well, death. To die in this
way no need to forsake this life and very certainly as for another death, there is
none. Death in life is hence all the death there is.67
    However, listen to us. When I say awareness I do not thereby understand
the ideological awareness upon which modern philosophy is based, a simple
feeling of our existence.  I mean this other awareness which lets us not only feel
ourselves living but that we know even how we live.  It is the power given to us
to act, in each moment of duration, upon the following moment, to make our life
ourselves instead of letting it flow as the brute does.  Should this awareness
there ever become entirely lost, that is what kills irrevocably. And do you know
why? That there is the perfect damnation. Can the intelligent being draw upon
itself any greater pain than that of nothingness?
   What the pagans called wisdom, virtue, sovereign good, we call all that
heaven.
     I do not know where bad thoughts come to me from, only the madman
thinks he knows where good thoughts come from.
    Christian charity: reason stripped of the faculty of relating to itself.
   The well-made mind gravitates towards a belief, towards a submission just
as naturally as the badly-made mind pushes away all belief, resists against all
submission.68
   The philosopher makes of God, a law, a harmony, a universal and I don't
know what all else. Then he says, "the divinity could not be conceived of, I
surely believe. How will I conceive of this incoherent God, multiple in infinity,
intelligence and matter at the same time?"  But that there is not the God who is, it
is the God that you have made. In place of the most simple idea of all, you have
put the most complex idea possible.  It is a great marvel truly that you would not
know how to make it enter into your mind.
  We only know a little tip of our being, that which we fmd in our present life.
We well know that it continues strongly after that, and yet, an unheard-of thing:
we wish to know the law of our entire being.
   The pantheist calls the world the all. He supposes it to be perfect. He finds
cause and principle of everything in everything.  This everything is eternal,
infinite, intelligent; he embraces all time, all space.  Finally all the attributes
which the Deist finds in God, the pantheist finds in his all.
   And at the right moment. A perfectly consequential system which can be
rigorously demonstrated. Once the word all is pronounced, the rest is only a
necessary! corollary of the principle thus posed.
   But all that evidently is reducible to one word substituted for the other.
Thus Spinoza could very well be strongly religious, and he was without any
doubt.  In reading him one senses, in effect, how he is carried along despite
himself by means of some extraordinarily devout thing, something which pierces
through the mathematical arrogance of his argument and makes all the more
impression, since one anticipated it the least.

--- PDF page 141 / 323 ---

138                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS

    Yet as for pantheism, it is basically a very exaggerated admiration of nature.
Forced to see intelligence everywhere, pantheists envision intelligence for
everything and all the universe is found in this way to be one great intelligence.
Look at Bonnet,69 Paley 70 and the others.
    Animal instinct and human instinct are two completely different things. The
fIrst is a physical or sensitive impulse; the other a confused perception of reason
which only becomes confused with sensation, because reason is confused but is
in effect well distinct from it.
   Man does not possess his instinct in the way beasts do. That would exclude
reason. But he possesses it in the way which is proper to him. In man instinct
all alone determines nothing.  It only acts in him by combining with his reason
whose energy he sometimes augments and sometimes weakens.  In animals
instinct is the unique principle of all their activity; that is why its power is so
strong in them and why in certain cases it seems to go beyond the power of
man's very reason.
   What to do to see clearly? Don't look around oneself.
   What is Christianity? Science of life and of death.
   What is the social order? Temporary remedy for a temporary evil.
   What do legislative, political, judiciary institutions and all combinations of
this nature do? They correct the evil which they have made.
   What have the barbarians, the destroyers of the ancient world become?
Christians.
   What would have become of the world if Jesus Christ had not been born?
Nothingness.
   Did no one ever come to dream that two and two make fIve? No. Then why
say that in sleeping one does not have the use of one's reason?
    In France what does one do with thinking? One says it. What does one do
with it in England? One practices it. What does one do with it in Germany?
One digests it. What does one do with thinking in our country? Nothing at all,
and do you know why?
   Men consider themselves to be in society when they are reunited in cities or
in other enclosures. One would say that they have only to press themselves
together and to park themselves like sheep in order to be in society.
    Five years ago in Florence I met a man whom I liked very much.?1  I passed
only a few hours with him, only a few hours -but truly very sweet and very good
ones. And yet I did not know how to extract from this man every part that he
had to extract from me. He was a English Methodist,_ established, I believe, in a
mission in the south of France, but when I made his acquaintance, he was
returning from the Holy Land.  In him was a singular mix of warmth, grace,
vivacity for the great object of his preoccupation, namely religion, and a singular
mix of indifference, coldness and tranquility for all the rest.  In the Italian
galleries, the masterpieces of art did not move him at  all, but the  little
sarcophaguses of the first centuries of the Church occupied him in a singular
fashion. He considered them and meditated over them with exaltation. He saw

--- PDF page 142 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                               139


there something or other saintly, touching and profoundly instructive, and he
was absorbed in the reflections which they suscitated in him.  Hence I only
passed a few hours with this man, a very short time, a moment, since then no
news from him. Well, there is the man in whom I am at this very hour the most
in society. Not a day passes that his memory returns to me, and always with it
an emotion, a thought amid the so great pains which rain on me, amid my
numerous discouragements, and that bolsters me up. There is the true society of
intelligent beings. There is how two souls really act on one another: space and
time can have nothing to do with it.
   One hears say sometimes about an old man that he has reverted to
childhood, the poor man! Oh no, the fact is that he still has not emerged from
childhood. Look at his life: always a child, more a child than at this hour, he is
that which he always has been.
    Fortunate would be the man who could retrace his steps.  That is not,
however, the established order; he must always advance, and he always does
advance, not even one step backwards, always in front of himself, and on his
head he gathers up guilt upon guilt. But then when death has passed over him,
yes, then divine pity, one must hope, will allow him to stop a moment, to revise
past times, perhaps even to revert a little.
    However, as you know, there is a·Christian -demonination which it does not
want to have anything to do with Purgatory; it wants us to jump with both feet
from this life into this other, where all is irrevocable, irremissable, irreparable.
A cruel doctrine, more cruel than false.
     If you wish to become acquainted with what the animal soul is, I beg your
pardon, but see what happens in you part of the day; you will learn something
from  it.  Pay attention to the fact that the most intelligent animal proves
absolutely nothing.. For example, if one sees it hesitate sometimes before
becoming determined to act as if it were reflecting, that is because the complete
extent of its sensation is not known and how far the sensation can continue in
beings which only have it as a guide. In animals there is a very sensitive almost
mechanical imitation which, if one understands it, would explain well all that is
going on in them and leave no difficulty at all about this subject. As for us
ourselves, we mechanically imitate a thousand things without the least bit of
reflection: without doubting at all the habits of the people with whom we live,
we contrast their allures, we imitate their gestures and even the sound of their
voices. That is our purely animal nature.
   As for this weak element of perfectibility which one finds in beasts, there is
no ne~ to revert back to sensation; the organic principle alone suffices to make it
reasonable. Don't plants have their habits? One could give them new ones, if
one better understood their structure better, and that would become the education
of plants. Then there would be progress there, as in beasts.
    Buffon72 and other naturalists have said almost the same thing. But it is
necessary to depend upon the consideration which the experience of our own

--- PDF page 143 / 323 ---

140                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


nature furnishes.  For, there is nothing so important to realize as that we are not
men all day long, one must admit.
    If I remember correctly, Buffon after having lifted every piece of intelligence
from animals, attributes -to them a sort of awareness -of their being. A singular
idea! As for myself then, do I constantly have this awareness? And more, don't
I have to make a certain effort to give myself the awareness? Hence could the
animal have something that I myself don't have at this hour? Foolishness.
    Repeat unceasingly: How can we be miserable? Are we not made in the
image of God?
    Yes, all is reflected in consciousness. No law in nature which is not found
repeated in the me. All phenomena of the -physical world are -reproduced in the
intellectual world.  Thought recapitulates all the movement of nature in its
interior.  But it knows and nature does not know.  The life of thought is
knowledge; the life of nature is passive movement. When thought has ceased to
know, it has ceased to be. That is why the Savior said: eternal life is to know
you, my Father,73
    Throughout all of his life man can believe in the annihilation of his being;
never has he believed it, never will he believe it at the instant before his death.
At that very moment when he feels himself dissolving, he feels himself
enduring. There, I believe, is the great principle of the universal persistence of
beings, expressed to the nth degree this time in the me.
    There is a plastic force in nature which only makes forms. Probably there is
the true vital principle, resume of all natural forces. Nowhere does it manifest
itself in a more instructive manner than in crystalization; there must one study it
and meditate on it.  This crystalization is, in effect, a singular phenomenon.
Nothing but geometry. And pay close attention; nature procedes in this way in
the formation of the primary matter of bodies: an immense subject for reflection.
    Also, a force which responds to that, exists in the intelligence, namely
imagination. Would not these two forces of universal nature be those which
hold most of the creative power and which would imitate it the best? Very
possibly.
    In achieving one's salvation what must one do?  It is life which one gives to
his soul:  a wholly divine work.  Hence the one who has given life to
innumerable multitudes of souls, the one who does not cease giving  it
perpetually, the one who will do nothing but always disseminate up to the end of
the centuries, sowing life everywhere - I ask you, is he a god or not?
   What is reason? Concerning reason, I only know my reason for me; but, I
do not call that one reason.  I call it nothingness: say, am I not the master of it?
   Do you believe74 that man would naturally understand death better than
birth? Certainly not.
   Around him he sees beings being formed and being dissolved and among
other beings similar to him: he does not know whether they lived under another
form, before they has assumed their current form; he does not know if they will
live when they will have lost it, however, he believes that he understands

--- PDF page 144 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                                  141


something about death, because he fears it.  It is not the pain which makes him
 afraid, for he does not know whether there is pain.   It is also not the
annihilation, for, after all, what is then so frightening about ceasing to be?
Hence it follows that he has learned in some way or other that after death he will
 still live. But he does not know what this other life will be like, and what it is to
live in this new life, in a way different from the current way of living, which
seems to him to be terrifying. Hence there we have only one of these great
traditions whose origin  is lost in unknown times,  like so many other
fundamental ideas ohhis human spirit, which the human spirit did -not give itself
at all, but which must have been communicated then when intelligence was
created in the universe.
    But finally what is death? Nothing but the moment in the total duration of
man when he ceases to perceive himself in his body, nothing but that.
    Immortality of the soul and  its immateriality cannot be rigorously
demonstrated. But that which can perhaps be demonstrated in its existence after
the instant which we call death. One has need of no more than that for universal
morality.
   The Christian passes unceasingly from earth into heaven and from heaven
onto earth and ends by installing himself in heaven.
    There are more legitimate beliefs than there are doubts. There that is what
this sublime word from St. Paul signifies: charity be lives everything.75
   No one claims to obtain anything unless he at least makes the effort to
extend his hand in order to seize it.  There is only one thing that one must
exempt from that; it is happiness. As for happiness, one finds very simply that
one possesses it without having done anything to acquire it, or, I would say, to
merit it.
   The only way to believe in God is to hope in Him. That is why he who
does not pray, does not believe.
    Christian immortality76 is life with the least amount of death, and not, as has
been imagined, a life after death.
   You venerated and you loved this man who has just died. At this hour he is
for you only a sad memory, sad and perhaps sweet at the same time. But you no
longer love him, you no longer venerate him, you only make yourself recall
him.
    And, in effect, how to love and to venerate a piece of dust?  But if, by
chance, this man still lived, I don't know where, but in some faraway region, in
some unknown land?  If he were only absent, nothing but absent, as so many
other of your friends? In that case why would you not bear him at present the
same feelings which you bore him at other times?  I don't see any reason at all
for it. .And there is the cult of saints. To believe seriously in good faith in the
immortality of the soul and not to venerate the men who merit our veneration,
because they no longer live here on earth - is that not absurd?

   Do you recall what happened to you during the first year of your life? No,
you say. Very well, what is strange then about your not remembering either
what happened before your birth?
  We are enjoined to love our neighbor, but why?  In order to love a thing
other than ourselves.  It has nothing at all to do with morality, it's very simply
logical.   I try vainly, but I always find something which interposes itself
between the truth and me. That thing is myself. Truth is hidden from me only
by myself. Hence there is only one way of discovering it, by setting aside the
me. One would do well, I find, to repeat often to oneself what Diogenes, as you
know, once said to Alexander:  "Stand back,my friend, you are blocking me
from my sun. "77
     It is only the eye of charity which sees clearly.  There is the entire
philosophy of Christianity.
   What do men do in living together? Nitrogen: they kill each other off.
   Do you conceive of the generation of truth in you? No. And that of the lie?
Certainly.
    All my being revolts -at the idea _of attributing some incapacity or other .to
God.  One does that every time when one imagines eternal laws, either
immutable order or pre-established harmony, either eternal matter, monads,
elements or every other similar thing.  One always supposes that God is
incapable of destroying that, whether one says it or not.
   As is known, Descartes78 could not support the idea of limiting divine
power. As for me, I can't doubt it.  It is the horror which I always had of this
idea which has been the star of my life.
   No matter what definition one makes for organic being you will find that it
can be perfectly applied to the terrestial globe.  Nature has been divided into
organic and inorganic. But, as the celestial bodies cannot belong to one or the
other of these two divisions, one has devised mathematical points of a new sort
because of the immensity of space in which _they move. As Jar the _planet which
we inhabit, knowing only the epidermis, one makes from this epidermis matter a
vast science. Very well, what have you to refute that?  Isn't empiricism perfectly
satisfied with this arrangement? What else do you need?
   You others, who give no credence to Christianity, think yourselves -to be
very strong. Then know especially that as for that force which you vaunt, given
the day on which Christianity is offered to you, it is no -longer a matter of
whether to become Christian or not become Christian.   It is prejudice and
superstition which causes the fear in you; very well, it is in lack of belief that
prejudice and superstition are to be found, not at all in faith. Say, how have you
become what you are? Is it not -the same as -someone becoming Christian? Do
you do anything but repeat, as people do, your cathecism without understanding
it?  Then do you believe that you made up what you spout out with such
confidence? Poor people! Look a little; is it not the parish priest who taughtitto
you?
