FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                                143

   When the philosophe pronounces the word man, does he always know just
what he wants to say?  I don't think so. Man comes into the world like other
animals; he differs from them only by an organization which is particular to him
and which makes him a unique type in the animal kingdom. Of course, this is
not the case of the intelligent man, i.e., the instructed man:  a separated being
which has no place in nature.  When one composes treatises on human
understanding, one seeks to discover how the human animal came to be an
intelligent being. Precisely there is the error and confusion. The animal human
becomes the intelligent man, yes, but he does not become it necessarily, but
fortuitously.
    There is the hypothesis concerning the animal human,and I say hypothesis,
because such a man has never been seen, given the fact that man, borne amid his
similar beings, could not remain in his native state for an instant. Hence this
hypothesis is very good in pathology and in philosophic hygiene. But what do
your think that philosophy properly -speaking can make out of -it? When
philosophy occupies itself with it, from a human philosophy it becomes animal
philosophy. And it is then only that part of natural history which deals with
animal customs that forms the human chapter in zoology.
     Infinite spirit, when it dons the form of finite spirit, when it realizes itself in
man, should naturally preserve in its new mode of existence, the conditions of
its previous existence - it should know before all else.  Thus, man does not
emanate from the two-footed animal, as the materialists imagine, but from a
naIve almost incomplete feeling of his nature. Hence human intelligence is never
found to be in absolute ignorance of everything; a more or less clear notion of
the law of its being appeared to man the very day when human intelligence knew
that it existed.  Otherwise, human intelligence would not have contained the
principle of its existence; it would not have been spirit. But it is also certain that,
in the course of time, the individual spirit, by the very reason of its free nature,
must be isolated, detached from universal intelligence, and become subjective,
and from that point on, absolute ignorance becomes possible at the same time as
the return of the individual being to the general being and the reconstruction of
the fallen me became inevitable. That is what Christianity proposes in the logical
order and what it realizes in so far as a revolution of this nature could happen it
without breaking the equilibrium among the different forces which move the
moral world, without basically ruining all the laws of creation. The day when
the human holocaust was consummated on Calvary, the universal spirit found
itself again manifested in the individual spirit, and this time it took up residence
for all time.  Since then man found himself virtually in -possession of the
absolute good and true. Evil had a boundary which it no longer dared to violate
and the good had none.  The profound shadow which the immense human
personality projected not long ago on all objects of its vision, no longer existed
and henceforth he tended only to live in the reality.
   But there were some phases that humanity had to pass through before
arriving at this stage in which it found itself established now, when humanity
still lived at the heart of its primitive nature or when it already gave itself an

--- PDF page 147 / 323 ---

144                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


impulse of its own or finally when its march was spontaneously determined by a
direct manifestation of the unversal spirit, humanity never departed from the
sphere of knowledge. Hence right there is the milieu in which the entire human
factor takes place.  Spirit is spirit, it cannot do anything but understand. From
morning til night man only does that.  Even his sensations in so far as he
experiences them, are transformed into perceptions in his head.  And this
continual participation of his intelligent being in all that which happens in his
physical being is precisely what constitutes his unity, that which makes of him
something more than a pure intellect.
    Thus, that man of genius who conceived of a doctrine of science certainly
came up with a good idea. But one should be very on guard to remember that
man does nothing but know and that it is perfectly true that there exists ::>utside
man something which is the object of his knowledge.  Consequently, it is the
relationship between knowledge and the object which one must establish, if one
wishes to obtain the law of knowledge itself. That is what one did not do. One
only occ\lpied himself with the subject, i.e., with that which knows and one did
not reach to the object, i.e., that which is known. By a natural consequence the
system which came first after that had to concentrate with all its power upon the
object of human knowledge and that is what in effect took place. But then the
individual was swallowed up in nature in the all; it was pantheism with all its
consequences. Thus a new reaction became necessary one time in which the me,
while completely reclaiming its rights, had to accept the accomplished synthetic
fact and place itself in front of the universe and of God in truth and in reality.
There is the last chapter of modern philosophy.
   One sees that in this system it will be the spirit which will be the supreme
category in a manner of speaking or the definitive form ofHfe. To -attain that,
being goes through many phases and also categories, which are much more
important than the ideological categories of Kant.  All these phases of
humanity's general life are linked together and follow logically, and the human
me is allowed only to catch the feeling more or less, since, in each given epoch,
it seeks to render  account of that which it experiences, of that which is
happening around it. Humanity'S progress would consist of approaching thus
more or less the moment when the achievement of the complete feeling of what
will then be contained in it, will be accorded to humanity. And, once having
achieved that, humanity would find itself in the state of absolute spirit, i.e., of a
spirit which knows itself and conceives of humanity perfectly, the last phase of
human development.
  We just saw what constituted the vice of the celebrated doctrine of the me, a
doctrine which claimed to establish that there exists nothing except knowledge,
which assumed that knowledge presupposes a known object, i.e., something
that man did not create and which existed before he even had knowledge of it.
But one could not deny that in its passing it left some profound traces in the
human spirit. A principle of extreme fruitfulness was contained in this daring
apotheosis of the individual. If Fichte did not see the object, it is assuredly not a
fault of his philosophic mind, it is very simply because he was absorbed in a

--- PDF page 148 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                                145


passionate work which he was obliged to do in order to arrive at constructing the
 internal fact.  But, Hege179 coming after him and having Schelling as master,
was to be led to constitute the external fact, and that is a task which he fulfilled
admirably.  It remains to know if in his tum Hegel did not allow himself to be
invaded too much by the object, if, in his work.of general reconcilation, ·he
assumed the role of the individual? An impartial examination of his system, seen
from this point of view, would give us the most exact evaluation ·of it. In any
case, his philosophy, as it was essentially synthetic, could not remain on target;
in the same way that Fichte had to fall back upon analysis despite himself, and
despite the powerful impulse that Schelling came to impress upon the
philosophical world.  That Hegel prodigiously advanced the synthesis of the
human spirit is certain, but we shall be allowed to add that he did not completely
elaborate his thought. Passed away too early in the midst of his career, Hegel
had not the time to state his final word, to put the finishing touches on his work.
Yet, here are some lines from Hegel himself which will state better than we can,
the tendency of his system:  "The human spirit" he says, "knew well how to
make the analysis of certain things, but it has not yet learned how to make the
synthesis. For example, it has separated the soul from the body in man, and that
was very good, for those are two different things. The human spirit has also
separated God from nature, and that also was very good, for God is spirit and
the world only matter. But having done that, the human spirit forgot the magic
word which was supposed to reunite these things again, a little like Goethe's
sorcerer's apprentice,80 who, after having inundated his master's house, did not
know how to get the water out of it and going to drown when very fortunately
for him the master arrived and saved him."  One· can guess who is the master
sorcerer in philosophy.                                                                    '
   Do you think that it be nothing at all to transport a meditative spirit to its own
sphere, to impose upon it the paltry world in which the common man works?
Around you you have a crowd of men, or fractions of men, who are not destined
to leave a trace of their passage. Why don't you go preach your sublime
doctrines to them? But let the serious man, obeying his own principle, pass by.
Don't detour him from the road which he follows, despite himself, the road from
which he could not deviate without breaking himself, without dragging you
along with him perhaps in his fall. Hence don't you see that the orbit which he
travels in has been traced in advance? To bar him the way is to attack the laws of
nature; Do you think that it is an innocent joke to cast stones on the road of the
man of thought so that he stumbles, so that he falls full length on the pavement
and gets up· only  all soiled, his face  all bruised, his clothes in tatters?
Undoubtedly you could not comprehend this man. Crippled, mutilated, tired of
the nothingp.ess surrounding him, a mystery for himself, for you even more so,
how to figure out in effect that which was hidden  at the basis of this
compromised soul! What you and yours have driven back in this poor heart,
what the world and things, in which he moves along panting, crushed with
germs, with power in this poor head! Not recognized by the world, he does not

146                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


recognize the world. He had the naiVete to believe that he would be assisted,
that the idea promulgated by him would be grasped, that his sacrifices which his
martyrs would not pass unobserved, that one of you would gather up what he
scattered with such largesse. Undoubtedly foolishness! But was it up to you to
lay traps for him, to play the role of agent provocateur with him? Was it your
job, you who yourself have called him powerful in word and in heart?  If that
was not a bitter derision, then how did you have the courage to gag his mouth
and put him in chains? And who knows what would have happened if you had
not crossed his path?  Perhaps a torrent cleansing the filth in which you are
interred? Perhaps a sword cutting the shackles which are binding you?
   From the day when we pronounced the word of West in relationship to
ourselves we were lost.
      It is not the Turks who destroyed the eastern empire, it is the western people
who let Constantinople be taken by Mehmed and who had already taken it in
passing once themselves.8!
   How does it happen that in a book on Christianity, generally acknowledged
as a good book in which all the religions and all the philosophies in the world are
discussed, there is not a word about the Orthodox Church, if only to refute its
doctrines?82
    Christianity exists; it exists not only as religion, but also as science, as
religious philosophy, it has been accepted not only by ignorant populations but
also by the most enlightened, the most profound minds: that is something which
the most implacable enemies cannot deny. Hence they must prove one of three
things:  either that as Jesus Christ never existed, the religion which carries his
name had been made by some adroit jugglers, or that, as Jesus Christ existed, he
was himself only a juggler, or finally that he was an exalted fanatic, convinced
of the things he was declaiming, in the same way as his divine origin. Having
admitted one of these three things, it would remain to explain how Christianity,
orginating from a lie, could have arrived at the state in which we find it today
and produce the results which it has produced.
    Socialism will triumph not because it is right but because those who fight it
are wrong.
    That which proves, among other things, that it is our Church which made us
such as we are, is the fact that at present it is still a religious question which is
the great concern ofthe country, that of the dissidents, the raskolniki [religious
 schismatics].                                                                                                                  I
   On the one hand, the unruly movement of society in Europe moving towards
 its unknown destiny, the entire western soil trembling and falling under the feet
of the innovating genius; on the other hand, the majestic immobility of ·our
country and the perfect repose of its people contemplating with a tranquil and
serene eye the immense tempest which pounds at our doors.  Such is the
imposing spectacle of our day which the two spheres of human society offer us,
a spectacle all full of lessons and which one could not admire enough ... ten pages

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                               147


    This man would be bearable if he decided not to know something, but no,
he must know everything, everything. However, there is one very simple thing
which he is perfectly ignorant of and that is that if one unfortunately finds
oneself encumbered with the exterior of a hippopotamus, a man of genius must
be modest.
    Another one has a mind turned in the same way, but that one at least remains
wisely at home in the sanctimonious and solitary contemplation of his own
merits and those of his child. He does not come to hang on your neck to inform
you that he knows every thing better than you.
    The entire philosophy of Hegel, as is known, is contained in the two notions
of existence and of spirit.  Certainly there is nothing to reproach him in that.
After all, what does one find in the world of thought and of free will other than
the principle of action and its product?  Nothing at all.  Unfortunately the
intermediate space between these two capital notions is filled by an undoubtedly
rigorous dialectic, or if you will, a too rigorous a dialectic, but one which often
degenerates into a veritable nominalism, into a narrow war of words possible
only in an idiom in which each substantive becomes an infinitive to your taste.
A particular grammar which expresses cause and effect with the same word and
destroys causality by a fault of language. The role which Hegel has assigned to
the dialectic is evidently too vast. Evidently he misunderstood his century, this
century so preoccupied with the practical ideas, so pressed to attain results, to
arrive at a reality to utilize an expression borrowed from Hegel himself. How, I
ask you, can you expect us to tire ourselves out in this interminable discussion,
in this scholasticism resuscitated from the Middle Ages, swept along as we are
on our railroads with the rapidity of the sun's rays towards the conclusion of all
things? Impossible.
     It is known that the acquisition of one thing or other operates in two ways
either by production or by exchange, or the opposite:  to acquire something one
must either make it or buy it. If you are ignorant of the process of producing the
thing or if you don't have the means to produce it, or if you don't have the
equivalent to offer in exchange, well, if you have credit, you could still obtain it.
The current owner of the object will cede the property to you willingly based on
the belief in an equivalency to come. But, it must be noted, nothing stimulates
the thirst for joy as much as the ability to obtain the possession of things by
means of this latter route. Nothing makes you dispense of your future with such
great ease; nothing increases so much the extent of your life and offers such vast
resources for your material existence in the present-day by anticipating the
material existence of a tomorrow.  But, in the intellectual order when the
acquisition of ideas and of knowledge is only obtained by the work of the mind
and by study, when consequently simple exchange cannot take place, when one
cannot obtain one idea for another, there it happens that one gives credit to
oneself, that one consumes things before even possessing them and that one sets
in motion certain ideas without understanding them. Nothing is morec.ommon
in our day.  It is this same thirst for joy, seeking to satisfy itself in another

--- PDF page 151 / 323 ---

148                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


sphere. One can see that the theory of credit applied to the intellectual order is
singularly appropriate.
   The diffusion of enlightenment has caused all the classes to be permeated
with new needs which yearn to be ·satisfied. That is really the ·actualsituation.
There it is - nothing more, nothing less.  It is up to you to teach us whether the
factual situation is legitimate or not. The worker wishes to have the leisure time
to read a new book as you do, to see a new playas you do, to speak sometimes
with his friends as you do. Undoubtedly he is wrong, .butthen why are you.&o
preoccupied with disseminating enlightenment,  in organizing primary
instruction, in rendering science accessible to every one? You should have left
the masses in their brutish state. The means which the classes, who have been
disinherited from the goods of the earth, use to conquer are undoubtedly
detestable, but do you believe that the means which feudal lords used to enrich
themselves were any better? You must teach them other more legitimate, more
efficacious means, which unsettle your habits of comfort andfar niente less, but
you must not insult them. The insult is not a treatise on political economy. The
miserable who wishes for a little well-being, which you don't know how to
arrange, is sometimes undoubtedly cruel, but never will he be as cruel as your
fathers were, those who made you what you are, who gave you what you
possess.
    People speak a lot about the absence of security in which society finds itself.
But since when did society enjoy any of this charming ·security which you regret
losing so much? Did society enjoy it in the Middle Ages when each baron
extorted the district which his castle dominated, when peasant uprisings
desolated the countryside? Did society enjoy security in Italy when each city had
its tyrant with his arsenal of torture instruments, or when there were the
Huguenot wars, when Coligny84 was assassinated and the king of France85 fired
on his own subjects? Did society enjoy security during the Thirty Years War
when Germany smoked, burned by the bands of Wallenstein and Tilly,86 or
when the Palatinate was ravaged by order of Louvois87 and when the dragoons
caused the industrial populations of France to flee? Did society enjoy security
when festoons of human cadavers decorated the white walls of the Kremlin,88 or
when under the regime of the imperial conscription the flower of the nation
perished on the battle field?  Finally, does society enjoy security today in the
land when~ credit does not exist, where the normal rate of interest is ten per cent,
where immense capital is at a standstill out of fear of being compromised, in
which masters oppress their serfs when they are not oppressed by them, a
country in which the state of siege is the permanent state of the country?
   Once again, don't confuse two perfectly different factors which have no
relationship to one another other than chronological order. Society is attacked,
but  it defends  itself and, while defending  itself,  it is being instructed.
Undoubtedly society will find a way to extricate itself from this situation.  It is
not as it was in former times a case of absurd and ridiculous prejudice debating

--- PDF page 152 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                               149

against general reason; it is now a serious interest which defends itself against a
serious interest.
   The philosophic me has nothing at all to so with that.  Religious feeling,
naIve belief, juvenile faith of the initial ages of Christianity are no longer
possible and can no longer return for the masses. Henceforth they should only
serve as consolation for some isolated beings, for some eternally primitive souls.
    Divine wisdom never claimed to stop the world.  It is divine wisdom itself
which taught the human species the majority of the ideas which trouble it today.
Divine wisdom could not and did not want to abolish the liberty of the human
spirit.  It placed in the -human heart the germ of all the goods which man is
permitted to enjoy, then it let humanity fend for itself. Would you dare to say
that this germ perished in the present torment or that it became sterile by the
storm's action on it? That would be a more guilty blasphemy than all the evil in
the world caused by revolutions, for that would result in eliminating hope from
the human spirit, that force which the Scriptures dared to make a virtue.
  We march for the deliverance of the rayah89 - to obtain equality of rights for
them.  Isn't that enough to make one laugh?
    Maritime power - without coastline, without colonies, without a merchant
marine - isn't that enough to make one laugh?
   You talk to me about the persecution which you suffer - a domestic quarrel,
that's all.
   You call that a sublime ending, as for me I call that stupid complicity. An
absence of generosity.
   You claim to represent an idea; strive to have ideas - that would be better.
    Law, and you know it, punishes complicity as well as crime.
    [The following three short paragraphs are in Russian]
   The Russian people are a singing not a talking people. One polyphonous
Russian song contains more of Russian life than an entire pile of Russian
chronicles.
    Don't begin a war for the territorial integrity of Turkey; America alone is the
competitor to be feared, in our age barbarism cannot overthrow enlightenment.
It is not the control of Constantinople which might make Russia powerful. The
Turks control Constantinople but look how miserable her condition is.
    This is how the question must be posed.  In the person of Peter the Great,
Russia realized her criminal solitude and her flawed character. She became -an
apprentice to Europe. She has walked this new path until now. The recognition
she received was rewarded by every kind of success and during the reign of
Alexander I was crowned with the most ex halted triumphs ever seen in the
history of mankind. But suddenly she decided that she could go it alone, that it
was time to return to her previous solitude. Europe was at first amazed by such
audacity, it looked her in the eye and seeing that she was indeed no longer
humble, Europe became angry, and then the fun started.90

Letter to Alexander Pushkin, September 18, (1831)1

    Well, my friend, what have you done with my manuscript?2 Has it been
infected with the cholera? But I hear that the cholera did not visit you. Perhaps
the manuscript has taken flight?  But if this so, please let me know at least
something about  it.   I was very happy to see your handwriting again.   It
reminded me of a time which, if the truth be known, had not been worth much,
but which still offered hope; the great disillusionments were yet to come.  I refer
to myself, as you of course understand; but you too, it seems to me, enjoyed
then the advantage that all the realities had not yet been exhausted. Sweet and
brilliant were these realities to you, my friend; nevertheless, were there among
them any that can be compared to the false expectations, misleading forebodings
and mendacious visions of the happy age of ignorance?
   You say that you would like to talk:  let's talk. But beware, I am not in a
happy mood; and as for you, you are nervous. And what shall we talk about?
You know that I have but one thought.  If, by any chance I found other ideas in
my brain, they would attach themselves to that one.  I wonder if this satisfies
you. And if you lent me some ideas from your world, if you evoked a response
from me? But you wish that I should be the first to speak; so be it, but again I
say, guard your nerves!
    This is therefore what I have to say to you.  Have you noticed that
something extraordinary is happening  at the center of the world of morality,
something similar to what they say is happening at the center of the physical
world?  So tell me, I beg you, how has this affected you?  As far as I'm
concerned,  it seems to me that this is ready material for poetry, this great
revolution in the scheme of things;3 you cannot remain indifferent to it, all the
more since the selfishness of poetry will, I believe, find ample nourishment in it.
How can one not be affected in one's most intimate feelings when set in the
midst of this general clash of all of the elements in human nature!  Recently I
happened to see a letter written by your friend, the great poet;4 the levity and
ribaldry in it were frightening. Can you tell me how such a man, who at one
time was filled with sadness for everything, fail to feel a single small twinge of
sorrow at the death of an entire world? For consider, my friend; in truth, is it
not an entire world that is perishing? What is taking place is nothing other than a
terrible catastrophy, especially for one who has no advance knowledge of the
new world which is to supercede  it.  Are you also left unaffected in your
thoughts and emotions by this? Tam certain that, unknown. to you, this. thought
and this emotion are buried in the depths of your soul; it's just that they do not
manifest themselves, they are in all probability hidden; they exist under that mass
of old ideas, habits and conventions with which, whatever you might say, every
poet is inevitably filled, whatever he does; for, my friend, from the Indian
Valmiki, the singer of the Ramaiana and the Greek Orpheus, to the Scot Byron,
every poet in his day has had to repeat the same thing, wherever in the world he
sings.

--- PDF page 154 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                                    151


    Oh, how I should like to be able to evoke all the powers of your poetic
being! How I should like to extract from it, even at this moment, all that I know
lies hidden there, so that we could hear from you one of those songs that the
epoch requires! And then, all that passes today before your eyes without leaving
any trace on your mind would immediately strike you! Everything would take
on a new appearance in your eyes!
    But until this -happens, let us talk. -Quite recently, about -a year -ago, the
world lived in perfect confidence about its present and future and studied in
silence its past, learning from it. The intellect was being regenerated in peace,
man's memory was being renewed, opinions were becoming reconciled to one
another, passion was being repressed,anger was deprived of-sustenance, -vanity
found satisfaction in good works; all human needs were gradually becoming
confined to the realm of mental activity and all the interests of man were
gradually being subsumed by the interest of the progress of the universal
intelligence. For me this was an article of absolute faith, a source of boundless
belief. In tlIis felicitous calm of the world, in this future I found my own calm,
my own future. And then the stupidity of a man, one of those men who are
called, without their agreement, to rule the affairs of man, suddenly intervened.
And the world, safety, future - all this became naught. Only think:  it was not
one of those great events that overturn kingdoms and bring death to peoples, but
the absurd stupidity of one man5 that was the cause of this! Living as you do in
a whirlwind you could not have felt about this the same way as I did; which is
quite understandable. But is it possible that you could see this unprecedented
and terrible event bearing the clear stamp of Providence as merely mundane
prose, or at best didactic poetry, like the earthquake of Lisbon, which could be
of no interest to you? Impossible! As for me, tears fill my eyes when I see the
vast calamity that has befallen that ancient, my ancient, society; this general
catastrophe, which befell Europe in such an unforseen manner, has doubled my
own unhappiness. And yet, I am quite certain that only good will come of this,
and it is a source of solace for me to see that I am not the only one to retain hope
that reason will revert to being reasonable.  But how and when will this
reversion take place?  Will it be achieved by some powerful spirit sent by
Providence on a unique mission, or will this be the result of a sequence of events
brought about by Providence for the instruction of mankind?  I do not know.
But a vague feeling tells me that soon a man will come bearing the secret of time.
Perhaps at first this will be something akin to the political religion currently
preached by Saint-Simon in Paris, or to the new kind of Catholicism6 with
which a few bold priests are trying to replace the old faith, sanctified by time.
And why not? Does it matter how the movement that will conclude the destinies
of mankind will begin? Much of what had preceded the great moment long ago
when the Good News was proclaimed by the Divine Herald was meant 'to
prepare the universe; undoubtedly much will also take place in our own time
with the same purpose before the new Good News will be brought to us from
heaven. We shall await it. It is said that rumors of a general war are circulating.

--- PDF page 155 / 323 ---

152                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


I insist that nothing of the kind will happen. No, my friend, the ways of blood
are not the ways of Providence. However stupid people may be, they will not
fight each other like beasts: the last river of blood has run its course and now, at
the moment  I write to you,  its source, thank God, has been exhausted.
Certainly, storms and disasters still threaten us; but the tears of the peoples will
no longer be the source of the benefits they are destined to receive; from now on
the only wars that will take place will be accidental ones, a few senseless and
ridiculous wars which will serve completely to destroy the desire of men for
destruction and murder. Did you notice what has just happened in France? Was
it not believed that she was about to ignite the four corners of the world? And
what? Nothing of the kind occurred; and what happened instead? The lovers of
glory and conquests became the subject of ridicule; the men of peace and reason
triumphed; the old phrases, which even recently had rung so beautifully in
French ears no longer meet with a response.
  A response!  By the way, a few words about  it.  Of course it is quite
fortunate that Messers Lamarck and his cohorts do not find a response in
France;7 but will I find one, my friend, in your soul? We shall see. However,
at the very thought that this might not be so the pen falls out of my hand.
Whether I will pick it up depends on you; I look for a measure of sympathy in
your next letter.  Mr. Nashchokin tells me that you are extraordinarily lazy.
Rummage a bit in -your head and especially in your heart, which beats with such
ardor when it wants:  there you will find more subjects for our correspondence
than we will need for the rest of your days.  Farewell, my good and old friend.
What about my manuscript?  I almost forgot about it. Pray do not forget about
it. Chaadaev

18 September

      I am told that you have been appointed or in some other manner authorized
to write a history of Peter the Great. Good luck!  I congratulate you with all my
heart.
    Before saying something on this subject I shall await your own comments
about it. And so, farewell.
     I have just read your two poems.8 My friend, never have you given me
such pleasure. At last, you are a national poet; you have finally divined your
vocation.   I cannot express to you the satisfaction that you made me feel. We
shall talk about this another time, and at length.  I don't know whether you
understand me correctly. The poem addressed to the enemies oof Russia is
especially admirable; it is I who tell you this:  It contains more ideas than were
expressed and implemented in this country in the last one hundred years. Yes,
my friend, do write the history of Peter the Great. Not everyone here agrees
with me, and you yourself probably suspect this; but let them talk, while we
march forward; when one divines ... the small portion of the force that moves us,

another time one will divine it... probably in its entirety.  I feel the urge to say:
here at last is our Dante ... perhaps one that is too precocious. Let us wait.

Letter to Schelling, 18321

Sir,
     I don't know whether you remember a young man of Russian nationality
whom you saw at Karlsbad in 1825.2  He had the opportunity of often
conversing with you about philosophical subject and you did him the honor of
saying to him that you got some satisfaction in communicating your thoughts to
him. You told him, among other things, that you had modified your ideas on
many points, and you advised him to await for the appearance of a new work,
which you were writing then, in order to find _our _about your philosophy. This
work has not appeared, and this young man was myself. In the meantime, sir, I
have read all your writings. To say that following in your footsteps, I was
elevated to those heights to which your genius has carried you by such a
beautiful flight this would be, perhaps, presumptuous on my part; I remember
that you found that Mr. Cousin3 had not understood you very well; I would be
very indiscreet, a man unknown in the European world, to pretend to surpass
one of such great literary reknown; but you will permit me, I believe, to say to
you, that the study of your works has opened a new world to me; by the light of
your spirit I have been able to see imperfectly into the domain of spacial thought
which had been entirely hidden from me, this study has been a source of fertile
and delicious meditations for me; permit me to tell you also that in following you
along your sublime routes, it often occured to me to stop at places other than
those you came to. Today I just learned through a friend who lately has passed
some days in your presence that you are teaching a philosophy of revelation.
This public lecture which you deliver now, sir, is, I presume, the development
of this thought which germinated in your mind when I saw you in Karlsbad.  I
am not aware of what the doctrine can be which you expose at this hour in your
auditorium, although, I admit I often believed that I had an inkling, in reading
you, that a religious philosophy was bound to flow from your system one day,
but I cannot tell you how happy I have been to learn that the most profollnd
thinker of our time, has arrived at this great idea of the fusion of philosophy with
religion. From the first moment when I began to philosophize, this idea occured
to me as the beacon and the goal of all my intellectual work All the interests of
my existence, all the curiosity of my reason, was absorbed by this unique idea;
and the more I advanced in meditation the more I became convinced that it was
also the great interest of humanity. Each new thought which came to be grouped
in my head with this first thought, seemed to me a rock which I carried to the
construction of ~e temple in which all men one day were going to unite, in order
to adore there  the evident God in perfect knowledge. Lost in the intellectual
solitudes of my country, I believed for a long time that was the only one

--- PDF page 157 / 323 ---

154                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


exhausting myself in this labor or at least with only a few companions
disseminated throughout the earth; I discovered later that every thinking person
was marching in the same direction; and this was a great day for me when I
made this discovery.  But at the same time I was struck by the need for an
individual lofty reason, an isolated great agent made for guiding all the reasons,
and all the agents from the masses. Thus, naturally I thought of you.  I said to
myself, as for this man so highly placed in the moral sphere of the world, to
whom humanity owes in great part the fact of having rediscovered its initial and
holy intuitions, can it be that the new light which is certainly going to enlighten
us all, is not going to light in his eyes with all its burst, before the new light
manifests itself to the eyes of everyone? Won't he who .reconciledso many
diverse elements of human thought, also come to reconcile the religious with the
philosophic element which already touch on each other? Finally, in my intimate
wishes for progress an for perfection, I destined you to accomplish the great
revolution towards which, according to me, the new reason was tending: and
here is what they tell me; that it is no longer terrestial science which your
eloquent words preach, but heavenly science, here my wishes, my feelings
about the future are in some way realized!
     First, sir, I wanted to write to you not only to thank you. But, at this hour,
I cannot resist the desire to know something about this new state of your system.
Would it be too much to ask you (without any other claim to this favor other than
my passion for the progress of human reason and my quality as a citizen of a
country greatly in need of enlightenment) some data on the general bases, or on
the fundamental idea of your present doctrine?  For, however powerful your
voice is, sir, it does not reach our latitudes; we are very far from you, sir; we
belong to another solar system; and the luminous ray parting from some star in
your world has an enormous road to cover before arriving in ours, and often it
gets lost along the way.
    If Mr. Turgenev, the friend about whom I was speaking with you before, is
still in contact with you, he could perhaps tell you that my studies and my works
render me worthy of your attention.  In any case, at this moment I don't wish
either to tell you of my own ideas, or to submit to your authority what I also call
my system; I know that, if I ought to count on something this time, it is only on
the interest which you yourself might find in introducing your philosophy, not
only to myself alone, but through my intermediary to the whole young
generation, poor at present, but rich with future, so desirous of enlightenment
that the young generation has few means of satisfying its ardor for instruction.
Its great destinies could not be indifferent to the wise man who aspires to
embrace the universal destiny of all things.  I desire strongly, sir, not to be
mistaken in my conjecture this time as the other, but, although it may happen, I
will never cease either to admire you, or to guard in my memory those few hours
during which I rejoiced in your conversation.
   Would you, sir, accept the assurances of my profound respect.

Letter to A. 1. Turgenev, April 20, (1833)1

    Here, my friend, is a letter for the illustrious Schelling, please make certain
 that it reaches him. A word which you said to me one day about him in one of
your letters to Madame your cousin,2 gave me the idea of writing to him. The
 letter is open, you will read it and you will see what it is all about. Since I speak
 about you, I wanted the letter to come to him through you. In sending it on to
him, I'd like you to let him know that I know German; for I would like him to
write to me (if he does me that honor), in the language in which he has so often
made my friend Plato live again, and in which through him science has become
poetry and geometry at one and the same time, and already at this hour, perhaps
religion. And heaven grant it!  It is time that all be but one and the same thing.
You tell Madame Bravoura3 that you do not know what to talk with me about.
Very well, here is matter for conversation, in order to begin; then, we shall see.
But you, my friend, you must write to me in French. Don't be displeased, I like
your French letter better than your Russian letters. In your French letters there is
more flexibility, there you are more yourself.  But, it is when you are wholly
yourself, that you are good.  Your circulars in the national language are
journalistic articles, my friend, journalistic articles! And I'm giving you a fine
compliment at that:  but it is for that reason that I do not like them. However,
your French letters given the appearance of being nothing, and it is for that
reason that I find them excellent.  If I were writing to a woman, I would say that
the letters resemble you.
    Moreover, you are essentially a European.  You know that I know  it.
French is really the garment which you have a duty to wear.  You have
squandered all the pieces of your national dress on the great routes of the
civilized world.  Then, write in French, and don't be ashamed please
considering that, thanks to the new school of such good composition, henceforth
you need no longer get into disorder in French as in Japanese, in which
language, they say, people write indifferently from top to bottom and from
bottom to top, from right to left and from left to right without it inconveniencing
anyone.
    Here (in a journal) they have just published an article on our philosopher: a
superficiality without equal, as you well surmise.   If he wants people to
understand him in this country, he would do well, I believe, to answer my letter.
As all people, so we others also advance today, in step, in our own way, if you
want, but we always run, that is certain. In a little while I don't doubt that great
ideas, once they will reach us, will find more facility in realizing themselves
among us, in incarnating themselves in individuals, than anywhere else, because
in our country the great ideas will have neither inveterate prejudices, nor old
habits, nor obstinate routines, to fight against. Thus, for the European thinker,
the fate of his meditations in our country at present could not be totally
indifferent to him, it seems to me. Besides, you will see, in reading my epistle,
that it is not meant to attract a letter for myself from the great man, that I write to

--- PDF page 159 / 323 ---

156                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


him, and that there is no vanity in my act, but that it's very simply that I want to
know what the point of it all is and where the human spirit is in this chapter.
     I would like my friend to talk with you a little bit. But I will wait, in order
to be better orientated, until you'll write me first.  Perhaps we'll have, who
knows, many good and serious things to say to each other, and which will not
be lost in space without leaving vestiges? In the meantime I must dispute with
you, as is my custom. How does it happen that you, living in Rome don't
understand it, after all that which we have said and resaid to one another about it!
But realize then once that it is not a city like the others, a mass of stones and of
population, it is an idea, it is an immense fact.  It is neither the tower of the
Capitol, nor the lamp of St. Peter that you must see, it is this intellectual summit
with which it is so easy to become enamored when one treads on Rome's sacred
soil. Then Rome transfigures herself entirely before you. You'll see the great
shadows of her monuments project themselves over the whole face of the earth
in prodi.gious teachings, you'll hear a powerful voice resound from her silent
mass and tell you of ineffable mysteries.  You'll know that Rome is the link
between ancient and modem times, because it is absolutely necessary that there
be one point on the earth towards which every man can sometimes tum in order
to discover materially, physiologically, all the memories of humanity, something
perceptible, palpable, in which is visibly contained the thought of the times, and
it is Rome which is that very point. Then this prophetic ruin will relate to you all
the destinies of the world; this will be a whole philosophy of history for you, a
whole doctrine, more than that, a living apocalypse. Then is there a way of not
humbling yourself before this prestigious image of so much eternity, is there a
way of not throwing a veil over her shocking face!  But the Pope, the Pope!
Very well, the Pope, what is this but only an idea now, a wholly abstract thing?
Look then at this figure of an old man, carried on his litter, on his dais, with his
triple crown, in the same way always for a thousand years, as if nothing had
happened; seriously, where is the man there?  Isn't this the all-powerful symbol
of the times, not this time which passes away, but this time which does not stir,
which everything passes through, but which itself remains immobile, and in
which, and by which, everything is accomplished?  Say, do you want then
absolutely no intellectual monument which lasts on earth? Do you need, as an
example of human work, nothing but the granite pyramid which knows how to
fight against the law of death, nothing?
   Good night, my friend. The rest for another time, if you want.  Write to
me. Adieu.
    Apropos.  I see many of your friends, all your women, the Pachkovs, the
Kindiakovs,4 etc. Everyone of them loves you and salutes you most tenderly,
and I do the same.

Moscow, 20 April5

Letter to A. I. Turgenev, May 1, (1835)1

--- PDF page 160 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                               157


    Your letter, dear friend, brought me much pleasure.  It was filled with the
warmth for things of universal interest which becomes rarer among us every
day: soon there will no longer be any question about it. But I must tell you also
that your letter brought me some troubled feelings. The writing about which you
speak isn't worth anything at all;2 it is for that reason that I wanted to get it back
from you upon your departure. Thus, I do not pretend to be answerable for it.
You are going to receive another copy of the same thing; throw that one in the
fIre and may no trace of it remain. You realize, therefore, that I have nothing to
reiterate against the benevolent censure of the countess Rzhevskaiia.3 Explain to
her please if you see her again, that I am very touched by her feelings, and that
in my capacity of philosopher for the ladies, I am making an infinite case of it.
Who knows? Perhaps, one day, I will tell her that myself in person. If ever I
leave my country, she can be well assured that it will not cost me anything to
change my direction in 200 places, -and even more, in -order to go to her to pay
my respects. But in waiting to go to find this intellectual woman there, imagine
 all intellectual women are leaving here. There's Madame Orlov4 who's leaving;
there's Madame Bravoura5 who's leaving; there is Madame Elagin6 who's
leaving; there's princes Meshcherskaia7 who has left. The last, at least, is going
to return; as for the others, they are going to look for you in Italy; I did not
refrain from saying bon voyage to them, think it over, for God knows, we have
enough of stupidity without that!
   Do people speak to you about our latest productions?  First we have a
volume of the short stories by Pavlov.8 Try to procure it for yourself, and read
the first story:  you'll tell me about the novels.  If I am not badly mistaken, this
piece is an event. Then we have a drama.  Another event, but in a different
sense. The play is entitled "Skopin Shuiskii"; the author is Kukolnik,9 a kind of
Victor Hugo on a small scale, and without the intentions, of course, You know
that this Skopin Shuiskii is one of the most remarkable figures in our history, the
sole figure of this grandeur in all our annals; this is a civilized hero, a hero in the
style of the West.  But, in the drama he is not the dominant character, it is
Liapunov. The latter is a savage, a barbarian, who crushes Shuisky completely
by his barbaric stature, and who is the great man of the poem. For him then the
applause, for him the fanaticism of the public. You understand the meaning of
this beautiful conception? There are bits of ferocious energy against all that
comes from the West, against every kind of civilization: and things for the pit to
clap abo.ut.  Finally, what shall I say to you?  It is the apothesis of barbarism.
There is, my friend, the spot in which we have landed. Still, there is a curious
thing which you will find in the library. A cry of the crowd enraged against the
German philosophy. Note that well; never did literary impudence, never did
spiritual cynicisrrigo -further; and -what is even more amusing, is -that this article
leaned upon a charming letter of Zhukovskii  , all molded, by the Germanic
spirit. 10

--- PDF page 161 / 323 ---

158                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


    In our country at the present moment a peculiar operation is taking place in
our spirits. People are trying to work out some kind of nationality, which, not
being able to base itself on anything, since any material for that is lacking
absolutely, will be, should it succeed in being fabricated, logically only a very
artificial creation. Thus, poetry, history, art, are all going to be precipitated into
the abyss of lying and deception, and all that in a century in which, in other
places, a formidable analysis lays hands on all illusions left in the intellectual
spheres.  It is impossible to foretell at present where that will lead us to; there is
perhaps basically a hidden good which will occur at the fulfilled hour;  it is
possible that this is no more than a work of analysis, which will cause us to
understand, finally, that it is on a high and profound evaluation of our present
position before the century, that we ought to seek to establish our future,and -not
on a past which is -nothing but a nothingness. Whatever maybe,in awaiting for
the views of Providence to manifest themselves, this tendency seems to be a
genuine calamity for me.  Well, isn't a pity to see us simply retreating into
ourselves and returning to parochialism at the moment when all peoples
fraternize together, when all local or geographical personalities are erased. You
know that, in my opinion, Russia was called upon to furnish some immense
intellectual development: Russia was one day supposed to provide the solution
to all the questions which are being debated in Europe. Placed beyond the rapid
movement which carries along the spirits there, being able to consider with calm
and with a perfect impartiality, all that agitates and impassions the souls _in
Europe, Russia was invested, in my opinion, with the task of explaining the
human enigma one day. But should this tendency be prolonged, goodbye my
beautiful hopes! Figure out whether I am happy about it!  I, who loved only the
future of my country, what do you wish -me to do about it then? Instead of this
detached, unprejudiced view, without the egoism which still holds back the final
development of intelligence in the old society, instead of the point of view which
the very nature of things imposes on us; instead of this powerful elan which
should carry us along in one single effort, where the other peoples could only
have arrived by unheard of efforts and by frightening calamities, instead of a
vast thought which moreover could only have been the result of a work of the
mind which has engulfed centuries and generations, people here prefer the
narrow idea which all nations spurn today and which is in any case fading away.
Very well, so be it; I am no longer going to get mixed up in it.  I have expressed
my thought aloud, the good God will do the rest.   Good day, my friend.
Adveniat regnum tuum.
   Do me the pleasure of picking up some bits of information under the name
Philarete Chasles,11 whose excellent articles I sometimes find in the Revue de
Paris. Finally, who is the abbe Lacordaire?12 Madame Svechin13 could certainly
give you some news about -it.  Princess Meshcherskaial4 whohas returned,
charged me to tell you that she has spoken of your Forgotten Ones, and that
these  little persecutions are practiced without the knowledge of the high

--- PDF page 162 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                               159


authorities, and that the persecutors have already been reprimanded in this
regard.

Letter to A. I. Turgenev(1835)1

   Thank you, my friend, for your very interesting communication.   It's a
genuine epistolary Revue. Your letter from London, excited my lively interest in
a special way.  It shows that a similar idea persists from one end of the universe
to the other and that there's really a universal Spirit hovering over the world, this
Welt-Geist, which Schelling spoke to me about and before which he bowed
down with such majesty; men can thus link arms across immense distances;
there's no limit to thought, and this infinite chain of men who think in unison,
who pursue the same object with all the strength in their souls and in their
spirits, advances then at the same pace and encircles the entire universe.
Continue to make me feel the movement of the world: your efforts, I hope, will
not be lost. There are, however, things which escape you. You haven't spoken
to me about the latest work of Heine.2  It's true that most of the chapters in this
unusual book have appeared previously in different journals, but it's impossible
for their reappearance together as a whole not to have moved the philosophic
world. Do you know what I call Heine? the Fieschi of philosophy. You know
that he compares Kant with Robespierre, Fichte to Napoleon, and Schelling to
Charles X.  I have then only continued the use of the comparison, and have
come very naturally to the shocking equivalency between those two satanic
beings, both king-killers each in his own way.  I like to believe that Fieschi has
not succeeded any more than the other; but nonetheless his book is an attempt
completely similar to that of the boulevard, only with this difference that the
kings of Heine are more legitimate than those of Fieschi; first of all, they go after
the good God Himself,and -finaUyall the balms of science and philosophy.
Besides, both signify a similar anarchial principle the same consequence of your
glorious revolution; lastly, both have decidedly come from the Parisian scum.  I
don't know what your present opinion is of this volcanic eruption by all the
rabble of France, which has distorted the lamentable golden mean in the world,
but, as for me, every day I find more reasons for wishing something of the
golden mean on the world. Believe me, that event has pushed the world back a
half a century,it has completely confused all social ideas; and God knows when
the social ideas will get out of these difficulties!  This judgment is that of the
most impartial spirit possible, since it comes from a Russian mind, carried to the
whole extent of the impersonality proper to it. You know that I consider the
Russian intelligence to be impersonal intelligence par excellence. The fact is that
only at our di~tance from European developments can men evaluate them well.
With reference to Europe we are placed at the historical point of view or, if you
prefer, we are the public, over there are the actors, it's up to us to judge the play.
    This great play which is being acted out by the European people and at
which we, cold and unmoved spectators, called The Dissatisfied assist, makes

--- PDF page 163 / 323 ---

160                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


me think of the little play of M. Zagoskin,3 which is going to be perfonned here,
at which a cold and unmoved public will also assist.  The members of the
Dissatisfied! Do you sense all the maliciousness in this title?  I don't understand
myself where the author has found the characters for his drama. Thank God, in
our country one sees only perfectly happy and satisfied men. A foolish well-
being, a sanctimonious self-satisfaction, that's the salient trait of the epoch in our
country; and at the moment when all these blind, passionate, mutually inimical
nationalities, which Christians inherited from paganism, are being erased, and
when all the civilized nations are starting to abjure their respective self-conceit,
it's remarkable that we dare to stand thus in imbecilic contemplation of our
imaginary perfections. They say that Orlov4 and myself, come on the scene in
the new play. A strange idea to make a frondeur -ofOrlov, the -most -perfectly,
the most fanatically -happy man in the world. And as for me, what -have I -done,
what have I said, that they accuse me of opposition?  I say and do only one
thing, I repeat that everything tends towards a goal, and that this goal is the
kingdom of God. Should perhaps the Lord's Prayer be placed on the Index?  I
say, it's true, sometimes that the earthly -powers -have never hindered the world
from advancing, since intelligence is an incompressible fluid like electricity; that
all good and great things mature in silence and in peace; that we should not get
involved in the western intrigues, because we do not belong to the West; that if
Russia understand _her mission _she _should take .the initiative in all noble ideas,
because she doesn't have European affectations, passions, ideas, or interests.
But what's heterodox in that, if you please? And why shouldn't I still say that
Russia is too powerful to be occupied with national politics; that Russia's
business in this world is the politics of mankind; that Emperor Alexander
understood that very well, and that this  is his most beautiful glory; that
Providence has made us too great to be egoists; that it placed us beyond
nationalistic interests, and has charged us with the interests of humanity;. that all
our ideas in life, in science, in art, ought to start from that principle and return to
it; that there' is our future, there is our progress; that we are an immense
spontaneity, without intimate  ties  to the world's past, without definite
relationship to  its present;  this  is our real, logical position;  that,  if we
misunderstand these bases, all our subsequent progress will be forever only
anomaly, anachronism, and absurdity. Blame M. Zagoskin for this rigmarole
and just let me say one word to you.  In my opinion, among us, there's
prodigious eccentricity. We accuse the government of all our crimes. The
government does its job, that's all; let's do ours, let's correct ourselves. To
consider indefinite liberty as the indispensable condition for the development of
intelligence is a particular error. Look at the Orient!  Isn't it the classical land of
despotism? However, there is a source for the world's enlightenment. Look at
the Arabs!  Have they any idea of the benefits of constitutional regime?
Nonetheless we owe them a good part of our knowledge. Look at the Middle
Ages. Did it have any idea of the ineffable delights of human equality? But the
human spirit acquired its greatest energy during the Middle Ages.  Finally, do

--- PDF page 164 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                                  161


you believe that the censure which put Galileo in prison was not equal to that of
Mr. Uvarov5 and company? However does the earth turn any less, launched by
 the kick of Galileo? Then think about it and you'll see what I mean.
      I just read an excellent article of Monsieur Marc Girardin6 in the Journal des
Debats about the new edition of St. John Chrysostom. If you know Monsieur
Girardin, tell him that a Moscovite philosopher, one of your friends, begs you to
thank him for it. They must know over there that reverberation for good things
resounds even in these regions in which the mean temperature stays at 300
degrees for two successive weeks, such as it did lately, and that the cold which
congeals the mercury does not congeal a profound idea. Perhaps they will also
realize that the century is not as atheistic as is said, when they see a religious idea
welcomed from so far away at the very instant when the idea was released by a
distinguished mind. Monsieur Girardin makes us see that all the recent progress
in the physical sciences serves as a support to the Genesiac system of the Bible,
and he backs his argument on the new treatise concerning electricity by M.
BecquereI.7 At the moment I write to you, I am finishing reading this treatise.
It's curious that the author in no way suspects the conclusion which can be
drawn from his book, even though he refutes even the proofs which Cuvier
furnished in favor of Moses' cosmology. There's another curious thing which I
find here. How is it that in this great work on electricity, in which men from all
civilized nations have participated, we have contributed nothing? There were
some observations on terrestial magnetism, made by strangers, by a Kupfer,S for
example but that's all. Once Simonov of Kazan9 is mentioned, but merely to
point out that his observation is valueless.  Let's admit that there's some
profound lacundity in our intellectual structure. For example, we absolutely lack
the capacity for logical deduction and methodical thinking. In his phrenological
classification of human capacities, SpurzheimlO designated this group by the
organ of causality,  it's precisely that organ which has remained without
development in our poor heads; you only have to test the cranium to be
convinced of that.  The reason for this is that ideas have never played the
dominant role in our lives, this is because we have never been moved by any
great beliefs, by any powerful convictions. Really what are the paltry events in
our religious history, in comparison with the conflagrations of Christian thought
in the West? And don't say that we're young, that we're behind other peoples
and that we'll catch up with them.  No, we no more belong to the European
fifteenth or sixteenth century than to the nineteenth. Take any epoch which you
want iit the history of western peoples, compareit to the one we belong to in the
age of grace 1835, and you'll see that we don't find that these nations always
lived an animated, intelligent, fertile life; and that an idea had been entrusted to
them from th~ beginning, and it's the pursuit of this idea, and its development,
which has formed all their history; finally that they have always created,
invented, and discovered.  Tell me what idea did we develop? What have we
discovered, invented, created? The solution then does not lie in running afterthe
others; we have to evaluate ourselves frankly, understand ourselves as we are,

--- PDF page 165 / 323 ---

162                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS

stop lying, and find out the truth.  After that we'll advance, and we'll advance
more rapidly than the others, because we have come after them, because we
possess all their experience and all the work of previous centuries.  The
European people are strangely mistaken about us.  For example, there is
Monsieur Jouffroy,11 who teaches us that we're destined to civilize Asia. That's
very good; but ask him then, please, what Asian peoples have we civilized?
Apparently the mastodons and the other fossil populations of Siberia are the only
races to my knowledge which we have drawn out of obscurity, and that still
thanks to men like Pallas12 and Fischer.13 They tenaciously kept handing us
over to the East; by a kind of instinct for European nationality, they drove us
back into the East, in order not to meet us in the West.  Let's not be duped by
their involuntary artifice; let's seek by ourselves to discover our future, and let's
not ask others to do what we must do. The East belongs to the masters of the
sea, that's evident; we are further away from it than the English, and we no
longer live at the time when all the eastern revolutions came from central Asia.
The new charter of the Indian Company is henceforth the real civilizing element
in Asia.  It's Europe, on the contrary, which we are destined to teach about an
infinity of things which Europe could not understand along. Don't laugh: you
know that this is my intimate conviction. A day will come on which we will
place ourselves amid intellectual Europe, as we are already placed amid political
Europe, then we will be more powerful by our intelligence than we .are today by
our material power. This will be the logical result of our long solitude:  great
things have always come out of the desert.  The powerful voice which  is
resounding in the world, will singularly serve to hasten the accomplishment of
our destinies. Struck with stupor and surprise, Europe has pushed us away with
hatred; the fatal page of our history written by the hand of Peter the Great.is torn
up; thank God, we no longer belong to Europe:  since this day then, our
universal mission has begun. Besides, see if ever an act of the government was
welcomed w~th more national sympathies, if ever more perfect accord was seen
between the sentiments of the sovereign and those of the people! That's because
it's Providence itself which spoke from the mouth of the prince:  that's why all
the national instincts have bowed down before this voice from heaven.
    That's already enough of philosophy; let's talk a little about ourselves.  I
have recently obtained news about our illustrious Schelling through the young
Gagarin.14  If all that this young man told me about his philosophy is not
exaggerated, I can not help being very interested in this application of the
teaching of Identity to my worthless person.  It seemed that the miserable article
of The Library has not reached him. But as for you, you no longer speak of
Monsieur Ballanche.15 Do you know that have built a whole philosophy on his
feelings.  I admit, however, that I was very surprised about the judgement that
he made about my writing:  certainly, the nasty copy which he has had in his
hands could not have made him unprejudiced against me. Whatever may be, in
the interest of philosophy, you should not let the links dissolve, since you
yourself contributed to form them. A man with whom I would still very much

--- PDF page 166 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                                163


like to come in contact with is M. de Genoude.1 6 There's life in this priestly
soul: he does not stand with his arms crossed watching the world pass by, he
knocks on all doors, he goes everywhere with his Christ.  This is Catholic
philosophy. The principle of Catholicism is first and foremost a principle of
action, a social principle. This is the character which you'll find in it at every
epoch in modern times. Catholicism alone has understood:thereignof-God;not
only as idea, but as a fact, because it is in possession of these sacred traditions,
this doctrine of the elect which, at all times, has caused the world to exist
without the world suspecting it: In mundo erat, et mundus per ipsumfactus est,
et mundus eumnon cognovit. You see that my religion isn't exactly that of
theologians, and you'll perhaps tell me that it's not even that of the people. But I
myself will say to you that it's the religion which is at the basis of the spirit,and
not that religion which is at the ends of tongues; this is the religion of life, and
not that of form; this is the religion which is, and not one which seems to be;
lastly, this is the long-awaited religion which all ardent hearts, all profound souls
evoke today, and which, according to the great historian of the future, will one
day become the definitive cult and the total life of humanity; but which in the
meantime does not clash against the popular beliefs, which, on the contrary
adopts them, in its vast charity, although surpassing them. If I had found such a
completed religion around me, when I was seeking one of them, I would have
assuredly taken it up; but not having found any, I was forced to embrace the
community of the Fenelons, the Pascals, the Leibnitzes, and the Bacons.  In
fact, you were wrong to stamp me a true Catholic.  I don't deny my beliefs, no
doubt about it; it would not become me, moreover, today since my head begins
to grow grey, to falsify a life full of convictions; however, I admit I would not
like to find the door of the hospital closed before me when I come to knock there
one of these mornings. May this peroration not affect you; you know that I have
long prepared for the catastrophe which should serve the ending of my history.
My country will not prove my system wrong, that's very certain.
   Good day, my friend, I love to believe that you will no longer reproach my
taciturnity, and that you yourself will go your way always. Give me news of
Madame Svechin;17 you should believe that I am only able to interest myself
profoundly in all that concerns this lady. As for your commissions, they will be
filled just as soon as they occur, if not always with success, at least always with
zeal. Besides, one must be more wise than I am in the physiology of passions in
order to serve you well in all things.  I always love you well, and that I do my
best. Ad. reg. tuum.

philosophical elegance, and that am going to write him incessantly. You never
