very bad of you.

Letter to Ivan Iakushkin, May 2 (1836)1

My Dear Friend,
    Here is a book2 which Madame Levashev3 sends to you.  I substituted the
copy which I myself used instead of the one which was meant for you, so that
you could focus your attention upon the things which attracted mine - you will
find them underlined with my pencil.  I experienced an extreme pleasure in
learning that you occupy yourself with these serious studies which are so well
designed to lighten the pains of your existence.  I know that you have not ceased
to study during your exile.  It is a great favor from Providence that you have
been allowed to preserve this scientific taste amid the miseries which human
justice has inflicted· on you.   It is impossible for you not to feel profound
gratitude to Him from whom all good things come, no matter what your
philosophical opinions happen to be. As for me, I have the intimate conviction
that the courageous and calm acceptance with which you took on your position
and the feelings of sweet good will and perfect charity which you have always
encountered under the -weight of your great misfortune, have ·already -won for
you new insights into many things. And so, I believe that in inviting you to
meditate carefully upon some of the underlined passages in this book, I will only
be continuing the same work begun by God. The general idea which emerges
from the remains of the new force under which nature is offered to our eyes -by
the sequence of recent progress in the physical sciences is that the entire
cosmo gonic and system from Genesis of HebraIc traditions is supported by each
new discovery, by each step forward that the human spirit makes. And what is
curious is that Becquerel,4 while refuting euviers who had already furnished
formidable evidence in favor of this system, himself provides even more
formidable evidence for it.
   You see, my friend, that this letter was only meant to serve as a preface to
the reading which you are going to do. But could I write to you without talking
about something else, without casting a sad regard on the past so filled with
friendship, on the days past in the sweetest communications on the brink ofan
abyss? Oh, my friend, how could God have allowed you to do what you did?
How could he have permitted you to play with your destiny in this way, the
destiny of a great people and of your friends, beings whose intellects figured out
a thousand things which other intellects can barely envision after study and
work?  I would dare to use this language only with you, since I know you well
enough not to fear hurting you with some profound conviction or other.  I have
reflected much about my country since a tragic catastrophe6 threw spaces up
between us, and nothingse.ems .mor.eprov.ed today than that what is Jacking .in
our nation is profundity.

--- PDF page 168 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                                165


  We have passed through time as others, or almost, but never have we been
moved by any idea at all, and hence that is why the entire future of our country
was played out one day by some young people between a pipe and a glass of
wine. Nineteen hundred years ago, truth became incarnate and appeared in the
midst of men; they killed it, and this immense crime was the world's salvation.
But if the truth were to appear today in our midst, it would pass without being
perceived, and that crime would be even greater than the other, for it would
serve for nothing.
   How happy I would be, my friend, if the day comes when you will be able
to write, and people say that this day is close. The first lines which you may
address to me might let me know that you appreciate now the great error
committed by you and that in your solitude you have come to realize that an error
cannot be erased before supreme justice unless one confesses it, in the same way
that calculation can be corrected only after one has become aware of it. Advice,
my friend.   I am proud to have been able to say these things to you in the
certitude that your soul will not be wounded and that your lofty intellect will be
able to discern the feeling which inspired these things.
     I have not talked with you about my brother, since he lives in Nizhnyi and I
get news very rarely.  Natalia Shakhovskoi and her sister talked to me often
about you. Your children came to see me the other day; I embraced them with
happiness and with sorrow.

Pierre Tchaadayeff

Letter to A. I. Turgenev, May 25 (1836)1

    Here, my dear friend, is a letter for Baron d'Ekstein.2  I don't know where
the letter will find you, for you spoke to me about departing but you did not tell
me in what direction you were heading. My delay in writing to the Baron was
caused by the fact that the gentlemen of the Observator proposed doing
something with his brochure in their journal. They have done nothing about it;
and in the meantime I put off writing from one day to the next, in order to have
something to say to you about that.  It is said that we are neighbors of India:
Witness then whether we are curious about India!  That is what you'll say to
Mi~ter d'Ekstein if these letters arrive in Paris before your departure. You
infqrmed me about all kinds of things which you have expedited to Viasemskii
and whieh he should have passed on to me, once he had finished reading them.
But I have as yet received none of them. Nothing about Dominicale, nothing
about ~acordai,re.3 And as for him, I hope that in Rome he will think about
becoming Pope:  I guarantee him the favor of the Holy Spirit. The Holy Spirit
has always been the Spirit-of-the-century; that's what must be well understood.
Today the Church needs a Hildebrand who would be just as good for his time
and the other was for his. Why wouldn't Lacordaire be the man called for his
task? There are profound things enshrouded in the democratic element of the

--- PDF page 169 / 323 ---

166                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


Papacy. Who knows whether or not the coming conclave is not destined to
regenerate the Church?
    Here we have Pushkin.4 He is wholly emersed in his Peter the Great. His
book will be very apropos when all of Peter the Great will be destroyed:  this
will be his funeral oration. You know that he also is bringing out a journal
which he calls the Contemporary. Contemporary of what? Of the XVI century,
and not even of that? A singular rage in seeking to assimilate us to the rest of the
world. What do we have in common with Europe? The .steam machine, _that's
all. A profound idea ofM. de Tocqueville,5 which he has stolen from me, is that
the starting point of a people determines their destinies. Men do not want to
understand this in our country:  there, however is our history. More than ever
then, ad. Reg. tuum.
    Princess Meshcherskaia 6 is just as impatient as I to have a .small fragment of
Lacordaire, but that does not hinder us from greeting you very amicably. Good
day my friend.

Letter to Princess Sophia Meshcherskaia, October 15 (1836)1

    Here, princess, is a brochure2 which, I am sure will interest you; it is my
piece of writing translated and published in the idiom of the country. Publicity
has come to grab me by the neck at the moment when I least expected it. At fIrst
you will find the fact very peculiar undoubtedly, but in reflecting on it you will
change your opinion. Then after all, what is so marvelous about finally finding
among us an idea, old by two thousand years, profused, venerated, preached by
a thousand eminent minds, by a thousand holy persons? Would it not have been
even stranger if it had never taken place? If it is true that Christianity, such as it
is constructed in the West, was the principle which developed -there .andmade
everything germinate, it follows that the country which has not reaped all the
fruits of this religion, even though  it submitted to the law, may have
misunderstood the principle of Christianity up to a certain point, may have
disregarded in some way its true spirit and may have repulsed some of its
essential truths.  Hence one could not separate out the consequence of its
principle and that which determined the principle to be produced, i.e., that which
equally obliged the consequence to manifest itself.
    People say that the rumor is great; I am not surprised. In any case, I know
that my writing has obtained some favor in a certain region of society. Certainly
it was not at all made to please the blessed populace of our literary salons, given
over to the glorious practice of whist and the card game reverie. You know me
too well to be assured only that this fracas is of very little interest to me. You
know that I have never thought about the public, that I have never even been able
to conceive of how one could write for a public such as ours; one might as well
address the fish in the ocean, the birds in the sky. No matter what, if what I said
is true, that will endure, if not, must not endure.

--- PDF page 170 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                               167


    There are, Princess, acquaintances of yours who find that it would be in the
interest of public affairs to forbid the author from staying in the capital. What do
you think about it? Is it not similar to belittling public affairs and exaggerating
the author's influence? Fortunately our government is always more prudent than
the clamor of the mob which would dictate the government's action. But, if by
chance the wish of these good people should be realized, I will come, Princess,
to ask for asylum, and people will thus learn that seriously religious views,
however fully divergent, are always sympathetic towards one another.

Letter to Myself in the manner of Michael Orlov October (1836)1

     I have something to -say to you, -my friend, about the effect that your letter2
had on the public and I have my reasons to say it to you in writing. You recall
that I often said to you that your opinions would not meet with any congeniality
in the land.  I do not claim to earn any merit by my predictions, but you will
notice that the evidence has confirmed them. My national instinct was not
mistaken.  I could even boast about another of my advantages:  I am not an
idealist at all, as you know.  I credit a large part of the affairs of this world to the
material factor, and that is precisely why I appreciated better _than you the
intellectual disposition concerning the grave issue raised by you. The abstraction
in which you live has hidden the real-aspect of things from -you.  I know you too
well not to be assured that the movement triggered by your piece of writing has
not satisfied you at all.  And, although you did not take the initiative in its
publication, the publicity, once it was accepted by you, I know is not the sort
which you had aspired to. But finally, since you have not convinced anyone,
what could have been the usefulness of this publication? Do you think that you
have attained your goal in stirring up some minds and turning their attention
towards these matters for an instant? That is just what I would like you to know
and what I would love to ask you in writing rather than by word of mouth, so
that you might be able to tell me your complete thought at your leisure. Hence I
invite you with all the force of my friendship in the very interest of your own
ideas to take pen in hand at once. And, believe me, though I don't share your
opinions on many things, I don't attach less interest to the march of an idea
which was pronounced with such energy.

Letter to Ivan Iakuskin, October 19 (1837)1

   One year ago, my friend, I wrote to you;2 it was at the moment when we
learned that you were going to be moved and that henceforth one could
correspond with you.  I congratulated you discretely on this modification in your
position by inviting you to give me some news about yourself.  Unfortunately,
this letter went astray twice in the strangest way:  the first time thanks to the
jealous affection of your mother-in-law3 passionately attached to a monopoly of
your friendship; the second time because of the course of an adventure which

--- PDF page 171 / 323 ---

168                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


happened to me at that time and which I am going to tell you about in order to
clear my conscience. You know then that I have been entangled in writing about
various religious topics for a long time. During the long retreat that I imposed
upon myself after my return from abroad what I wrote remained ignored. But
no sooner had I left .my solitude and had reappeared .in the world .then all my
scribbling became known and soon obtained some favor so easily accorded to
every written work. My writings were thus read; they were copied, they became
known outside the country, and I received some flattering testimonials from
some literary celebrities. Some fragments were translated into Russian. There
even appeared a serious work all filled with my ideas which frankly honored me.
But then one fine day a Moscow journalist, whose journal was sadly slipping,
perceiving somewhere one of the warmest pages of mine, somehow obtained the
censor's approval and launched it in his journal.4 Then came a general uproar:
the journal suppressed, the editor frrst sent for to Petersburg and then exiled to
Vologda, the censor thrown out of his job, my papers seized, and fmally, as for
myself, declared insane by a supreme order and by special grace, they say.
Hence here I am insane for almost a year now .andeven awaiting a new order.
Such is, my friend, my lugubrious and ridiculous story. Now you understand
why my letter did not reach you. The fact is that is took a very different route,
and I have not seen it ever again.  I like to think anyway that the letter has not
been fruitless for the people who found it like a lawful prize, for, if I remember
well, the letter contained some things which could serve as a means for their
particular instruction. Let's now talk about other things.
    Undoubtedly you don't know that your cousin communicates some of your
letters to me from time to time. When your mother-in-law is not sulking, she
also lets me know those which you address to her.  I know with what courage
you support the weight of your lot.  I know that you have given yourself ovena
serious study, and I admire the innumerable, solid pieces of knowledge which
you have acquired in your exile.  I cannot tell you how happy I ·am about all that
and proud to have predicted it so well for you.
  My friend, there is an old adage, which moreover smells a  little of
paganism, that there is no more beautiful spectacle than that of a wise man
fighting against adversity. But I prefer the 10okJul1 of serenity which -you cast
upon the world from the bosom of your desolate solitude. That is what superb
antiquity was never able to discover; that is what a well-made spirit finds
naturally in our day.  However, although I do not know what your religious
feelings are today, I cannot believe, I must confess, that you have achieved this
peace of soul by means of this glacial Deism which minds in your category
professed at the epoch when we parted. The studies which you have engaged in
since must have led you to some strong meditations about the gravest questions
of the moral order, and it is impossible that you could definitively have remained
in this state of pusillanimous doubt which Deism is incapable of surmounting.
The natural sciences are moreover far from being hostile to religious beliefs to
day. Hence I like to think that the lucidity of your judgment will soon let you

--- PDF page 172 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                               169


envision the truths towards which they gravitate.  I must even say to you that in
this wandering letter about which I just talked to you,  I already had the
opportunity to remark, on the occasion of a volume of Becquerel5 which was
supposed to accompany the letter, that all the recent discoveries of science, of
electricity notably, came to the support of Christian traditions, confirming the
cosmo gonic system of the Bible. One day we shall return to these matters, but I
would just like you to know if you are acquainted with the works of Cuvier,6
given the fact that nothing could serve us as a better point of departure in our
philosophical excursions than the geological works of this man. You must say a
word about it the fIrst time that you write to me.
    Please pardon me, my friend, if my first letter is already very saturated with
my habitual preoccupations, but you understand that at this hour it is more
diffIcult than ever for me to remove the influence of ideas which form the entire
interest of my life, the only support for an unsettled existence. In any case, I am
far from imposing my opinions on you.  I know the direction of your mind, and
I realize that neither the age, nor the meditation nor the experience of a life
crossed by an immense disaster and by an immense teaching could modify an
intelligence such as yours. But I also know that the epoch in which we live is so
permeated with this regenerating fluid, which has already produced such
admirable results in all the spheres of human knowledge, that your mind,
however situated far away geographically from the great hearths of the
intellectual movement, could not be entirely foreign to its influence. You have
followed as best as you could the march of contemporary ideas. The orbit which
you run, however eccentric it may be, is nonetheless determined by the law of
universal gravitation of all things towards a same center and is enlightened by the
same sun which shines on all humanity. Hence you could not be so very much
behind the rest of the world. But, no matter what, you will certainly share my
view on one point,. namely that we have nothing better to do than to adhere, as
much as we can, to the scientifIc region. For the moment, we can do no better.

Goodday then, my friend.

To Michael Orlov, 18371

    Yes, my friend, let us cultivate our glorious friendship and let the world roll
along towards its indecipherable destiny. Both of us, battered by the storm, let
us hold hands and stand up amid the breakers. Let us not bend our bare heads
before the squalls which whistle around us.  But especially, let us not hope
anymore for anything, absolutely nothing for ourselves. Nothing renders one
slothful like a foolish hope. Undoubtedly hope is a virtue and there is one of the
most beautiful discoveries of our divine religion, but sometimes it also happens
to be only a nuisance. In effect, it is an immense nuisance like hoping when one
is plunged into a stagnant pool in which each movement causes you to sink even
more.  Hence, of the three theological virtues, let us practice the first two,

--- PDF page 173 / 323 ---

170                                 FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


Charity and Faith, and let us pray to God that he deign to pardon us if we forget
the third.2 In any case, let us hope for our brothers, for our children, for our
august fatherland, so great, so submissive, so calm. As for you and me, if the
world is so sad for us, who hinders us from mounting to heaven?  Isn't heaven
for those who carry it off by force? On this point, it is true, you are not of the
same opinion as 1. You have the misfortune to believe in death. For you heaven
is I don't know where, someplace beyond the tomb. You are numbered among
those who still think that life is not all of one price, that it is broken up into two
parts and that there exists an abyss between those two parts. You forget that it
has already been eighteen and a half centuries since that abyss was filled up.
Finally, you believe that the gravedigger's shovel lies between you and heaven.
Sad beliefs which do not wish to understand that eternity is nothing else but the
life of the just, the life for which the son of man afforded the model.  Eternity
can and must commence already in this world, and it will commence in effect
from the day when we will devotedly wish it to commence.3 Who does not
realize that the world which surrounds us is the world such as it really exists.
Who does not see that it is not a world fabricated by our hands, not one which
depends on us to annihilate it. Who imagines, as little children, that heaven is
the extended blue over our heads and that there is no way to ascend way up
there. A fatal heritage of the centuries in which the earth, not yet sanctified by
the sacrifice, found itself still not yet reconciled with heaven. Then when, oh
my God, will I see all my friends finally abjure all these bits of ignorance from
unclean paganism, when will they all know that there is only one way to be
Christian and that is to be it completely!   Still once I dreamed that it was up to
me to spread some good truths among them, and I spoke to them and sometimes
they heard me. But one day the hurricane came, the desert wind blew; then the
sands of the desert rose up, eyes were closed, ears were stopped up and my
voice was choked off. Thy will be done, oh my God! Your pauses are always
just and our wishes always indiscreet. However, it was a beautiful dream, and I
dare say, one of a just intellect and a good citizen. Why do I still not speak? For
a long time, I admit, I aspired to the sweet satisfaction of seeing reunited around
me some chaste and serious minds, some warm and pure souls, in order to
invoke together favors from heaven for humanity and for the fatherland. Since
my country was undefiled by all the emotions which have left such profound
marks on intellects everywhere else, emotions which turned them so often, even
at this hour, from the good roads in order to launch them on bad roads, I thought
that my country was destined to be the first to proclaim great and holy truths
which the entire universe was expected to accept sooner or later.  I thought that
Russia was vested with the august task of realizing the promises of Christianity
before all the other countries in the world, because it had remained pure from
contact with human passions and earthly interests, because it had, like its divine
founder, done nothing but pray and humiliate itself, because it was probably due
to that very fact alone that it would be favored with the last and most powerful
inspirations.  Myths, my friend, they were all only myths.  Let the future be

--- PDF page 174 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                                  171


accomplished in what ever way it should be. Cross your arms and let it come.
Or else, bowing before holy images, as our pious and brave ancestors, those
heroes of all imaginable acts of obedience, await in silence and in prayer. Let it
burst upon us, for good or bad, no matter!

Letter to A. 1. Turgenev, 18371

   You ask our dear K[aterina] A[leksandrovna]2 why I am not writing, and I
ask you, why are you not writing?  Still, I am ready to write, all the more since I
have something to write about, namely the book3 that you were pleased to send
me with that scandalous post scriptum, a qui de droit.4 In my opinion it lacks
even that virtue which all the author's previous works had, the virtue of style.
And this is not surprising: an idea that is totally false cannot be expressed well.
I was always of the opinion that this man's point of departure was a lie, and now
I am even more convinced of that. How can one search for reason in the mob?
Where has it been seen that a-mob wasrational? Was hat -das Yolk-mit -der
Vernunft zu schaffen?5 I once said to some German. Let your Mr. Lamennais
come here and listen to what the mob says in our country; I should be curious to
see how he would apply his vox populi to vox dei here. Besides, this is not at
all a Christian belief. Everyone knows that Christianity first of all postulates that
truth resides not on earth but in heaven; and second, that when it appears on
earth it emerges not from the multitude but from among the select or elect.  I find
it inconceivable how a mind so elevated, endowed with such extraordinary gifts,
could assume this strange orientation, while seeing what was going on around
him, imbibing the air that had engendered the revolution incarnate _and the abs.urd
juste milieuu.6 He has only one precursor in the history of Christianity,
Savanarola; but what a difference! How profoundly did the latter comprehend
his mission, how exactly did he answer the exigencies of his time.  Political
Christianity has outlived its usefulness; in our age it has no meaning; it was
needed when the new society was being constructed, when a new law of social
life was being formulated. And that is why Western Christianity seems to me to
have fulfilled completely the general task with which Christianity was faced,
particularly in the West, where all the preconditions for the creation of a new
civil 6rder exited. But now things are different. The great deed has been done;
society has been constructed; it has received its laws; the instruments of endless
improvement have been presented to mankind; man has come of age:  Neither
episod,es.iof anarchy, nor episodes of oppression can any longer stop mankind
from following its path. Thus the reins of worldly power inevitably had to slip
out of the harids of the Roman Pontiff; political Christianity was replaced by a
purely spiritual Christianity; and where all earthly powers had reigned for so
long in every way imaginable, now there remain only thesymbolofthe unity of
thought, the great lesson and the monuments of times past.  In a word, today
Christianity must be nothing else but that supreme idea of time which contains
within itself all the ideas of the past and future ages, and consequently must act

--- PDF page 175 / 323 ---

172                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


upon the civil world only indirectly, through the power of ideas rather than
matter. More than ever before it must dwell in the realm of the spirit and
illuminate the world from there, and seek its final expression there.  At no
previous time was the multitude less capable of that collaboration which
Lamennais expects and demands of it. There can be no doubt that today much is
being done and said in the world, but is it possible to find the Voice of God
among the discordant accents of the thinking and unthinking people, in this
striving of one group for the purely material, of another for the purely
unrealisable?  It is also true that the eternal reason is occasionally manifested in
the deeds of man and that it may be to an extent traced in the history of the
peoples, but should we take as its expression the shout of every rabble which,
having momentarily stirred the air, leaves not the slightest trace? Here is what I
wrote to a friend of mine about that book.
    There is not an ounce of Christianity in all of this. Instead of demanding of
the heavens new inspirations of which the Church might have need in order to
regenerate herself, it is the peoples that he addresses,  it is the peoples he
questions,  it is of the peoples that he demands the truth, this heresiarch!
Fortunately for him, as well as for the peoples, the latter do not suspect that there
exists in the world a fallen angel, wandering in the darkness that he himself has
spread around himself, and crying to us from that darkness.  'Peoples, arise,
arise in the name of the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit!' Yes, this sinister
call has frightened all true Christians and has postponed the fulfillment of all the
final results of Christianity; in it the spirit of evil tried once again to destroy the
sacred unity, the precious gift that religion gave to men; finally, he himself
destroyed that which he had once created. So let us leave this man to his errors,
his conscience and the mercy of God, and may the burden of the scandal he has
caused be if possible a light one to him.
     I have just finished reading 'The Fire' by Viazemsky.7  I did not know him
to be such a good Frenchmen and a good Russian. Why didn't he decide to
write in the language of the infidels before? Without offence, he writes much
better in French than in Russian. Here we see the effect of good models which
unfortunately our country still lacks. In order to write well in our language one
must be an extraordinary man, one must be a Pushkin or a Karamzin.8  I am
aware that today few would agree to recognize Karamzin as an exceptional man;
the fanaticism of the so-called populism, a word that I believe lacks grammatical
meaning for a people that avails itself of the entire surfeit of its gigantic existence
in the way in which the latter was created by necessity, this fanaticism, I say,
causes many now to forget the conditions in which the human intellect develops
and how hard it is in our country for a man who was born with great abilities to
turn himself into a good writer.   Effectrix eloquentiae est audientium
approbatio,9 says Cicero, and this applies to all works of literature. As for
Karamzin in particular, I will tell you that every day I am learning to treasure his
memory more and more. What sublimity resided in that soul, what warmth was
contained in that heart! How sensibly, how intelligently he loved his country!

--- PDF page 176 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                                 173


How artlessly he admired its vastness and how well he understood that the entire
essence of Russia was contained in that vastness! And yet how he knew the
worth of everything foreign and gave it its just due! Where would one find the
likes of  this now?  And as a writer, how ·elegant and -sonorous his periods,
how true his aesthetic sense! The vividness of his pen is extraordinary; and in
the history of Russia this is the most important thing; reason would have
destroyed our history, only an artist's brush can re-create it. Now they say,
what does style matter? Write any way you can, just write sense. Sense, sense!
where is one to find it and who wi11listen to it?  I know that the intelligence of
other peoples did not develop in this way, there thought extended its arm to
imagination and the two proceeded together, there for a long time thinking was
conducted in a ready-made langauge, but others should not be an example to us,
we have our own path to follow.
   To return to V.,10 no one else in my opinion is better able to introduce
Russia to Europe. His case of mind is precisely of that kind that the European
public likes today. One would think that he had grown up in the Rue St. Honore
rather than next to Kolymazhnyi Dvor.

Letter to Ivan Iakushkin October 30 (1838)1 (Gershenzon published this letter
erroneously as a letter to Alexander Turgenev)

     I read your treatise2 with real pleasure, my friend.  I was exceedingly happy
to see the facility with which you have mastered this difficult subject,assimilated
the most recent scientific discoveries and applied them to it. Your piece is, in my
opinion, distinguished by a novelty of outlook, the soundness of its main ideas
and the diverting nature of the exposition; yet I cannot but make certain
observations with regard to the final lines, in which you touch upon matters that
are of great import to me and express opinions that it is impossible for me to
leave unanswered.  Still, even these lines pleased me, for in them I also see that
new, beneficent tendency of the universal spirit which I am so fond of following
and which I was able so often to foretell. And so let us turn to the crux of the
matter.
    You, as of old, distinguish between church teaching and science.3 I believe
that they should not be regarded as being different. There is of course a science
of the spirit and a science of the intellect, but both belong to our cognition and
both are subsumed in it. The method of acquisition and the extenal form are
different, but the essence of the thing is the same. The distinction you draw
dates to tre time when it was not yet known that our reason does not invent
everything on its own and that in order to come into motion it needs to contain
within itself something that it did not create, namely the instruments of motion or
rather the force of motion.  Thanks to the new philosophy,4 no thinking
individual can, I believe, doubt this any longer:   'tis a pity that not everyone
remembers this.  In general, this obsolete division which opposes science to
religion is not at all a philosophical one and, in addition, permit me to say, is

--- PDF page 177 / 323 ---

174                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


somewhat redolent of the 18th century which, as you yourself know,was very
fond of declaring the inaccessibility for our mind of the verities of faith in this
way, under the guise of a feigned respect for the teachings of the Church,
concealed its hostility towards her.  Your piece is written in a completely
different spirit, but ·that is ·exactly why the contradiction ·between its ideas and its
language is that much more striking.  .Still, it must also .be said that who .among
us does not happen to think modern thoughts but speak in the language of the
last century, and vice versa? and this is a most natural thing:  for how can we
manage to keep up in every aspect of .our gigantic, incoherent existence with the
evolution of the condensed, cohesive, long-established existence of the peoples
of the West, the inheritors of antiquity?  - It's impossible.
   The antediluvian events described in the Book of Genesis completely
belong, if you like, to history, intellectual history, of course, but one which
neverthless is the only true history. Without them, the progress of the human
intellect would be inexplicable; without them, the great act of redemption would
be meaningless and the so-called philosophy of history itself completely
impossible. Moreover, without the fall of man there is no psychology or even
logic; everything is darkness and absurdity. How are we to comprehend, for
example, the origin of the human mind and, therefore, its law, if we suppose
that man emerged from the hands of his creator in a form other than the one in
which he now recognizes himself? Besides, it must be stated that in the eyes of
pure reason there is no account more credible than the one given in the first
chapters of the Holy Bible, for there is no other so permeated with that absolute
truth which is superior to all other truths and particularly to one that is purely
historical in character. Of course this is a tale and one that is quite artless, but it
is also exalted speculation, and therefore must be verified not by ordinary
criticism but by the laws of reason.  Finally, if the biblical story of the first days
of the world is to a Christian nothing more than a canticle of an inspired witness
of the Creation, to the student of antiquity it is the oldest legend of mankind,
profoundly understood and cogently related. How could it be a part only of
religious teaching and not of history as a whole?  Is not deleting it from the
earliest annals of the world the same as removing the first act from a play or the
first canto from an epic? Indeed, how could a primary teaching, in which every
omission appears irretrievable, in which every word resounds throughout the life
of the pupil, fail to speak in its appropriate place, that is, in the history of the
Creation,  concerning what was in a sense Man's first encounter with God,
namely, the creation of his intellectual nature? How can one begin the history of
mankind without explaining whence mankind came? How can one start to study
a science from the second or third chapter of that science?
   The young mind which one wishes to prepare for the study of history must
be guided in such a way that all its subsequent concepts relating to this sphere
would necessarily stem from the primary concepts, and it seems to me that for
this to be achieved one must discuss everything in a place and at a time that are
appropriate; otherwise logical development will never occur.  Recall the time
when the human mind acquired those attributes, those instruments that it wields

--- PDF page 178 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                                175

so powerfully today? Was it not a time when all knowledge was religious
knowledge, when all science was founded on theology, when Aristotle was
almost a Father of the Church and St. Anselm of Canterbury the most celebrated
philosopher of his age? Of course, it would -be impossible for every -one of us to
repeat this entire development at home; but we can avail ourselves of these great
lessons, we should not voluntarily deprive ourselves of the rich heritage we
received from centuries past and from foreign peoples. Someone said that we
Russians lack a certain consistency in our minds and that we do not know how
to use the syllogism of the West. One cannot entirely agree with this harsh
judgement about our intelligence pronounced by an embittered mind, but neither
can one completely reject it.  There can be no doubt that our intelligence is
constituted in such a way that among us concepts to not arise one from the other
in a necessary sequence, but appear separately, suddenly and almost without
leaving a trace. We guess, rather than study; we appropriate with great skill
every foreign invention, but do not invent ourselves; we know not gradualness
in anything; we apprehend immediately, but also let much slip out of our hands.
In a word, we do not live by continuous mediation but by momentary thought.
And why is this so? Because we did not advance in a systematic way; because
in the course of our hurried progress we missed some things, learned others at
the wrong time and as a result found ourselves, without knowing how, at the
spot where we are now.  But if we genuinely wish to enter the field of the
endless progress of mankind, we must certainly endeavor to assimilate all our
future concepts with all possible logical rigor and pay more attention to the
method of our instruction than to anything else. Then perhaps we shall cease
merely to glide on the surface as we have been doing until now; then gradually
all the forces of our flexible and perspicacious minds will be revealed; then we
shall learn to comprehend things in their entirety and will at last become the
equals not only in appearance, but also in deed, of those peoples that followed
different paths and developed in a fashion that was more correct than ours, and it
could be that we will rapidly surpass them, for we have in comparison with them
great advantages, a selfless heart, a simiple faith.  Unlike them we are not
depressed by some dolorous past, a cloud of stubborn prejudice does not cast its
pall Clver us, and we use the fruits of all their inventions, endeavors, and labors.
   You say also that one must revere the wisdom of God in silence.  I must tell
you, my friend, that this too is a decayed phrase of the last century. One should
revere. the wisdom of God, but why in silence? No, it must be venerated not
with a voiceless, but with a full understanding, that is, with a profound thought
in the soul and with a living word on the lips. The wisdom of God never meant
to turn us into speechless animals and deprive us of that advantage which
distinguishes us from all other creatures. Revelation did not come into the world
in ordeno immerse it in some mysterious fog, but in orderto illuminate it with
an eternal light.  Revelation itself was a word: and a word is meant to evoke
another word, rather than silence. Tell me, where is it written that the Ruler of
the Worlds demands blind or mere worship? No, He rejects that inane faith
which turns a rational creature into a senseless brute; He demands a faith that is

--- PDF page 179 / 323 ---

176                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


full of vision, voice and life. And this is eternallifi, says the apostle, that they
know thee as the only God. And if faith is nothing but the knowledge of God,
then you yourself be the judge of whether it is not blasphemy to preach
senselessness in the name of faith?
    In conclusion I will say that one must never forget that our intellect is
composed not only of that which  it discovered or invented itself, but of
everything it knows. What does it matter whence and how that knowledge came
into it?  Some of it was acquired unconsciously, and now our intellect
apprehends it with full awareness; some of it was gained through centuries of
effect and labor, and now our intellect uses  it mechanically; but all that
knowledge belongs to it inalienably and is now forever a part of it. In a word,
reason or rather spirit is one in heaven and on earth; the continuous irvisible
emanations from the upper world into the lower, from the flrst moment that the
two worlds were created, always preserved an eternal identity between them; and
when the complete revelation or the incarnation of Divine Wisdom took place,
then there occurred the fusion of the two worlds into a single whole, which in its
essence can never be broken into pieces, neither through excogitation born of
arrogant speculation nor through the obdurate wilfullness of an intellect swollen
with its sense of identity, nor through the voluntary renunciation of a corrupt
heart. The World Spirit, renewed by the new exalted idea, is no longer capable
of rejecting it, breaths it, lives it, is guided by it and in spite of all the revolts of
various titans, deists, pantheists, rationalists and others, solemnly follows its
path and leads the whole of mankind to its exalted goal.
    This, my friend, is what I wanted to tell you; but I repeat that I read your
interesting piece with special pleasure and that I wish with all my heart that you
should continue your efforts.

The Madman, 1837 October 30th

"Two Letters to Princess Sophia M." Letter One.
Letter to Princess S. Meshcherskaia, May 27 (1839)1

   You haven't understood, Princess, and I'm not at all surprised. In order to
understand the refutation of a writing, you say, one has to be familiar with the
writing being refuted:  nothing is more true.  So, in a few words I'm going to
put you at the heart of the matter and tell you the foundation of my idea. But
flrst I have to warn you that it is impossible for me to admit the doctrine about
the equal importance of everything contained in Scripture. The works of a
healthy exegesis have taught me to distinguish in the holy book between the
direct action of the Holy Spirit and that of man's handiwork; the divine
inspiration which looks down on this marvelous work could not, as I see it then,
render each word equally sacred and inviolable, nor each syllable, nor each
letter, which men utilized, in order to translate the thought which the Holy Spirit
placed in their heart. In order to make itself understood by human reason, the

--- PDF page 180 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                               177


divine word had to utilize human language and, as a consequence, to submit to
the imperfections of this language; in the same way that the Son of God, in
making himself the son of man, accepted all the conditions of the body, so also
the spirit of God, in manifesting itself  in that of man, had to accept all the
conditions of the human word; but just as the Saviour does not triumph over the
body in each of His acts in life, but rather in His life viewed as a whole, so also
the Holy Spirit does not triumph over the human word in each line of Scripture,
but in its totality. To say that everything in the Bible, from one end to the other,
is inspiration, truth, teaching, is in my opinion, a misunderstanding of both the
nature of the influence exercised by the spirit of God on that of man, as well as
the divine principle of Christianity, so that because of that same divinity, the
letter could not always succeed in containing  it within the necessary
modifications. No, certainly, this is not what I wanted to say to you. God grant
that I never revert to these profound errors which cause so much evil for
humanity! Who knows whether or not all the dissentions of the Christian
society are due to this excessive veneration of the biblical text?  Or, that
supported on a text each sect, each heresy, has from time to time proclaimed
itself the sole, true Church of God? Or, that it is due to a text that some people
came to attribute the title of visible chief of Christianity to the Roman pontiff, the
vicar of Jesus Christ, and that it is with this text in hand that men contested his
supreme dignity with him and that men dispute it still?
   And allow me to say this to you, Princess, it is necessary, in my opinion,
that the road which leads through texts should be the most direct, it seems to me,
and not that which is the most tortuous and the longest: witness, once more, the
eternal discussions of the theologians and doctors of law which, as you know
very well turn only on the letter of Scripture.  The straightest road, in my
opinion, is that of a well disciplined reason, guided by a lucid belief disengaged
from any kind of greedy feeling. The text has the commodious factor in its favor
in that it imposes silence and causes foreheads to bow before it; thus at all times
it has been the asylum of religious pride.  But, how do you expect pride to
march on the straight road?  That's impossible. As for me, who am not, thank
God, either theologian or doctor of law, but merely a Christian philosopher,
only in this capacity have I taken up my pen in order to combat the opinions of a
man whom I love, and esteem, a man of knowledge, a man of the mind, but not
a philosopher, and one who believes, as so many others, that faith and reason
have nothing in common with each other: there is the error which I have sought
to refute, and that is the whole object of my letter.2
   Our friend pretends first that, in historical teaching, the cosmogony of the
Bible, that is to say , the revealed history of the creation of the universe, should
be omitted, because, according to him, this history belongs to the domain of
faith and not to that of science.  I have then tried to demonstrate that the history
of humanity would make no sense, if we did not trace it back to the first days of
the world and to the creation of man; that all would be necessarily chaos and
darkness in this sphere of human knowledge, if we did not cause the clear light

--- PDF page 181 / 323 ---

178                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


from the profound truths and admirable symbols contained in Genesis to
illuminate human knowledge. Coming to the crux of the subject, I said that the
divine founder of Christianity never planned to impose a dumb and myopic faith
on the world, as the author seems to suppose; that since Christianity is the word
and the light par excellence,  it should naturally provoke the word and
disseminate the greatest daylight possible on all the objects of man's intellectual
vision that, far from contradicting the facts of science, Christianity supported
them with its lofty authority, while science in its tum daily came to confIrm the
Christian truths by its discoveries; that Christianity had furnished new and
numerous instruments to the human spirit by means of the immense exercise
which it was made to undergo, when .the most illustrious saints of the Church
were at the same time the greatest philosophers of their age; finally, that
Christianity had demonstrated that the most fertile epochs in the history of the
human spirit were those in which science and religion clasped hands together.
   What I especially had in mind in the pages which you have just read is this
false tendency to perpetuate the schism which the 18th century established
between religion and science and which, neither the fathers of the church, nor
the doctors of the Middle Ages, those giants of religous thought, conceived of at
all, a tendency to which unfortunately more than one eminent and earnestly
religious spirit obstinately clings even now, despite the wholly opposite direction
of the century as a whole, which aspires, on the contrary, to return with all its
power to the behavior in the good times of strong beliefs and to fuse these two
great beacons of human intelligence into one sole cluster of light.
     I cannot naturally reiterate all that I said, but I should still add some words to
this summary recapitulation of my first words.  Yes, the Bible is the most
precious treasure of humanity; it is the source of all moral truth; it has spread
waves of clarity in the world, it has rehabilitated human intelligence and society;
humanity is obliged to the Bible in great part for the goods which it enjoys and
the Bible is destined probably to become the end of the miseries which still afflict
humanity; but let us be careful not to fall into the cult, the idolatry of the letter, let
us be careful especially not to imagine that all Christianity is confined in the
sacred volume.  No, a thousand times no.  Never would the divine word be
imprisoned between two plates of any book; the whole universe is not large
enough to contain it; it is in the infInite regions of the spirit that the divine word
lives, it is in the ineffable mystery of the Eucharist that it is embodied, it is on the
imperishable monument of the cross that the word has engraved its all-powerful
aphorism.
   You see, Princess, these lines contain a whole declaration of faith.  I thank
you for having furnished me with the opportunity to do this, and especially to
address it to you. My religious opinons, exposed to the absurd interpretations of
our salons, needed to be formulated in a manner be it ever far from absolute, and
when that is done, my religious opinions could not fInd a better judge than you,
whose whole life has been fIlled with the exercise of a sincere and enlightened
religion.

Letter to Ivan Gagarin, October 1 (1840)1

      I recommend, dear prince, to your national feelings of good will and others,
Mr. Galakhof,2 the bearer of this letter. You will find, I am certain, some
pleasure in being useful to him. He will remain only a little time in Paris,
consequently he will not abuse your patronage.  Please introduce him to
Turgenev and encourage the dear giddy-brained one to work a little in his favor.
If Madame de Circourt3 is in Paris at the moment when he presents himself at
your place, please introduce him in her salon, all the while recalling for us the
memory of the kindly countess.  In six months our voyager will be back in
Moscow; don't you find that this would be a good occasion to write to us post-
paid?
   Our society of the past year is not yet full; the majority of our debaters are
still debating themselves with famine in their respective provinces;4 hence I
cannot tell you anything about our little world except that it still exists. In any
case, we have not been jogging along any less than usual.  Our indigenous
doctrines, for example, acquire more consistency from day to day, march
towards their last phases with huge strides, and more than one serious question
unfolds in life mechanically in awaiting discussion.5 In fact, isn't life the best of
demonstrations among people so little trained as we are in logical conduct?
Discussion will not fail assuredly to come - by a wintry route. But, dear prince,
we really miss you, especially I do, since I like so much to follow with eye and
soul your youthful and lively spiritedness.
   Dear prince, accept my assurances of the most profound friendship from
your very devoted, Tchaadayeff.

"Two Letters to Princess Sophia M." Second Letter.
Letter to Princess S. Meshcherskaiia, Dec. (1841)1
     It is, without a doubt, very interesting, Princess, that there are these new
tendencies within the Anglican Church which the book of Reverend I. shows us.
We should bless the heaven which inspires the different Christian communities
to draw closer together in a mutual way. The courteous language, so different
from that utilized not long ago in similar matters, the moderation of the
accusations made against opposite faiths, finally the spirit of charity which
distinguishes this little volume, merits all our congenial feelings.
   Much can be hoped for, I believe, as a result of this new advance which
religious conviction in certain countries are making in our day, but I must admit
to you that r would have liked the discussions to have been established on
another plane.  I believe that the best way of appreciating a principle which has
reigned on! earth is toconsider-thefruit which it has brought forth; to reduce the
question to a purely theological one is, in my opinion, to limit it much too much.
Your Anglican minister heatedly attacks, for example, the cult of the Virgin and
that of the saints; but in admitting my position (what I am naturally far from
doing,) that this cult such as it was professed by our great mother churches, was

--- PDF page 183 / 323 ---

180                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS

in some way tarnished with superstition, the salutary influence which  it
exercized in the world must not be forgotten.  In a controversy among good
Christians, it's not enough that the spirit be in the truth, the heart must also be
there.  Didn't this cult render evangelical morality practicable in making flow
torrents of love from the mother of God onto the earth and in offering a certain
number of models for human weakness to imitate before being able to attain the
great model placed at the summit of the Christian ladder? Isn't it to this cult that
we owe what was most fertile in the Christianity ofthe Middle Ages?
   Take away the exalted adoration of the Virgin from this epoch of savage
grandeur and the profound veneration of the sacred halo, and today perhaps the
world would still be in the state in which it existed then.  In this age in which
brutal force was sovereign, do you believe that the simple morality of the BIble
and the superhuman virtues of the Savior alone would have been sufficient to
tame the ways of these written men whose iron nature came to be initiated into all
the depravations of Roman civilization degenerated into interminable saturnalia?
Wasn't it necessary to show them virtue at their own level and to teach them to
bow their foreheads, to bend their knees before these virtues?  Wasn't it
necessary to speak a language understandable to them and to speak more to their
heart, to their imagination, than to their intelligence.  It is certain, for example,
that Christian art, this beautiful flower of the purest religious feeling, would not
have been possible without the cult of the saints.  Very well, this art was
certainly more useful to the society than volumes of cold predication will ever
be.  Even more, I am convinced that, as for me, even at this moment the
admirable temples with which the fallen-away church populates England, as
silent as they are, preach the Bible there much better to her populations which are
too ungrateful, it must be said, to this magnificent heritage, than the preachers of
her present church.  I admit, moreover, that I have trouble understanding how
this church, whose very name Established Church indicates its origin, finds
itself to be this very same church which was founded there since Apostolic times
and destroyed after that by the Saxons.
   But whatever may be, from the moment that the Church of England
disavows its recent origin, and decides to date its existence only from the epoch
in which there was only one sole Church in the world, this Church, which
always exists, will, without a doubt, .be very happy to open her.arms ·to it. This
sort of abjuration of the impure source which it previously glorified has left, and
that is certainly a great step forward, and we should felicitate it from the bottom
of our heart. How, really, could the ancient religious communities, the sources
of the spread and the formulation of Christianity which has conquered the world,
not rejoice themselves to see their young sisters finally understand that there
could only be one sole Christian Church, and that, not some unknown
metaphysical church hovering in ideal spheres, but a very visible church very
definitely established by Jesus Christ on this earth, a church which He bathed in
His blood, which He sanctified by His sojourn among us?
     I perceive, Princess, that I have only begun to broach the matter and that I
am already at the bottom of my second page; I don't know if you're going to be

--- PDF page 184 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                                  181


satisfied with what I'm going to say to you, but I don't wish to close my letter
without trying to summarize my feelings on this interesting question in a few
words.   I believe then that since the mission of the Church throughout the
centuries was to civilize the world in a Christian way, it had to be constituted
with force and with power for that; since it had to make men understand that
there was only one sole way of knowing God and of adoring him, the church
naturally had to be dominated by the need of preserving its own unity.  If the
church had taken refuge in an exaggerated spiritualism or in a narrow asceticism,
if it had never left the sanctuary, it would have been struck down with sterility
and would never have come to accomplish its work; finally, since the church's
earthly destinies cannot be fulfilled except within the conditions of human reason
- conditions which imposed on it the obligation of always combining itself with
the spirit of the times which it had to pass through - the church should not be
reproached for having followed the route which was traced for it by the nature of
things, which was, as a consequence, the only route which would have been
possible for it to follow.
   One word more! You know that once during the strongest of feudal furors,
the church forbade any kind of hostility during four days of the week, and it was
obeyed:   but,  I ask you, do you believe that if the church had not been
constituted as it was, it would have ever considered proclaiming this famous
God's Truce, true code of peace and mercy, which, even in the opinion of
Protestant writers, contributed more than any other thing to develop all the
humane feelings among modern nations? No, assuredly.  In this general and
peaceful controversy which is thus going to be established in the religious
world, it seems to me that we should never lose sight of the services rendered to
humanity by the ancient beliefs, nor the necessity which they had to erect
themselves with social power and to submit all other powers to themselves.
Then, so full of the recognition for the good which the ancient beliefs have done,
the new beliefs stretching out their hands in charity, without a doubt, the Holy
Spirit himself will, I hope, deign to enlighten them and to discover for them a
whole world of love in which the most divergent opinions will soon come to be
plunged and fused. May this happy moment not delay to rejoice the hearts of
true Christians!  Especially mayan insolent philosophy which pretends to
accomplish a thing much to be feared in our day, namely, the reconciliation of all
the irreconcilable things through the aid of some barbaric formulae, not come to
pose itself as mediatrice between sincere and profound convictions, whose
nature.andmeaning this philosophy could neither understand nor measure and
which would cause all this holy work of religious minds to culminate in some
clumsy compromise, in some philosophic gossip unworthy of the religion of
Christ.

Letter to Ivan Gagarin, October 1 (1842)1

    After having reflected here, I believe that one must adopt the anecdote about
Princess Anna.2 The silence of our chronicles proves nothing, since they also
do not speak about the marriage of Iaroslav's other daughters, which have been
completely substantiated elsewhere.3 One can suppose that since the separation
of the two churches was more pronounced at the time of Nestor,4 it was too
repulsive a matter for him to cite facts which testify in favor of the primitive
unity of the two denominations. No matter what, it is clear that our religious
relations with the West were of a very favorable nature in that epoch-frequent
marriages with princes and princesses of Europe without there ever being a
question of the changing of religion, the support requested by Isiaslav from
Pope Gregory VII,5 and the popular favor which surrounded him upon death,
the history of the Roman Anthony who sailed straight from Rome to Novgorod
and established a monastery there,6 especially Metropolitan John's letter to the
pope7 and a number of our facts sufficiently prove it.
   Good day, dear prince, I will not be in your localities this evening; I am
going to Sesviatskoie.8 If you want to speak with me a little before departing,
come dine at the English Club. You will find me there around 5 o'clock and I
will register you.

All to you,
Tchaadayeff

Letter to Schelling, (1842)1
Sir,
    Since you did me the honor of writing to me,2 many things have taken place
in the philosophical world, the one thing which interested me most was your
appearance in the new theater to which a prince, a friend of genius, called you.3
As soon as I had learned of your arrival in Berlin, I wanted to address my good
wishes to you for the success of your teachings in the center of German science;
diverse circumstances, independent of my will hindered me from doing this;
today I have only to felicitate you on your triumph.  I don't presume to believe
that my compliments cOQld touch you infinitely, and if I had nothing else to say
to you, perhaps I would have abstained from writing to you, but I couldn't resist
the desire of informing you about our powerful interest in your present teaching,
as well as profound feelings with which the small group of our philosophic
minds greeted your entry into this new period of your glorious career.
   You undoubtedly don't ignore the fact that speculative philosphy has
penetrated into our country for a long time now; a great part of our youth, avid
for new enlightenment, hastened to commune with this completed wisdom,
whose varied formulations offer to the impatient neophyte the precious
advantage of dispensing with laborious meditation, whose lofty allures are so
pleasing to juvenile intellects. But what you probably don't know is that we are
at this moment in the midst of a kind of intellectual crisis destined to exert an
extraordinary influence on the future of our civilization; we are in the grips of a

--- PDF page 186 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                                183


national passionate, fanatic, studious reaction,4 a natural result of the exotic
tendencies under the empire in which we have lived too long, but which in any
case, in its narrow exclusiveness, tends towards nothing less than a radical
reconstruction of the idea of our country.  This idea was caused not by the
violent effect of some social cataclysm, which could in some way legitimize a
violent return towards the past, but very simply by the natural course of things,
by the infallible logic of the centuries, and especially by the very character of the
nation.  But, the philosophy which you have come to dethrone in Berlin, was
introduced among us, and was fused with the ideas around in our land, which
coupled with the spirit which dominates us at this hour, now threatens us with a
complete falsification of our national feeling, that is to say, this principle hidden
in the depths of each people's heart which forms the people's conscience, the
manner in which it understands itself and directs itself on the road which has
been assigned to the nation in the general ordering of the universe.
   The prodigious elasticity of this philosophy which lends itself to every kind
of possible application came to create among ·us the most bizarre images .about
our role in the world, about our future destinies; its fatal logic which almost
suppresses the free will, while constituting it in its fashion, which finds
inexorable necessity everywhere, in turning back towards our past, began to
reduce our entire history to a retrospective utopia, an arrogant apothesis of the
Russian people;  its sytem of universal reconciliation by a wholly new
chronological procedure, a piquant example of our philosophical capacities, led
us to believe that in encroaching on the advance of humanity, we had already
realized the philosophy's ambitious theories among us; finally the philosophy
was going to deprive us of the most beautiful heritage of our fathers, this
humility of mind, this temperance of thought which a religion strongly imprinted
with contemplation and with asceticism, and brought into being. Judge thep.
whether you were for all those of us who seriously cherish their country, a
welcome sight at the center of this philosophy whose influence could have
become so fatal for us. And don't think that I exaggerate this influence. There
are moments in the life of people in which every new doctrine, no matter what it
be, will always be invested with an extraordinary power by reason of the
extraordinary movement of the spirits which characterize these epochs. But, it
must admit the ardor with which men in our country flounder on the surface of
the  society in order  to rediscover some unknown  lost  nationality,  is
unbelievable.
   Men dig in all the corners of history in the country; they remake the history
of all people in the world; they impose on all the peoples of the world an origin
common with the privileged race, the Slavic race, according to their merit; more
or less, men shake up the whole crust of the globe in order to discover the titles
of God's new people there; and whereas the rebel nationality escapes all this vain
labor, men fabricate a new nationality from it, which men attempt to impose on
the country, perfectly indifferent, moreover, to the feverish transport of this raw,
young science. But the fevers are mounting, and if the doctrine of the immediate

--- PDF page 187 / 323 ---

184                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


manifestation of the absolute Spirit in humanity, in general, and in each of its
members in particular, should continue to reign in our wise metropolis, you may
see, I'm sure of it, our whole literary world soon converted to this system,
obsequious courtisan of human reason, charming flatterer of all its pretensions.
You know that, in the matters of philosophy we are still at the beginning; it's a
matter of concern then to know if we're going to abandon ourselves to an order
of ideas excessively provoking all types of personal infatuations, or if, faithful to
the route which we followed up to this day, we'll continue to follow in the ways
of this religious humility, and this modesty of spirit which were always the
distinctive trait of our national character, and lastly, the fertile principle of our
unique development.  Continue then, Sir, to triumph over a vain philosophy
which had pretenses of supplanting yours. You see the destinies of a great
nation depend in a certain way on the success of your system. Some day may
the fruits of true philosophy mature among -us -and may we owe it to you!

Letter to Madame Sverbeeva, July 10 (1842)1

   Your letter, dear Madame Sverbeeva,2 has come to distract me much from
my troublesome preoccupations. In approaching the moment which must exile
me from this city in which I passed fifteen years of my life in constructing a life
of affections and of good will, which neither persecution nor reverses were able
to break, and which all are now going to be broken, I perceive that this forced
emigration is a real catastrophe. You find Razhestven03 solitary; then what
would you say about the -charming asylum which is going to gather me in, if you
have any idea about it! Imagine nothing but purses and bags in one round place
and throw into the bargain no place in the house for even part of my books!
That is what my good aunt wrote to me, despite her ardent desire to see me at her
place.  Situated in an almost funny country with a comfortable habitation,
embellished by your being a neighbor, Razhestveno is a promised land in
comparison.  But let's not talk about that;  it would be very bad for me to
acknowledge your friendship by forcing you to endure the tableau of life which
awaits me. Moreover, I have still decided upon coming to take provisions for
happiness in your latitudes before retiring to my Sinaniary.4  I learned from
Cosina about the visit that the beautiful countess5 made to you; if I meet her, I
shall engage her to see you again towards the time of my trip. One evening
between two romances I will make my goodbyes:   that will sweeten the
bitterness for me.
    Let's get to the objects of your curiosity. The article of Khomiakov,6 they
say, caused very little of a sensation among agrarian proprietors; I am not
surprised at that.. It contains very little of a practical nature but an enormous
amount for thought.  As for me, I am delighted with  it.  I translated some
phrases for Marmier and added a little commentary for his particular use.
Unfortunately the interesting traveler only came to see me on the evening of his
departure; as a result I was not able at all to make him appreciate all the

--- PDF page 188 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                               185


significance of things given the short time that he spent at my place. Here is -one
of those phrases accompanied by its commentary: "The rural commune," says
the author, "subsequently found a chief in the person of the landed proprietor."
Nothing is truer, but what follows from that? The fact that the commune was
first without a chief, that this latter was established in our country in a legal
manner, and that its establishment was a benefit for the country, since it served
to organize this admirable commune in a definitive way, the base, according to
the author, for our social order and the germ for all our future prosperity.
Finally, one must not tamper with slavery, unless one might wish to disorganize
the commune and send us back to the golden century of antideluvian Russian.
The text says: "The village commune subsequently received its definite head in
the person of the landowner." You see that my paraphrase is perfectly correct.  I
call upon the author himself.  Another proposition of Khomiakov which I
confided to the discrete meditation of Marmier: 7 "The forms of our existence,"
he says, "organic product of the soil and of the national character, contain the
mystery of our greatness."8 Marvelous! But then is not serfdom, one of the
most salient forms of our existence, also a necessary product of the nature of the
country? Does it also not contain the mystery of our greatness? Once again then
don't dare to tamper with it!  Well, that's enough about Khomiakov and his
article. Madame Orlov9 is set up in Sesviatskoye, she is still courageous but
very sad.  The idea of leaving her and not seeing her again is one which
oppresses me the most.  I hope that this good Orlov will not bear a grudge
against me. One sees clearly, they say, in the land which he inhabits. Hence he
will know how dear his memory is to me and how much I would -have -been
happy to prove it to his family. Turgenev is en route for Moscow. His patron
should be here in a few days,lo So, you will not have long to wait before seeing
the dear man at your feet.  Natalie!! has had two -bits -of -news from -her -sister,
that wandering heart seeking -at this -hour emotions -on the picturesque shores of
the Rhine. May •heaven help her!  You probably know about the incident
involving the beautiful Dasha, a very natural incident given her figure. The
result is that Lisen travels without a personal maid, so she is doing marvelously,
she says, but could you tell me how she makes up her hair?  It is true that she
has the.resources to not do it at all.  There, I believe, I have satisfied all your
curiosity.
   So good day, my dear and living cousin.  I forgot to tell you that our friend
Golitsyp. marries a princess Dolgorukaia, daughter of Elise Dolgorukaia; 13 the
wedding will take place here in a few weeks.  It is John Gagarin who tells me all
that; he is just leaving my place and charges me to present his good wishes to
you. Good day once again. Please accept my profound and sincere devotion.

Pierre Tchaadayef
P.S.  I was going to post this letter, but your husband took charge of it.

    Here, my friend, is the next to the last issue of the Moscovite; as for the last

Shevyrev5 read at the university session and you will be able to amuse yourself

is all that I am able to tell you for the moment. Good day, my dear friend. Give

To T. (Alexander Turgenev)l (before 1843)

    Mr. xxx2 is sick, hence it is I who will respond to you; you know whether I
have the right to do so. And frrstly I must say to you that I regret very much
having provoked your letter.3 But what do you want?  Sometimes one is
condemned to obey one's first impulses, if only to be sorry for it afterwards.  It
is the language from a wounded man that you are going to hear, I warn you, in
any case from a man for whom neither your shabby whims nor your raging
angers have smothered his good feeling for you.
   Your soul is sick, you say! Heaven grant that your mind were in better
health! You would evaluate things and humans better.  That's a fact. You are
good, easy, without pretensions to profundity or to seriousness, an indefatigable
and sometimes interesting gleaner, an arm able charlatan, and-don't reject that title
but accept it with good naturedness.  Finally, a fault-finder by privilege and
philanthropy by posture: that is the way you are known and that is the way you
are 10ved.4 But look one fine day when your patron leaves the court, then you
would suffer the consequences.5 As the official support just left you, you
needed another.  Instead of requesting this -support from -longtime honorable
friendships, from services rendered and received, from the esteem of your
compatriots, you thought to find it in some unknown glorious antecedents, in
your liaisons with some complacent celebrities of Europe, in the imaginary
austerity of your principles and especially in this immense philanthropy for
which the convict has the premises and the world has the noise.6 Hence, there
you are among us in your new costume. Unfortunately, the clothes are not at all
made to fit your figure.   It was cut for a mind of a certain hardiness and
uncommon competency; you know how formed that one is which puts it on
one's back. From this flow all the perplexities into which you have fallen during
your stay in Moscow. First you found the public had become cold towards you.
It is not so at all. The public remained the same; it is you who have changed.
As for the public, one must really tell you, it never saw anything in you but a
joke. But this time you had wanted to be taken seriously. The public simply did

--- PDF page 190 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                              187

not wish to lend oneself to it. Then came your burlesque whims, your fantasy
misunderstandings, your jealous suspicions. You became peevish, brutal and
haughty.  All the evil passions, dormant for a half a century within a blessed
contentment, awakened suddenly, noisy, cynical and envious. Hence the need
for the public to turn its back on you a little.  In any case, having been
accustomed to treat you with indulgence, the public did not stop being favorable
to you:  only instead of sympathy the public had only complacency for you.
What I just told you, you know as well as I. But what you probably do not
know are other things which were so well instructed to you, and that is precisely
what you must know.
   You understand that it is not possible to cause the amiable person whom you
know to descend into the ridiculous arena where, thanks to you, we play
around.  I cannot speak to you about the most cruel teasing which you made him
experience, and all this at the moment when he was plunged in a supreme
sorrow,7 nor can I speak to you about your pretentions about having a monopoly
on his friendship, nor finally about the resignation with which he supported all
this overflow from an unheard-of egoism. But I can and I must speak to you
about me, for that then is something which you understand perfectly. Moreover,
no matter how ridiculous the debate between us may basically be, yet I could not
accord you impunity of discourse on my account which you grant yourself so
liberally. So to listen to you or better to see you, because you do not articulate
anything, one would say that you have some great grievance held against me.  I
am mistaken, grievance is not the word; your bad attitude towards me is
perfectly disinterested; it is a virtuous indignation, a noble modesty which alone
motivates you against the versatility of my opinion, against the incontrovertible
evidence of my own self-Iove.8 Very well, I admit, I am not at all one of those
who remain voluntarily moulded in one idea, thereby arranging history,
philosophy and religion to fit their theory.  I have many times changed my
opinion on a thousand things and I promise you to change them once again, each
time that I again recognize my error. As for the second point, concerning my
own self-love, yes, I am proud of having preserved all the independence of my
mind and of my character in the difficult position that was created for me, and I
dare to believe that my country will take that into account.  I am proud for not
having se~n any of the serious congenial feelings of those inclined towards me,
become re~essed amid the irritating debate raised by me. Finally I am proud to
count·amo~g my friends weighty and sincere minds of all opinions. One word
m~re.   .O~, undoubtedly Christian humility "is an admirable thing and in
practicing hone. experiences an ineffable happiness, but unfortunately in certain
given con4itio~s, Christian humility has the air of laziness and you who are so
much and sow~ll experienced in imaginary airs, you must know all that just as
well as I. lleturn to my opinions, it's the capital point.
   There was a time when, like so many others, I found the actual state of
things in my cQuntry to have been extremely bad, when, like so many others, I
thought that this great cataclysm which we call Peter the Great caused us to

--- PDF page 191 / 323 ---

188                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


revert instead of making us advance and that consequently we must retrace our
steps and begin our career again in order to attain some great results in the
intellectual order.9 Better instructed, I changed my mind. Today I no longer
think that Peter the Great committed violence on his country, that one fine day he
robbed it of its national principle and substituted western Europe for it, or that,
launched into space by this formidable arm, we wandered aimlessly like a star
plunged into a strange solar system and that at this hour we need some new
centripedal impetus or other in order to have us re-enter our natural sphere.
     Certainly, this man was a revolution and I am very far from denying it, but a
revolution brought about, as all those of the universe, by the nature of things.
Peter the Great was only the energetic expression of his country and of his
epoch.  Initiated into the march of humanity despite herself Russia, had for a
long time recognized the superiority of European countries especially in the case
