of armaments. Tired out from her old routines, bored with her isolationment,
Russia aspired only to enter into the grand family of Christian people. The
human idea had already penetrated into all her pores and fought there, not
without success, against the idea stained by the soiL  Finally, at the moment
when the great man called upon to reconstruct Russia came to sit on the throne,
Russia asked for nothing better than to be reconstructed. Thus, he had only to
add the weight of his strong will in order to make tip the scales in his favor.10
   As for the means which he served to realize his program, he found them
very naturally in the instincts, the forms of life, and in the very physiology, so to
say, of his nation of which he was at one and the same time the truest and most
marvelous symboL So his people did not disappoint him; if they protested, it
was in a profound silence and history never know anything about it.
  A militia, drunk on anarchy, great doers of nothing in their pillaging, driven
mad by their label of slave, some mutinous priests, some stupid sectarians are
not able to be witnesses of the national feeling.  Moreover, the type of
opposition which he encountered in a part of his people did not address itself at
all to his reforms, since they dated from the day of his ascent to the throne.  I am
too good a Russian, I have too high an opinion of my nation to believe that he
could have succeeded if he had found a serious resistance on the part of the
country.   I know well that certain initiates of the new national school, lost
children from this dear school which contradicts so adroitly the great historical
school of Europe,11 will tell you that Russia, yielding to the impulse imprinted
by Peter the Great, in effect abjured momentarily its nationality in order to find it
again later by some sort of process unknown to the rest of humanity. But one
instant of reflection will make you see that what you have there is only an
ambitious phrase, badly borrowed from this elastic philosophy which ravages
Germany today and which believes having explained everything when it had
drawn up a formula in its strange slang.12 The truth is that in the hands .of Peter
the Great, Russia abdicated her prejudices, her barbarous arrogance, some
remains of liberty which she cared little about and nothing else by the very
simple reason that a nation cannot in any possible case renounce itself totally and

--- PDF page 192 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                               189

even less so in order to give itself the singular pleasure of rebounding towards
its past one day with a new energy - a bizarre evolution that human reason could
not conceive of nor its nature accomplish.
    But, you must know that this was not at all the first time that the Russian
nation used this right of abdication which all people possess naturally, but which
 all people do not like to make usage of as frequently as we. Remark that this is
not a reproach that I address to my nation, certainly great enough, strong
enough, and powerful enough to be able with impunity to permit itself from time
to time certain whims of humility. This taste for abdication, firstly the fruit of a
certain complexion of mind particular to the Slavic race, exalted subsequently by
the ascetic character of our beliefs is a necessary fact, or, as people say today in
our country, an organic fact. One must accept it with good grace, just as the
country accepted the different foreign or national yokes which weighed upon it
one after the other. To deny an essential trait of the national character is to
render a very bad service to this nationality which we are today so preoccupied
with finding again.B
   So here are some of these abdications of which we speak:  First our history
opens with the singular spectacle of a foreign race invited in to the government
of the country by its very own citizens, a unique factor in the annals of the
world, even in the viewpoint of Karamzin, and which would be inexplicable if
all our history did not serve as commentary in a manner of speaking. Then
comes our conversion to Christianity. You know how it took place.  If the
prince and his grandees, the people said, find this doctrine good and wise, it
must be so and the people ran to jump into the waters of the Borysthen.14
    Finally arrives the long Tatar domination, this great thing which a false
patriotism hypocritically and obstinately refuses to understand and which
contains so formidable a lesson. IS As is known, the Mongols never occupied
Russia; but there is no real conquest, i.e., one winning a necessary submission
without occupation. One could say that a vague instinct was revealed to our
fathers that in isolating themselves from the rest of the world, they had sinned
before God and that the scourge of the Mongol invasion was only a just
punishment. Such was the resignation with which they endured this horrible
yoke.  So, however horrible it was, it caused more good than bad for us. Far
from breaking the national principle, the Mongol yoke made it develop and
mature even more.   It is the Mongol domination which shaped us for all
imaginable submissiveness; that is what rendered the famous reigns of Ivan m
and Ivan! IV possible, reigns in which our power was consolidated, when our
political ~ducation was achieved, when all the pious virtues of our ancestors
were manifested with such brilliance.  Finally it is the Tatar -yoke -which -als:o
rendered the ta,sk of Peter the Great so easy and which had more of a part in the
formation of this national colossus than one perhaps thinks. The reign ofIvan
IV itself can be considered in a way as a prolonged abdication during which the
nation deposed at the foot of her prince not only all her titles but even her beliefs,
since we see the nation near to recognizing as heir to the throne the fruit of a

--- PDF page 193 / 323 ---

190                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS

polygamy without example among Christian people and spill her most pure
blood for this imaginary symbol of royalty. 16
    This strange peripeteia of this strange regime is known, a real pact
concluded between the nation and her sovereign, in virtue of which the nation
delivered herself feet and feet linked to an insane master. 17 This fact alone says
to you much more than anything else I could say to you. But there is another
more important abdication in our history, an abdication more fruitful in results
perhaps than all those which I have just cited, and which I hasten to speak with
you about since it is directly attached to the reproaches which you addressed to
me. You guess that it is the establishment of serfdom in our countryside that is
the issue.
    Here is what you find in a journal well known for its national inspirations.
The article deals with the imperial decree creating a new method of liberation in
favor of cultivators,18  After having wisely established that the unity of the
commune is an essential element of our social system, the author adds: the rural
commune obtained subsequently a direct chief in the person of the lord.19 These
remarkable words, escaping from the elegant and patriotic pen of one of the
decades of the national school contains, in my opinion, all the past and all the
future of our agrarian population. So I need only make a few words of analysis
to make you understand and appreciate my point of view on this matter.
     First of all, what is striking in the great social pact which definitively
constituted the inferior levels of society in our country is that not only does one
not find anything similar among other Christian nations, but also that among
them things followed a march absolutely inverse to that which things followed in
our country.  Starting out from servitude, the cultivator in western Europe
arrived at liberty, starting out from liberty he arrived at servitude in our country.
There Christianity abolished slavery, the Christian era saw it born in our midst.
As for the very manner of the establishment of servitude there is nothing in
common between the way it was established in the countries of western Europe
and the way it was established in Russia. The result of invasion or of victory, it
was in a way made legitimate in those countries by the ancient right of war.
Everywhere over there where you would find masters and serfs you would also
find either one race superimposed upon another or else men reduced to slavery
on the field of battle.  In our country it is very simply one part of the nation
which subjugated the other to itself, and that happened without the subjugated
part ever being advised to demand the loss of its liberty back, without the
subjugated part of the nation ever feeling humiliated, blemished, or degraded the
least bit in the world by this change which came about in its destiny.20
    Here are the different phases of this singular history:  First came a simple
administrative measure fixing a time of the year for the renewal of lease between
the landed proprietor and the cultivator of the land.  Then came another
administrative measure attaching the cultivator to the soil. A third which
included him or registered him in a kind of crude land survey of the landed
proprietor. At last came a final administrative measure which confused the land

--- PDF page 194 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                                  191


cultivator with the domestic serf, or the slave properly speaking, and there
riveted his chains for all time. Such is the simple march of facts.  It is evident
that this march, in which the sovereign only intervenes as an administrative
power must necessarily be the effect of an order of things on the very nature of
the social milieu in which  it came about, or dependent upon the moral
conformation of the nation which experienced it, or else both at the same time.
Hence, in effect we see all these measures provoked by the impervious need of
the epochs in which they saw the light of day at the same time that we find in our
own very institutions, so profoundly imprinted with the national character, a
natural inclination towards this necessary denouement in our social drama.
Open the initial pages of our history, meditate upon them not with ambitious
patriotism, the braggart of our days, but with the modest religious patriotism of
our fathers, and you will see everything announced in the forms and in the
diverse combinations of our national existence since its infancy, this inevitable
development of the society. You will see that in its very cradle the nation already
bore in its heart the germ of all that today causes revulsion among superficially
intelligent persons, cast in the mold of a foreign world. You will see that since
those early days all already tend, all aspire to submit themselves to the yoke of
some personal power.  All is organized, all is arranged within the narrow
conditions of the domestic regime and finally all seek shelter under the paternal
authority of a direct chief. Amid all that, you will be able to note also an elective
element, weak, confused, powerless, being introduced sometimes somehow in
the family enclosure, but never encountering the positive idea of some law or
other and dominated everywhere by the sovereign element; the elective element
petrifies in the family.  Finally the elective element is such a nullity that our
history only seems to mention it in order to demonstrate its sterility, when it is
not at all combined with the feeling of human dignity. That is what the learned
author of the article understood perfectly.
    According to him, it is the rural commune which is the cornerstone of our
social edifice. In it reside all the energies of the country; in it is hidden the entire
mystery of our greatness.  According to him, it is a unique principle in the
world, it is something which only belongs to our nation alone, something
intimate, profound, singularly fruitful, something which made all our history,
which marks all the memorable incidents in our national existence and envelopes
it completely. Finally, it is from the bowels ofthe rural commune that the cry of
salvation came from when our beautiful fatherland was set on fire by its own
children and delivered over to foreigners.
     ;But, one must know that there was a time when this commune was very far
from being. so admirably constituted as we find it in an epoch more close to our
own.  It is tnie that the author acknowledges the operation of communal element
ever since the origin of our society, but it is evident that this operation could
never at all be e~.ercised in a very profitable way in our country so long as the
population moved about constantly on its immense surface.  This was caused
either by the very effect of the geographical nature of Russia, or else by the
empty spaces which .for.eign invasions created, or else .finally .by this taste for

--- PDF page 195 / 323 ---

192                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


migration so natural to the Russian people, a taste to which we owe in great part
the enormous extension of our empire.21 The learned author himself would
undoubtedly be very embarrassed if he had to make us understand the precise
form of his commune as well as its legal character, amid this tossing about of a
not very numerous population between 64 and 45 degrees of latitude, but
whatever the communes were like, moreover, it is certain that they needed to be
modified, that one had to put an end to the vagabond existence of the peasant.
Such was the motive behind the first administrative measure which tended to
prepare the peasant for a more stable social state. As is known, this measure
was due to Ivan IV, this prince so long misunderstood by our historians, but
whose memory was always dear to the Russian people. A narrow moral feeling
ridiculously branded him, but ihe broad morality of our day absolves him
completely. The above measure was due to this prince whose bloody axe did
not cease to hack away around him for forty years in the interest of the people.
Hence, it would not at all be reasonable to .see in it anything other than an act
dictated by sincere good feelings for the agrarian c1ass.22
   The second measure belongs to the reign of his son or rather to that of
Godunov, the person elected by the country.23

To Count Adolphe de Circourt, Jan. 15 (1845)1

     I just received, my dear sir, the issue of the Semeur containing the fragment
from the sermon of our archbishop.2 The journal was addressed directly to the
Monsignor who kept it up until this moment. That is why I have been so long in
answering you.  It would have been assuredly better had the indiscreet issue
come to me first and even better still had the sermon been printed in its entirety
and without the strange commentary of the editor.  Fortunately, His Eminence
did not pay much attention to it.  I just saw it; I received for it the most gracious
reception, the good words which pr the fragment will have put things in
equilibrium.
   You know very well that all our prisons are empowered with chapels and
that there is no more indulgent church on earth than the orthodox church.
Perhaps it is too much so.  It is in the nature of the religious principle to exalt
itself in that which it encloses most intimately and to hyperbolize, in a manner of
speaking, that which it contains most profoundly. But our church is essentially
ascetic just as yours is essentially social.  Hence the indifference of the one
towards everything which happens outside herself and the lively interest which
the other has in everything.  There are the two poles of the Christian sphere
turning on the axis of its absolute truth, of its real truth.  In practice one often
sees the two churches changing roles, but one must not judge principles based
upon isolated cases. To see a Reformationist in our holy prelate is enough to
make one burst out laughing. He himself laughed about it heartily.  The
journalist very simply took an oratorical movement, very well motivated as it
was, for a religious revolution.  I don't get tired of admiring your most serious

--- PDF page 196 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                               193


minds when they do us the honor of speaking about us. One could say that like
inhabitants of another planet, one can only observe us by the use of those optical
instruments which make one see objects in reverse.  It is true that it is a little our
own fault. The error into which you so often fall in our regard comes partially
from the small part that we have yet taken in the general movement of the ideas at
the heart of humanity. But the time is not far off when, I hope, we shall come to
take the place reserved for us among the civilizing people of the world. You
who come to see us with your own eyes, you must have learned that the
qualifications are not lacking in us to obtain that. If nonetheless despite that, you
 still may not know possibly what exactly are these qualifications. You have only
to become informed about them by this new school, the honor of Russia, whose
sublime enthusiasm and lofty significance you yourself can appreciate. And I
tell you that this new school could furnish you with an ample catalogue of these
qualifications. You are aware that I am becoming a little Slavic, as Madame de
Circourt would say. What do you want?  It is the way of escaping a contagious
disease which is all the more active in that it is a very new pathological
phenomenon in our climate. At the moment when I write to you, for example,
we have here a course in the literary history of Russia which stirs up all sorts of
passions at the same time as national ·dust.  The learned professor is truly
marvelous.3 You could not imagine all the admirable consequences which he
extracts from the very small number of literary monuments scattered on the
immense steps of our history, and all the buried powers which he discovers in
our past. Then, in the face of this noble past he casts an eye on the paltry past of
Catholic Europe and makes it feel ashamed with an unbelievable nerve and
disdain. And don't go imagining that this new doctrine does not find warm
congenial feelings among us.  It's been a stunning success. Remarkably adepts
and adversaries alike all applaud it; the latter even more vividly than the former,
charmed as they are apparently with what they also take to be the triumph of their
ridiculous ideas.  I don't doubt that the learned professor is going to come to
demonstrate with evidence the superiority of our civilization over yours, a thesis
which contains his entire program. What is certain is that more than one
recalcitrant mind is already reduced to silence by the ascending of his lucid,
warm, picturesque wording which is strong especially because of the support of
an enlightened patriotism, which recalls so well that of our fathers, strong
especially because of the well-assured favor it finds in the supreme spheres of
society, where the tendencies have been pronounced a thousand times with verve
on these. interesting questions. People say that he plans to publish his course.  I
will be happy to make it known to the learned of Europe in the universal idiom
of the c~vilized world. Transcribed into French he could not fail to produce.a
profound sensation in your latitudes, perhaps even to convert a good number of
people in this old Europe completely exhausted by its sterile routines. And, I am
certain, old Europe does not suspect at all that there exists at its doorway a
completely unknown world, rich in all the elements of progress which Europe

--- PDF page 197 / 323 ---

194                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


needs, containing solutions for all the problems which preoccupy her and which
she is unable to resolve.4
    So, nothing is more natural than this superiority of our civilization over that
in your countries.  After all, what is your society all about? The incoherent
product of a multitude of heterogeneous elements, the confused amalgamation of
all the civilizations of the world, the result of violence, conquest and usurpation.
Whereas for us, to the contrary, we are nothing else but the simple, logical result
of one sole supreme principle, the religious principle, the principle of love. The
only element foreign to Christianity which could have entered into .the
construction of our social edifice is the Slavic element, and you know how much
it is elastic and docile in nature. So, all the leaders of the literary movement,5
which is being accomplished at this moment in our midst, no matter what,
moreover, is their divergence on other questions, agree in toto on this point that
we are God's true people in modem times - a point of view which is not lacking,
if you will, in a certain perfume of the religion of Moses  , but which you will
nonetheless find to have a singular profundity, if you consider the magnificent
role which the church played in our history and this crowd of our ancestors
crowned by the church with the sacred halo. There is more, one of our most
remarkable minds (and by that trait you will easily recognize him) comes to
prove, with that powerful logic which is peculiar to him, that Christianity was
only possible in principle in our social milieu and that it could only expand
completely there, since we were the only people in the world conveniently
organized to receive Christianity in its purest form.  From this it follows, as you
see, that strictly speaking, Jesus Christ could have saved himself the trouble of
sending his apostles out to the entire world and that the apostle St. Andrew
perfectly sufficed for the task parcelled out among them.6  It is of course well
known that once revelation arrived at its complete development in this
atmosphere prepared for it, it would be pointless for it to continue its march in
order to attain itsuniversal·regenerationand you consequently can nourish the
hope of one day seeing it come among you up to a certain point. Perhaps people
find that it would be quite awkward to reconcile all that with the cosmic principle
of Christianity, professed with such obstinacy in the other hemisphere of the
Christian universe, but it is precisely this capital difference between the two
doctrines which gives us so many advantages over you.  Far from being
condemned as you are to a perpetual immobility, far from being encased in
dogma as you are, our beliefs, on the contrary, admit the most fortunate and
most varied applications of Christian principle and notably there are applications
to the national principle, an immense privilege for which you could only be very
envious of us. Our dear professor also told us the other day from the height of
his chair, in the accent of the most profound conviction and in the most sonorous
tone that we were the chosen vessel, destined to gather together and to preserve
the evangelical dogma in its purity in order to transmit it at the proper time to
nations less fortunately constituted than ours.

--- PDF page 198 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                                195


    This new itinerary of Christianity, an interesting discovery of our native
intelligence, will be admitted undoubtedly by all the Christian communities as
soon as it will become known, but meanwhile you must not be too surprised if
one fine day you learn that at the moment when you are plunged into the
darkness of the Middle Ages, we march in giant steps along the route of all
imaginable progress and that we possessed since time immemorial all the
benefits of modem civilization and most of the institutions which no longer exist
in western Europe, even at this very hour, except in the utopian state. There is
no need to tell you what was the fatal event which stopped us in the midst of this
glorious march across the centuries. You have heard it repeated a thousand
times during your stay in Moscow, but I cannot allow you to ignore my personal
opinion on this matter. Yes, it is the invasion of western ideas, of these ideas
repulsed by all our historical antecedents, by our national instincts which
paralyzed our powers, which falsified our beautiful tendencies which disfigured
all our virtues, which finally made us descend to your level or close to it. Hence
we must retrace our steps, we must return this past which we have so nastily
thrown off, we must reconstruct our past as best we can and stay there never to
leave again. Such is the work which today occupies all our good minds and to
which I associate myself from the bottom of my heart.  Its success is the object
of my wishes and that all the more so since I am convinced that the type of
rotation which we are experiencing at this hour can only be fully appreciated on
the day of its definitive triumph.  I do not know the opinion which you could
form for yourself concerning all that I just told you.  I trust that you will not
disdain my opinion, but it is always so that if you return to see us in some years
hence you will be able at your leisure to admire the fruits of our retrograde
development.
   At the moment when I was going to fold my letter I received your article in
Semeur.  I trust that His Eminence takes the advice of a good Christian. Your
observations seem somewhat severe to me, however just they basically are. We
shall speak about them when I shall have the opportunity of seeing what effect
they have on our venerable pastor.  I have not yet been able to procure the issue
of the Bibliotheque de Geneveof -which you spoke in your letter,but I -am
counting on reading it for you one of these days.
   Would you please remand me to the good graces of Madame de Circourt and
assure: her of my devotion.  I pride myself that she has not kept too odious a
memoi),of our Slavic fanaticism, in general, and of mine, in particular.  I also
have little taste for exclusive and narrow nationalities.  I even admit that I
modet;atelY esteem this geographical virtue which pagan antiquity made such a
noise abdut and which the Bible ignores. However here is a reflection that
Madame'Circourt will permit me to submit to her without, of course, any
correction: No doubt that Paris is today not the great nucleus for the social
movement of the world, its salons are not the privileged centers of the verbal
thought of the century, but it is nonetheless certain that it is to Paris that ideas
and minds still go to seek their enthronement, their glory and their title. But one
should not deny either that there are also, here and there, some little nuclei, some

--- PDF page 199 / 323 ---

196                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


obscure centers and that in these nuclei, in these centers, some poor ideas, some
poor intellects exist who lay claim, without too much presumption, if not to
profound interest than at least to some serious curiosity, especially on the part of
people whom a bad wind has sometimes stranded on our arid shores and who
could consequently appreciate the efforts which we make to clear up the
landscape.
    Accept, my dear sir, the expression of my devoted sentiments.

Letter to Princess Meshcherskaiia, Apr. 2 (1845)1 after the winter of 1843-44

   Do you pardon me, princess, this long delay?  I could not -doubt -it; your
letter itself is my guarantee. But I must tell you what impeded me from writing
to you.   It is something intimate which you understand perfectly, you who
possess the wisdom of the heart as much as that of the mind. So imagine how
one fine day I intended to ask if I had truly merited the human favors of which I
was the object throughout the course of my life? After a moment's reflection, I
naturally found that my life had all amounted to nothing.  And do you
understand what an honest mind must experience when it comes to recognize
that throughout its life on earth it had only lied! Such was the mental disposition
in which your letter found me.  But, I ask you, how to find the means of
replying to charming letter under the influence of such a despairing idea.  I was
literally obsessed with this nightmare for a month.  Finally, here I am delivered
thanks to a very simple procedure and one which I would be charmed to teach
you if I were less pressed to speak to you about other things.  Please believe,
princess, that your letter brought a moment of true happiness to me. A precious
episode in my life is based on the fact that you deigned to fill it with your
feelings of good will at once amiable and also serious. Even if I should never
see you again, it would still remain the most precious of my memories. But I do
not want to believe in such a sad future; I wish to live in the hope that these
soirees of which you speak to me in such a touching manner could return one
day. You ask me what person has replaced you in the habits of my life. No
one, I beg you to believe it, has had this temerity. Your place is empty in my
feelings as well as in the hours of my day. You were very correct in regretting
for me the departure of xxx,2 but you know that it is due to the friendship of
xxx3 that I owe mine, whereas it is to you alone that I owe yours. Hence permit
me to be a little angry with you for not having considered this capital difference.
xxx4 is assuredly an eminent person, but she is far from possessing this frank
and cordial benevolence with which you honor your friends.  She has the
misfortune of putting too much spirit in feeling; she calculates too much, which
results in her not calculating enough. The heart is not geometry; its calculations
are always false; it never calculates better than when it does not calculate at all.
All that does not impede her from meriting all imaginable affections from her
friends, but she must be content with a reasoned friendship, since she is unable
to accord any other herself. As for me she will always have my most sincere

--- PDF page 200 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                                197


and profound sympathy, if only because of the memory of this excellent man5
whom I found one day amid life like those soft winds which one sometimes
fmds in the country, which make you experience the moment better only after the
chill of the surrounding atmosphere. Perhaps you don't know that xxx6 is going
to Italy by way of Constantinople. xxx wants to keep her away from Moscow
as long as possible. Nothing is wiser in my opinion. For a long time already
the sojourn in Moscow could only be fatiguing for her. Her existence must be
newly arranged completely in order for her to find some charm again in this city,
so filled with her husband. Besides, family affairs go marvelously well thanks
to Mr. xxx the losing guardian and xxx the giving guardian.7
   xxx is very sensitive about his memory of you, despite the somewhat severe
manner in which you treat him. He has one of those good natures which are
always content provided that one occupies oneself with them a little, no matter
how. You want to know what he does.  Well, here it is:  the dear fellow is
falling into dust, in this kind of dust which only arid soils provide, one that the
slightest breeze carries out and transports in all senses. He fastens as best he can
upon his antecedents, real or supposed, and upon good souls from whom he still
filches favor. He always goes around as he used to, exploiting what he finds
within his reach but with a little less artfulness.  Finally he always practices a
little banal philanthropy here and there, a little worn-out liberalism which helps
him fill the emptiness of his existence for better or worse. And thus in this way
he descends headlong into oblivion. 8
    Princess xxx has just written to me. There are very beautiful things of the
world - and, certainly, I have not at all merited those things. Fortunately I have
my recipe against illusions of friendship.  Princess xxx has deigned to inherit
also a little of your feelings of good will towards me; at least that is my personal
interpretation of the gracious smile which she allows to flow to her feet when I
bow before her charming person.   I don't know if I shall see princess xxx
during her passage, but I shall do my best to prolong my sojourn in Moscow
until then. All the persons from your family become dearer to me day by day. If
I must retire from the world scene one day, it will be, believe me, with a very
lively interest that, at the base of my retreat, I will follow watchfully the
pilgrimage of each of them across life's laughing or somber fields. In regard to
retreat, I must say a word to you about myself: My new proprietor9 has decided,
I believe, to reconstruct the little pavilionlO that I occupy; hence I am forced
finally to take the initiative.  Probably during the first days of May I must
arrange for my definitive retreat, not without some admitted regret. Never has
Moscow been so good to live in; I would even say that Moscow has never been
so loveable to me!  Never have so many fruitful matters been dangled here
before serious minds! Good night to all that. Pardon if I present you with all
the details about my indiginous person but it was you who asked for it.
     I don't know whether you have been informed about the acquaintanceship
that I made on one of these days with an eminent personage from the Church?l1
I found him to be infinitely more interesting than I supposed. Unfortunately he

--- PDF page 201 / 323 ---

198                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


is so obstructed by an irksome world that one is hardly able to see him in his
natural state. Good day, princess. You see from the date of this letter that it was
written on the day after the Feast of xxx.  I passed the evening this day in the
home of prince xxx.  Will xxx graciously accept this homage of my sincere
affections?  I would like to know whether little Marie remembers me?  I hope
that prince xxx still wishes me well and that xxx does as well.
    Princess, please accept the assurance of my sincerest devotion.

To Count Adolphe de Circourt, April 26 (1846)1

     I thank you, sir, for your parcel and for your letter. The memory of our
dead friend2 is very dear to me, and your article responds perfectly to the wishes
which we formed to see him honored not only in our country but in those in
which he lived for so long. The editors of our journal will be charmed to give it
a place in our so vividly felt pages, which express so well our own feelings.
The exact knowledge of our country and the kindly tone which distinguishes
your article are rare things among foreign writers who have done us the honor of
speaking about us. But do you not find that the man whom we regret has grown
well since he is no longer in this world? He was not at all appreciated while
alive. One could say that one part of his person concealed the other part from
us. He was not taken seriously until he was taken away from us.  It is only at
this hour when the emptiness which he left among us leads us back so often
towards him that we begin to render him a late justice.  Surrounded by his
amiable gossip, by his bubbling agitation, we were not able to perceive the basic
seriousness which was hiding behind all that. However also an existence given
over to the practice of a thousand generous congenial feelings merited well that it
be considered from  all  its facets.  Undoubtedly today his qualities are
exaggerated, just as once his faults were exaggerated, but it is always so that at
this hour he has the end of his foot in history if not his complete foot.
   What you say to me concerning the state of France does not surprise me in
the least.  I am not a partisan of your current regime,3 but I admit to you that I
absolutely am ignorant of this new character which you attribute to your society.
What is this bastardly imperialism of which you speak?  Is this the ambitious
spirit of the imperial epoch or else its spirit of submission - that is what I am
unable to decipher.  Just as you, I think that the absolute abolishment of the
aristocratic element was a real calamity for France and that a country which
makes the claim of being a serious monarchy cannot dispense with that element.4
A royalty placed at the pinnacle of society without intermediaries between itself
and the nation is only logical in despotism; in the constitutional order of things it
is nonsensical.  This supreme grandeur must be tempered by an inferior
grandeur, otherwise it will only wound the regards of the multitude, only bruise
all vanities, all ambitions without being able to content them; it will excite all
passions, all the impatience of the people and of individuals without having the
means of calming them.  It seems to me that the role of the aristocracy in a

--- PDF page 202 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                                199


constitutional regime is first of all to turn the view of the inferior classes away
from the pomp of a monarchial power, which is always a little too lively, always
a little too dazzling for them.  I find that there is something singularly insolent
and cynical in this nudity of a royalty stripped of aristocracy.  That, in my
opinion, is what explains most of the things which happen to you without
excepting the assassination attempts which revolt humanity.
   You are always curious about the movement of ideas in our country. What
can I say to you about it? The national reactions still continues. If it sometimes
happens that it becomes a little too passionate over its own creations, to take on
airs of power, to make like a great .lady., _one should .not hold too much of _a
grudge against  it.   It is appropriate for  all reactions to be smitten with
themselves, to have too robust a faith in their truth, to fall into all kinds of
arrogance, and you know that contradic_tion _on this _terrain is just about
impossible in our country. The indigenous idea, Le., the idea exclusively such
as it is, triumphs because there is truth and good at the basis of this idea, because
the indigenous idea should naturally after this long submission to foreign ideas
from which we emerge.  Undoubtedly the day will come when  -a -new
combination of universal ideas with local ideas puts an end to its triumphs, but
until then it must experience its success as well as the abuse which it causes.
   Come to see us, Sir. You will judge things better when you will have
observed them with your own eyes. There are traits in the current movement
which I am not able to transmit to you and which characterize it perfectly. Don't
think that I wish to speak about a political tendency; nothing of the sort. But you
are aware that certain words in passing from hand to hand are sometimes subject
to changes of sense. For example, nothing is more discreet than these wishes
which we form in favor of a certain return to an imaginary past, but alongside
this imaginary past we have a real past, and it is certain that a number of things
from this real past may sound strangely in more than one _ear, if one day in.our
day one were to reproduce them in our public places. The task is purely literary,
but nothing is easier than to find in it, as one says in your country, a social
character, nay even an even more pronounced character.  Still, these are only
studies, but studies made in the midst of society -and which replenish a -part of its
existence, no matter how discolored they be. Another time perhaps we could
talk more at your leisure. Meanwhile please believe that I will always find a real
pleasure in satisfying as best I can the intelligent curiosity which we inspire in
you. Please accept the assurance of my perfect esteem.

To Adolphe de Circourt, June 15 (1846)1

     I just wrote to you, Sir, and I take up my pen again to beg you kindly to use
your influence in seeing to the publication of an article by my friend Khomiakov;
it has been translated by me and I wish to see it published in one of your
periodicals.2 You will receive the manuscript without delay from Mr.
Melgunov3 whom you know, I believe.  I do not need to tell you the pleasure

--- PDF page 203 / 323 ---

200                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


which I found in talking with you again. The article deals with the opinion of
foreigners about Russia. You know that I do not share the opinions of the
author. But you are going to see that I tried to render his idea with all possible
care. Perhaps I would have had mor.e .satisfaction in r.efuting it. But I think that
there was no better way to make your own public appreciate the literary products
of our soil other than handing them over the consummation of yours. The fact is
that, although at this very hour we are very disposed to confide ourselves to our
own judgment, the old habit of relating ourselves to those in your countries still
continues to prevail today among us. You are so well initiated into our intimate
life, you know our family secrets, so you will perfectly understand my thinking.
There is no progress possible in our country, even at the present time and despite
our many attempts at emancipation, except by calling upon the European
tribunal.  It is not that we do not basically in our innermost selves the germ of all
imaginable development, but it is no less true that the initiative for our movement
still belongs to foreign ideas just as the initiative has always belonged to them.
A singular phenomenon of social dynamics, for which there is perhaps no
example in the history of people! Note first of all that all our intellectuality is
only the product of the religious principle. But this principle does not belong to
anyone people in particular. Hence it is a foreign principle to us just as it is in
relationship to other people in the world.
   One must add to that the fact that Christianity underwent national or local
influences everywhere, whereas in our country  it remained just as it was
imparted from Byzantium, just as it was formulated one day by the force of
circumstances, an important fact, which our church correctly glories in, but
which also to no lesser extent characterizes an essential trait of our national
physiognomy.  It is under the inspiration of this unique principle that our society
developed. At the moment when Peter the Great came to impose his reform on
us, this development had attained its high point, but that was not a truly social
development,  it was an intimate fact, a thing from the internal force of
conscience and of the family which naturally had to fade away insofar as the
country grew politically.  So, that entire domestic regime applied to the
government of the state was already falling in ruins, when a strong arm launched
us on the career of universal progress.  I know very well that today people claim
to prove that Peter the Great encountered obstinate resistance in the national
sentiment, over which he triumphed, they say, only by spilling waves of blood:
Unfortunately history forgot to consign this superb struggle of the nation with its
prince to its annals.
    Moreover, what, I ask you, could have impeded the country from going
backwards after the death of Peter the Great to it ancient customs, to its ancient
institutions? So what could have stopped the national feeling from asserting
itself in all its energy, in all its truth, under the two regimes which followed that
of the reformer?  Certainly not Menshikov,4 who governed Russia under
Catherine I, nor the young Peter II, guided by the Dolgorukis, residing in the
ancient metropole of Russia, source and meeting place for all our national

--- PDF page 204 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                                  201


prejudices5 - they never could have dreamt of opposing a national reaction, if
such a fantasy had come to the country.  After the horrible episode of Biron6
came the reign of Elizabeth, well known for  its Russian tendencies,  its
sweetness and  its glory.7  It is useless to speak about this in the case of
Catherine II, so perfectly national that perhaps never does one witness a nation
identified with its government to the point which the Russian nation was during
those days of triumph and prosperity.8 So it is evident that the reform of Peter
the Great was accepted with good grace by us. The weak resistance which it met
in one fraction of society was only a whim of a party directed against the
reformer's personality and in no wayan opposition directed against the idea
which he wished to see triumph. Proof for this is the fact that this opposition
dates back to the first days of his regime, consequently back to a time much
before his innovations. This facility of submitting to impulses imprinted on us,
be it from outside or from our masters, to bow to ideas imposed upon us, is
hence an essential trait of our temperament, whether it's native or acquired is
irrelevant. One must neither blush nor deny it; one must try to explain it, and not
by means of some ethnographic theory or other so much a la mode in our day,
but rather one must explain it very simply by a frank and courageous conception
of our history.  I feel like telling you all my thinking about -this.  I will-not -be
long.
     I just observed that we are the product of the religious principle and that is
perfectly true, but that is not all. One must not forget that this principle is only
truly productive when it is independent of temporal power, when the place from
which it exercises its influence on people is situated in a region inaccessible to
the powers of this world. Such was the case in Egypt, in India, in all the eastern
countries and finally in Roman Europe.  Unfortunately, it was not so in our
country. Despite the profound respect which our princes had for the clergy and
for the dogmas of Christianity, spiritual-power was far from enjoying the
fullness of its rights in our country. One must go back to the very origin of the
constitution of our Church in order to take this fact into account. All the world
knows that the acceptance of Christianity by Constantine the Great as the state
religion was an immense political development. But people don't have an exact
idea formed at all of the influence which this event excited on the religion itself.
   No doubt the character which Constantine imprinted upon the Church would
not have been more fatal than favorable had Constantine not decided at the same
time to .transport the seat of government to the new Rome.  In this way the
Church was in a great part of the world freed from the tutelage of the temporal
power so that the church could march all alone towards its destiny. One must
recall that the Roman government was no longer during this epoch the republican
monarchy of the Caesars, but rather the oriental despotism inaugurated by
Diocletian and consolidated by Constantine. Hence the emperors soon became
masters of the spiritual as well as of the temporal. They considered themselves
to be like external and universal bishops.  They placed their thrones in the
sanctuaries. They presided over the church councils. They called themselves

--- PDF page 205 / 323 ---

202                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


apostolic, and finally as the historian Socrates9 informs us, they render
themselves masters of religious affairs and sovereignly took command of the
great councils.  St. AthanasiuslO reports that Constance said to the bishops
reunited around him, what I wish should be regarded as the law of the Church.
And you undoubtedly know that Theodosius the Greatll was greeted with the
title of pontifical sovereign at the council of Constantinople.  Such was the
march of the imperial power during the first centuries of the Church.
   But at the same time and in the face of these encroachments of the emperors
upon the spiritual we see the western Church, thanks to her remoteness from the
center of the empire, being constituted in a perfectly independent way, her
bishops extending their authority even over the temporal, the Roman patriarch
profiting from the prestige attached to the great name of Rome and to the blood
of the martyrs which was imbued in the soil of the eternal city. Due to the
heritage of the prince of the apostles, due to the memory of another great apostle,
due to the primacy which was generally bestowed upon it and especially due to
the need which Christian humanity felt for a center and for a symbol of unity, the
Roman patriarch lost little by little to this supreme grandeur which was destined
one day to struggle with the empire and triumph over it. This triumph, I know,
found little approval among your philosophical minds. The evil which resulted
from it for society was visible, the good escaped them. But as for us others,
witnesses disinterested in the cause, we can judge it better than you; faithful
continuation of Byzantium, we know what we are in for in the case of a Church
delivered over to the hands of the powers of this world.   I just named
Theodosius the Great.  Very well, as for this same Theodosius treated as
sovereign pontiff in Constantinople, you know how Saint Ambrose12 treated him
in Milan. Not content with forbidding him entry into the church, he even had the
imperial throne taken out of the sanctuary.
    Nothing, I find, describes better the character of the two Churches. On the
one hand, a clergy animated with a profound sense of independence, aspiring to
erect the empire of the mind and of thought beyond that of force and of earthly
interest, and, on the other hand, a Church Herself submitted to material power
and tending to elevate it into a kind of Christian Caliphate. There you have the
heritage which we received from Byzantium with the dogmatic integrity and the
primitive purity.  It is true that this purity is an immense-good, the preservation
of which we should consider as a favor from heaven accorded to our
unrecognized merits and one which should perfectly console us for that which
we otherwise lack in our spiritual regime. But at the same time one could not
deny the fact that the religious system of the West was much more favorable to
the social development of the people than that which fell to our lot.
    Don't forget that in our country there was nothing at all of any other moral
principle or other national principle besides the religious idea. Hence to this idea
alone the nation owes, in a way, all its historical and social education and that
everything must be attributed to it in our country, the good and the bad.

--- PDF page 206 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                               203


   To return to our subject,  it is evident that this docility at submitting to
different directions which are imprinted on us is partially the effect of a religious
system deprived of liberty, where moral thinking preserved only the appearance
of its dignity, where moral thinking was honored only on condition of remaining
quiet, where moral thinking exerted authority only insofar as it was accorded it
by the political power, and finally where moral thinking is unceasingly
compromised in its ministers, in its action and in its spirit.  I don't know if you
will share my opinion, but it seems to me that all our history is explained very
well by the sole religious factor which dominates it. See for yourself: A simple,
easy-going people, a people whose first steps were marked by this famous
abdication in favor of a foreign race, an abdication so naively recounted by our
annalists, this people, I say, embraces the sublime beliefs of the Bible in their
primitive expression such as they were conceived by the majority of the faithful
before the movement of Christian society had imprinted upon them the social
character, the germs of which were contained in the sublime beliefs, but which
were destined to become manifest only later and during a given epoch. Hence it
is clear that the moral idea of Christianity should produce on this people only its
most direct effect, to make it prefer the ascetic element to the exclusion of all
other principles which are contained there, principles of development, progress
and future.
   Emanated from supreme reason Christian dogma undoubtedly -could neither
develop itself or perfect itself, but it is susceptible to a host of applications,
provoked by the life of nations and by the diverse needs created by it.  All the
great phenomena, all the immense results which the life of western people,
combined with Christianity, have accomplished are known. The fact is-that this
life, itself filled with all kinds of fruitful elements, was not at all petrified by a
narrow spirituality. This life found protection, sympathy and liberty, whereas
life in our country only encountered a rigorous monasticism and submission to
the authority of the prince. Hence we marched from abdication to abdication.14
Our entire social evolution is only a series of facts of this nature. You know our
history too well for me to need to enumerate details.  Suffice it for me to point
out the immense fact of the gradual enserfment of our rural population, which
resumes, in a way, the entire spirit of our history, which forms  all the
philosophy, which is the rigorous and logical deduction of it.  Slavery had
everywhere the same origin, namely conquest; in our country nothing of the
sort, it is one part of the nation which one fine day found itself the slave of the
other by the very nature of things, by the effect of an imperial need of the
country, by the natural bent of society, without abuses from one part without
complaints from the other.
    Notice that this unique work was achieved during the most beautiful days of
our Church, during the epoch of its greatest power, during the period of the
patriarchate, a memorable period in which for an instant was seen a bishop
sharing the throne with the prince.15 Amid this social development, unique in
the history of the world, where all flows from the principle of subjection of the

--- PDF page 207 / 323 ---

204                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


individual and of thought, where all is absorbed in the idea of power, how can
you expect that the national intelligence could manage to shake off the yoke of a
civilization imposed by this power itself? Impossible. So the moment of our
emancipation is still far from us. All the work of the new school will serve for
nothing so long as we have not arrived at seeing things from our past in their
true light. Science is powerful in our day undoubtedly and the destiny of society
depends upon it in great part, but science can exert its influence on people only
on the condition of being as disinterested, as impersonal in the order of social
ideas as it is in the sphere of positive thought.  It is only then that its formulae
and its theories can come to explain the laws of society, to act upon society the
way theories act in the study of nature, to express its laws, to borrow from it its
power and to react upon society.  It don't doubt that the time will come when it
will be given to us to conceive of our past in such a way as to be able to extract
from it a thousand fertile results, a crowd of useful lessons, but until then we
must content ourselves with the simple appreciation of the facts, without being
too preoccupied with what they can contain of matter or of substance for the
edification of our future destiny.
   The epoch of our genuine emancipation from the influence of foreign ideas
will only date from the day when we perfectly understand the roads traveled by
us, when the confession of all the faults of our past life will escape from our lips
despite ourselves, when the cry of repentence and of sorrow will become heard
from the depths of our souls. Then we shall naturally take our place among
people destined to figure in humanity, not only as battering rams or as a club,
but also as ideas. Perhaps even that moment is not so far away from us as I first
thought, for at the very heart of this new school which claims to lead us back to
an imaginary past more than one lucid mind, more than one sincere soul have
already been led to admit to the sins of our fathers. The courageous study of our
history will necessarily lead us to unanticipated revelations which will shed a
new light upon our inner life and inundate our entire being with their light.
Nothing is more fortunate than the direction which minds have taken in our day.
A mass of considerable facts have been exhumed from oblivion, the most
interesting epochs of our history have undergone a complete reconstruction.
And at the moment when I write these lines more than one important work, they
say, is on the point of seeing the light of day.
   The opinion opposed to that of the national school, in its turn obliged to give
itself over to these serious studies on historical matters, though departing from a
different point of view, has come to results no less unexpected. One could not
deny that the intrepitude with which the two opinions accost the most interesting
questions of our history does honor to our times and allows one to predict a time
when our tongues more unbound and our minds more free will no longer be
enchained in the bowels of a hypocritical reticence. Comparison of our historical
life with that of other people, so often reproduced in our days, shows us each
day more of how we are different from them. So we will end by understanding
that if it is truly permissible for a people to separate themselves voluntarily from
the rest of the world, if one really forms a part of intelligent humanity when one

--- PDF page 208 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                              205

has furnished to humanity only a geographical, more or less vast map. If I have
contributed my part in putting into focus the two ideas which today preoccupy
our serious minds, I am happy for it, and you can see that I continue to speak
my mind frankly every time that I have the opportunity. At the moment when so
many expiring or reviving nationalities influence serious minds, the best way, I
think, of elucidating one's country is to show it to everyone, strangers and
native, such as the country appears to ourselves in our moments of sincerity.
Everyone is free to rectify our errors, if we are mistaken.
      I promised to be brief.  I don't know if I kept my word, but what I know
very well is that if I believed in the pleasure that I experience in talking over our
affairs with you then you would experience an interminable letter - I think you
don't doubt my perfect and sincere devotion.

Letter to Count Circourt, June 15 (1846)1

     I've just written to you, sir, but I take up my pen once again, to ask you to
consent giving your support to the printing of an article, translated by me, and
written by our friend Khomiakov, who would like to see it printed in one of
your periodicals.2 You'll receive the article at once from Mr. Melgunov,3 whom
I believe you know.  I don't have to tell you about the pleasure that I get in
talking with you again. The article deals with the opinion of foreigners about
Russia.  You know that I don't share the same opinions as the author.
However, you'll see that I tried to express his ideas with all the care imaginable.
Perhaps, I would have got more satisfaction in refuting it, but I thought that the
best way of making our literary products appreciated by our public was to turn
them over the the great literary consumption in the European world. No matter
how disposed we already are now to trust in our own judgement, the old habit of
relating our judgement to that of your public still prevails among us. You who
are so well acquainted with our inner life, you who knows our family secrets,
understands our thoughts perfectly. In my opinion, progress is still impossible
in our country, without appeal to the European tribunal.  It's not that we don't
possess the seeds of all possible development in our own mature foundations,
but it's nonetheless certain that the initiative for our development still belongs to
foreign ideas, and, I'll add, that it has belonged to them at all times: a curious,
dynamic development without examples in the history of mankind. You realize
that I am not only referring to a fairly recent epoch, but to our total advance
throughout the centuries.
     First, all our intellectual activity is evidently a fruit of the religious principle.
But, this principle doesn't belong to any people in particular:  It is, then, a
foreign principle to us, just as to other people in the world. But everywhere it
has undergone national or local influences, whereas in our country the Christian
idea remained just as it was imported from Byzantium, i.e., just as the idea was
formulated one day by the force of circumstances, it's an important fact which
our Church rightly boasts about, but which nonetheless does not specify the

--- PDF page 209 / 323 ---

206                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


particular nature of our national character.  It's under the influence of this unique
idea that our society developed. When Peter the Great came to impose his
reform upon us, this development had attained its zenith. But there was really
no social development in it, this was an intimate fact, a factor of the inner moral
consciousness, a family affair, which should have necessarily started to fade
away as the country grew politically. Moreover, this whole domestic regime,
applied to the State, fell in ruins when a strong arm pushed us along the road of
universal progress.  I realize that today men would like to convince us that
among his people Peter the Great found obstinate resistance, over which he
triumphed, they say, only by waves of blood.  Unfortunately, history hasn't
recorded this superb fight of the nation against its Prince. Moreover, nothing,
after the death of Peter, prevented the country from returning to these ancient
customs, and these ancient institutions. Who then could have prevented national
feeling from manifesting itself energetically under the two reigns which followed
that of the reformer? Certainly, neither Menshikov, who governed Russia under
Catherine I, nor young Peter II, directed by the Dolgorukis, residing in the
ancient metropolis of Russia, hearth and center of all our popular prejudices,
could have ever dreamed of opposing a national reaction, if such a fancy were to
have struck the nation.
    After the terrible episode of Biron comes the reign of Elizabeth, well known
for her Russian tendencies, her sweetness and her glory. There is no need to
speak about the reign of Catherine II, which was so perfectly national, that
perhaps never was a nation identified with its government as much as the
Russian nation was in its time of triumphs and prosperity.  It is evident that we
willingly accepted the reform of Peter the Great. The feeble resistance which he
encountered in one faction of the Russian people was only a caprice of one party
against his person and in no wayan earnest opposition against the idea which he
wanted to triumph. This facility at submitting to influences conveyed to us and
at bowing to ideas imposed on us, be they from the outside or from our·masters,
is thus an essential trait of our temperament, whether it's native or acquired,
makes no difference. We must not be ashamed of it, nor deny it, we must seek
to explain it to ourselves, and not by some kind of ethnographic theory which is
very fashionable today, but very simply by a frank and sincere .understanding.of
our history.  I would like to communicate to you all my thoughts on this matter.
I'll not be long.
     I've just made the observation that we are the product of the religious
principle, and that's certain, but it's not all. We mustn't forget that this principle
is fertile only whenitis perfectly independent of temporal power, ·onlywhenthe
place from which  it exercises its action on people is situated in a region
inaccessible to the secular powers. So it was in ancient Egypt, in the entire
Orient, especially in India, and finally in western Europe. Unfortunately it was
different in our country. Despite the profound veneration that our princes had
for the clergy and the dogmas of Christianity in our country, spiritual power was
far from enjoying the fullness of its natural rights in society. In order to account
for this fact, we must go back to the very origin of our Church's structure, that

--- PDF page 210 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                               207


 is, to Constantine the Great. Everyone knows that the acceptance of Christianity
 as the state religion by this prince was an immense political event, but it seems to
me that men do not generally have -an exact idea of the influence which this
acceptance exercised on religion itself. No doubt, the character imparted to the
church by this revolution would probably have been more fatal than favorable, if
Constantine had not luckily dared to transport the seat of government to the new
Rome, which delivered the ancient Rome from the troublesome presence of the
 sovereign.  At this epoch, Roman government was no longer the republican
monarchy of the first Caesars, it was the oriental despotism inaugurated by
Diocletian and consolidated by Constantine.
    The emperors thus soon became the sovereign masters of the spiritual as
well as the temporal. They considered themselves as exterior and universal
bishops; they placed their thrones in the sanctuary; they presided over the
assemblies of the church; they called themselves apostolic; and finally, as the
historian Socrates informs us, they rendered themselves the masters of religious
affairs and had sovereign command over the greatest councils. Saint Athanasius
 tells us that Constance said to the bishops reunited _around him: what -' will,
must be regarded as the law of the church, and you know, without a doubt, that
Theodosius the Great was hailed with the title of sovereign pontiff at the council
of Constantinople.  This was the march of imperial power during the first
centuries of the church.  But at the same time and in the face of these
encroachments is the emperors over the spiritual order, the western church,
Roman church, thanks to its distance from the seat of the empire, is seen
forming itself in an independent manner, its bishops extend their authority even
over the temporal. Profiting from the prestige attached to.its great name, by the
blood of martyrs with which the soil of the eternal city was impregnated, by the
heritage of the prince of the apostles, by the memory of another great apostle,
and especially by the need which Christian humanity experienced for a center
and for a symbol of unity, the Roman patriarch is seen rising, little by little, to
this supreme position of power, which one day was destined to come in conflict
with the empire and to triumph over it. This triumph, I know, was approved by
few of your philosophic minds, but we others, disinterested witnesses in the
case, can appreciate it better than you; faithful disciples of Byzantium, we know
better what it means for a spiritual power to be delivered over to the hands of
secular power.
  A moment before I referred to Theodosius the Great. Well, you know how
this same Theodosius, whom they treated as sovereign pontiff at Constantinople,
was harshly treated by St. Ambrose at Milan, and it must be added that the latter,
not content with forbidding him entry into the church, also had the imperial
throne taken out of the sanctuary. Nothing better describes, I find, the character
of the two churches. On the one hand, animated with a profound sense of
independence, a clergy aspiring to erect the spiritual power over that of force; on
the other hand, subjected to the physical power, a church tending to become a
kind of Christian Caliphat. There then is the heritage which we received from
Byzantium, along with the integrity of dogma and its primitive purity.  This

--- PDF page 211 / 323 ---

208                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS

purity, without a doubt, is an immense good, and it should console us for what
is moreover lacking in our spiritual system, but since here our only concern is
our social development, you'll agree that the western religious system was very
much more favorable to a development of this nature! than what fell to us as our
lot. One thing must always be remembered, and that is that there was in our
society no moral principle other than the religious idea:  it's then to the religious
idea alone that our nation owes its historic education, everything in our land
ought to be attributed to it, the good as well as the bad. But to return to our
subject, it's evident that this docility in submitting ourselves to the different
movements imposed onus, is a necessary consequence of a religious system
devoid of liberty, in which moral thought has preserved only the appearance of
dignity, and is honored only on condition that it is maintained tranquUly; in
which a system moral thought exercised only as much authority as it was
accorded by political power, and finally in which the ministers of moral thought
are unceasingly compromised in movement and in spirit.
     I don't know if you'll agree with me, but it seems to me that all our history
can be explained very effectively in this way. A good,natured,andsimple"souled
people, whose first steps on the social scene were marked by this famous
abdication in favor of a foreign people, so naively related by our annalists - this
people, I say, embraced the sublime teachings of the Bible in their original form,
i.e., before the movement of Christian society had on them imparted social
character, now the germ of social character was there, but it would and could
only manifest itself at a given epoch.   It's clear that the moral idea of
Christendom had to have only its most immediate effect on this people, by
making the ascetic element prevail there, to the exclusion of all other principles
which are contained in Christendom principles of development, progress and
future. The Christian dogma, emanated from supreme reason, can neither be
developed nor perfected, but it's susceptible to many applications .based .on the
life of nations. Men know that all the great phenomena, all the immense results
which the life of western people engendered, were combined with Christendom.
But that's because this life, itself filled with all kinds of fertile elements, was not
petrified by a narrow spiritualism,  it's because this life found protection,
sympathy, liberty there, where life in our land met only monastic rigor and
subjection to the interests of the prince. We proceeded then from abdication to
abdication. All our social evolution is but a series of such typical facts.
   You know our history well, without my having to enumerate it for you:  it's
enough for me to signal out the immense fact of the gradual enslavement of our
rural population, which in some way summarized the whole spirit of our history
(which is only a rigorous and logical result of our history). Everywhere slavery
had the very same origin: conquest. Not so in our country. One fine day one
part of the nation found itself the slave of the other, by the very nature of things,
by the effect of an imperious need in the country, by the inevitable march of
society, without abuse, on the one hand, without protest, on the other. Note that
this iniquitous work was completed during the epoch of the church's greatest
power, during the period of Patriarchate, a memorable period in which for a

--- PDF page 212 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                              209


moment men saw the chief of the church share the throne with the prince. Amid
this social development, unique in the history of the world, in which everything
tends in principle towards the subjection of the individual and thought, how
could you expect that spirit could come to throw off the yoke of your
civilization, your enlightenment, your authority? Impossible. The moment of
our emancipation is then still far away from us. All the work of the new school
will accomplish nothing, so long as our retrospective point of view is not
completely modified. Science is powerful in our day, -without a doubt; the
destinies of society are dependent in great part on sCience, but science cannot
exercise its action on the people, except on the condition of its being just as
impartial, just as impersonal in the order of social ideas, as it is in the sphere of
pure thought.  It is only thus that the formulae, and theories of science can come
to an explanation of social laws and act on society, just as science attains an
explanation of its laws in the study of nature and reacts on nature.
    Doubtless, time will come when we will be given the possibility of
understanding our past in a way so as to be able to draw fertile results from it for
our future, but until that time we must be content with the simple appreciation of
the facts, without preoccupying ourselves too much with their meaning or
substance for the edification of our future destinies. The epoch of our genuine
deliverance from the influence of foreign ideas will come only on the day when
we perfectly understand the roads which we traveled over, when the confession
of all our past errors and faults will escape from our lips despite ourselves, when
from the depths of our nature will be heard the cry of repentence and sorrow,
whose resonance will fill the earth. Then we will naturally take our place among
the peoples destined to act among humanity not only as battering ram or club,
but also as ide!j.s. And don't go believing that we have long to wait for this
moment. At the very bosom of this new school which claims to re-establish the
past, more than one lucid spirit, more than one sincere soul has already been
guided to admit the sins of our fathers. The courageous study of our history will
lead us necessarily to unexpected revelations, which will throw a new light on
our earlier life, people will finally learn to know not what we possessed, but
what we lacked; not what must be re-established from the past, but what must be
done away with.
    There's nothing more fortunate than the movement which our minds have
taken in our day. A considerable mass of facts has been exhumed from oblivion;
the most interesting epochs of our history have undergone a complete
reconstruction; and at the moment in which I write to you, an important work is
on the point of seeing light of day.  The school of thought opposed to the
national school, must also devote itself to earnest studies on historical matters,
though h starts from a different point of view,  it arrives at nonetheless
unforeseen results.  It could not be denied that the courage with which the two
schools of thought approach these matters does honor to our times, and allows
us to look forward to those times in which our tongues will be less tied and our
intellects freer, so that we will no longer be enchained by the fetters of eternal
and hypocritical reticence. The comparison of our historic life with that of other

--- PDF page 213 / 323 ---

210                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS

people, so often represented in our days, shows us step by step how much we
differ from other people.  Later, we'll know if it's permissible for a people to
separate themselves thus from the rest of the world, if men really form a part of
historic humanity, when they have nothing more to furnish historic humanity
than a few pages of geography. For my part, if I have contributed in bringing
face-to-face the two ideas which divide our earnest minds today, then I'm happy
about it, and you can see that I always continue to express my thought on my
country frankly. At the moment in which expiring and renascent nationalities
preoccupy so many intellectuals, it seems to me that the best way of enlightening
one's country concerning its own nationality is to show it to everyone, foreigner
and native, such as our nationality appears to us ourselves. Everyone is free to
rectify our errors, if we have been mistaken.
     I promised you to be brief.  I don't know if I have kept my word, but what I
know surely is that if I hardly believed in the pleasure that I find in conversing
with you about our affairs, you would undergo an interminable letter.

To George Samarin, Jan. 29 (1847)1

   Your letter, dear Mr. Samarin, touched me deeply.  I tell you truly, you have
a heart as good as you mind.  Perhaps some people will find the praise
exaggerated, but these will unquestionably not be those who wish you the most
good. Man is made all of one piece more than one thinks.  So, from the very
first days when I learned to know you, my opinion was formed in your regard
and it is the nobility of your feelings as much as the distinction of your mind
which attracted me to you. There are, moreover, so many things which are only
perceived by the heart's eye, that one could never appreciate perfectly the
measure of our intelligence if one does not consider one complete being at the
same time.
     I am delighted to have found the opportunity of expressing to you what I
think about you and it is sweet to imagine that I perhaps have contributed in my
humble way to the development of the best powers of your nature. Dear Mr.
Samarin, please accept this heritage from a man whose feelings, I dare say, were
not always sterile.  If my tired existence should soon become terminated,
nothing will sweeten it as much the last days of my life ·as the ·memory ·of
affections accorded me by some young hearts, warmed in return for the warmth
which I felt for them.  I count you among those hearts.  I infinitely regret that
upon your departure you found me in one of my worst moments and I trust that
this impression does .not leave any traces upon your prosperous existenc.e. By.a
singular arrangement of life it happened that I found myself in contradiction with
things and men surrounding me almost upon leaving ·childhoodhehind. My
nervous constitution naturally was bound to be felt by me and at the age at which
I am, nothing remains for me to do about it other than accept with resignation the
destiny fallen to me. Fortunately human life does not·endon the day ·ofdeath, it
begins again the next day. Whether this moment is near or not for me, let me

--- PDF page 214 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                                 211


nourish the hope that you will not preserve any less until then the feelings which
you just expressed to me. Though we were not always of the same opinion on
many things, perhaps we shall find one day that the divergence army opinions
was not as profound as we thought it to be.  I love my country in my way, that's
 all. More than I could tell you, it cost me to pass for an unfaithful Russian.
Enough of sacrifices. Now that my task if fulfilled and my chalice is emptied, as
 I have said almost all that I had to say, nothing any longer hinders me from
giving myself over with reservation to this innate inclination for my fatherland
that I have repressed too long in my breast. The truth is that like so many others
before me and greater than I, I thought that in finding ourselves in the presence
of an immense civilization, product of all previous centuries and of a thousand
efforts of humanity, we had nothing better to do than to seek to appropriate to
ourselves this civilization in all imaginable ways.  I thought that in the situation
