which was created for us by the very force of things we could no longer
continue our old history step by step, won over as we irrevocably were by this
new history of humankind which precipitated  it towards  all kinds of
denouements. Perhaps it was a mistake, but a very natural mistake, you must
agree. No matter what, some new studies and some new pieces of research have
made known to us a multitude of things unknown before, and it has become
evident at this hour that we resemble the rest of the world too little to be able to
attain some serious result in taking ·acommon route with .the rest ·of the world.
Hence if we have in effect deviated from our natural march, one must above all
try to rediscover it in every way possible, if it can be done, that is certain. But
once this retrogression has been done, this work accomplished, then what must
one do then that is the question. Meanwhile let us all work such as we are in
good faith, each in his own way towards this interesting research. To do that
there is, I find, no need at all to abjure our convictions; it is enough to recognize
the historical importance of our ancestors and from that to arrive at a lucid and
sincere appreciation of our present state. Whether one approves of the march
which we followed in days of yore or not, it is always so that we must one day
return to a certain point, for it is clear that our derailment did not at all succeed
for us in all things.  After all, it is the way to stay cowering on one's opinions
amid this frightening steeplechase which carries us towards some great unknown
future!  Aren't all people extending their hands out today?  Very well, may
opinions do the same without being confounded, without becoming debased, but
to extend themselves and to effect a rapprochement on the neutral terrain of love
and of charity, just as the nationalities do, once enemies towards one another,
today pacific rivals in research of the greatest welfare possible in this world.
Isn't the epoch of the railroad also that of all kinds of rapprochements? At the
moment when time and space are going to be abolished, when all the enclosures
of the world are going to be torn down, how can you expect -that ideas would ·not
also change a little in appearance and allure?  I will be delighted to speak with
you again about all that, but I must warn you that your friends wish that you use
the honored idiom of the West. As for me, as you know, I am not one of those
who considers the usage of a language a .privilegedorgan of .civilization, as a

--- PDF page 215 / 323 ---

212                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS

crime against our own nationality provided that one is understood, which is not
always the case even in the language ofthe country, no matter.
   Here is the new volume of Gogol2 which falls to us from the skies. You
could figure out for yourself the emotion which it generated among us. The
strange phenomenon which this extraordinary man offers us is certainly very full
of instructions, but who knows if we will be able to profit from it! An eminent
writer, while being the expression of his epoch, outdoes it always a little; this
personal infatuation of talent, enveloped in religion, is perfectly explicable in my
opinion by the general phenomenon of the times in which we live, a singular mix
of restless religiosity and of all sorts of pride.  It is no less very curious to
observe the influence which general tendencies exert upon the minds of the elite.
Also I am far from sharing the public rancor against our little great man whose
principal crime is basically only to have offended some people filled with self-
love who believe themselves to be better sheltered under their tent than they
really are. So where, I ask you, has one ever seen a poet who was a profound
thinker? Viazemskii writes to me that he is going to compose an article on the
new book.3  I think that it will.be like my opinion. lam impatient to .know
yours.
   Good day, dear Mr. Samarin. You are greeted from all parts and in all ways
without even speaking about me who greets you in the most cordial way in the

Letter to P. A. Viazemskii, April 29 (1847)1

   Thank you, my dear Prince, for your kind letter. We shall try to arrange the
affair of K.2 ourselves; but we are nevertheless grateful to you for your interest
in this matter.  I find myself in complete disagreement with your opinion of our
life here, although you are right about many things. That we are intelligent there
can be no doubt; but that our intelligence is quite useless, with that I cannot
agree. Must one be practical in order to practice something? Of course, it's
possible to do both; but -thi·s ·does ·not mean ·that ·an -idea which is yet to find
expression in reality could not be something very sensible. The time will come,
and it will manifest itself in that sphere as well. Do people inhabit only
departments and offices? You will say that our ideas not only don't manifest
themselves in reality but are not even put down on paper. But what are we to
do? Who knows, we may be bad at reading and writing. But if you could only
hear our discussions! There is not a single contemporary or non-contemporary
question that we have not resolved, and all to the honor and glory of Holy
Russia. Believe me, our discussions contain much sense. The world will throw
up its hands when all this will be seen in the light of day. But let us talk about a
subject that interests both of us.
     I hear that among you Gogol's book3 has been greeted with much pleasure;
among us, on the contrary, it has provoked great displeasure.  I believe this is
due to our being more partial to the author. He somewhat deceived us, that's

--- PDF page 216 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                               213


why we are angry with him. As for me, I believe that what is most interesting
here is not Gogol himself, but rather that which has made him the figure we see
before us now.  In our arrogant age which pompous with popular pride, how
can a talented writer surrounded by clouds of incense not give himself airs, how
can his head not spin?   It's simply impossible. We are now so pleased with
everything that is native and particular to us, we are so happy with our past, so
content with our present, we boast so much of our future that the feeling of
universal self-satisfaction is unwittingly applied to the individuals among us. If
the Russian people is superior to all the other peoples in the world, then it
follows that every talented Russian is superior to all the talented individuals
among other peoples. Among those peoples for whom national self-conceit is an
ancient tradition, where, so to speak, it has willy-nilly emerged from historical
events, where it is in the blood, where it is something trivial, there, by virtue of
this fact, it belongs to the multitude and can have no effect on an elevated mind,
among us, on the -other -hand, this weakness has -suddenly manifested itself
contrary to our whole existence, all our centuries-old ideas and traditions, so that
it took everyone by surprise, the intelligent and the unintelligent: are we then to
wonder that even men who have been endowed with extraordinary gifts are
intoxicated by it! We need only look -around ourselves .to see at once that .this
national conceit, which previously was alien to us, has suddenly perverted our
best miIlds; how great the ecstasy of self-satisfaction in which they have been
drowning since they performed their imaginary feat, since they discovered their
new worlp of spirit and intelligence!  It seems that the precepts of wise antiquity
were not. carved deeply in our souls; was it so long ago that, obeying the
arbitrary will of a-great man, we went against them in front of the entire world,
and now we contravene them again, obeying some sort of national feeling which
we have received God knows from where!
   The flaws of Gogol's book belong not to him but to those who madly praise
him, who worship him as the supreme manifestation of the original Russian
mind, who await from him some sort of transformation of Russian letters, who
ascribe to him a significance almost on a world scale, who, finally, have foisted
on him an arrogant patriotism foreign to him with which they themselves are
infected, thus confronting him with an insoluble problem, the impossible
problem of reconciling good and evil: whereas its merits belong to him. His
humility, to the extent that it may be found in the book, is the fruit of the
author's i new outlook; the pride that he displays was inculcated in him by his
friends.:  i He says this himself in his letter to Prince Lvov which he wrote in
connec~ion with this book.4 Of course he was not born totally without pride, but
neverthe~e~s the main damage was done by his admirers.  I refer in particular to
his adm~rers in Moscow.  And do you know where Moscow's habit of
unquestidningly worshipping talented writers comes from?  It is due to the desire
we feel for a writer whom we could rank with all the giants of the human spirit,
with Homer, Dante, Shakespeare and whom we could place above all the _other
writers of the past and present. This may be strange, but it's true.  I know these

--- PDF page 217 / 323 ---

214                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


admirers well, I love and respect them, they are intelligent and worthy people;
but they feel the overwhelming need to raise our modest, pious Russia above all
the peoples in the world, they have decided at all costs to convince themselves
and everyone else that we are destined to be in some sense the teachers of all the
peoples. They happened to come across a little teacher of this kind, and they
repeated this to him in a variety of voices, speaking aloud and whispering in his
ear; and he, as an ingenuous, trusting poet, believed them. Fortunately for him
and for Russian literature there lay hidden within him, as I have said above, the
germ of that very pride which their laudations were meant to stimulate in him.
He was satiated by their praises; but he did not feel the least respect for these
people. This may be seen in his book and is reflected in his conversations in
every word. Owing to this there appeared in him an anxious feeling towards
himself which was at first reinforced by his morbid disposition and then by the
new outlook he had adopted, perhaps as a means of escaping the melancholy that
afflicted him and the painful, unrealisable task he had been set by the whims of
the modem world. There can be no doubt that if these whims had not distracted
him, if he had continued to follow his own path, he would have achieved
wonderful things; but now God knows where his friends will lead him and how
he will endure the burden of their arrogant expectations, irrational suggestions
and immoderate praise!
   Some among us in Moscow, by the way, imagine that he owes his new
outlook to the so-called West, the land where he is currently residing, and to the
Jesuits. Our un straightforward friend5 dwells on this felicitous thought in the
'Moscow Gazette' and in his next letter he will probably develop it further with
his customary ingenuity. But Jesuitism as it is understood by these gentlemen
has existed in the heart of man since the dawn of humanity; we need not look for
it abroad; we can find it around us and even in those very people who reproach
poor Gogol with it.  It consists of the practice of employing every possible
means to achieve one's goal, which is something that is common everywhere.
To do that it is unnecessary to be not only a Jesuit, but even to believe in God;
one only needs to become persuaded that one must become known as a good
Christian or an honest man or something else of the kind. There is nothing like
this in Gogol.  He is too conceited, too disinterested, too candid, candid
sometimes to the extent of being cynical, ina word, -he is too artless to be -a
Jesuit. Some of his critics are particularly distinguished by their artfulness, their
ability to profit from everything that falls into their hands, and in this sense they
are truly jesuitical. He is nothing else but a talented writer who has been praised
to excess, who has turned in a new direction and does not know how to come to
terms with it. But he is still the same man we knew before and even in that
morbid state of body and soul in which he currently finds himself he is still a
hundred times greater than all his critics - and when he will be moved to do so,
he will crush them with one word and scatter them like useless weeds.
    These lines were written before I received your brochure;6 since then I have
been ill and unable to write.  I thank you for sending it.  I shall not change my

--- PDF page 218 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                               215


letter; I will only tell you in a couple of words as well as I can my opinion of
your article. Y ouprobably know that it has been greeted here with tremendous
anger.  I of course don't share this anger.  I am certain that if you forbore to
demonstrate all the flaws in the book, this was because they did not concern
you, for they had been pointed out my many others.  It seems to me that above
all you wished to demonstrate its importance from the point of view of morality
and of the inevitability of the revolution that took place in the ideas of the author,
and this, in my opinion, you did admirably. Whatever may now be said about
your article, it will remain in the memory of reading and thinking people as the
most honest word said about that book. Everything that had been said about it
by others is filled with a strange anger against the author.  It's as if he cannot be
forgiven because, after having entertained us for so long with his intelligent wit
he had taken it into his head to speak to us without laughing, because he had
experienced what every day happens in ordinary life to people who are not as
famous and because he dared to inform us about this as is the age-old want of
writers who entertain the notion of their own importance. They forget that a
writer, and one who is so well-known, is not a private individual, and it would
be impossible and incorrect for him to conceal his new, intimate feelings; that he
belongs to the people to whom he has dedicated the gift he had received from
above not only with his writings, but with his very soul; they forget that despite
some poor and even sinful pages, his books contain pages of amazing beauty,
full of infinite truth, such pages· that, when reading them, one feels proud that
one speaks the language in which these things are said. Only you have shown
love towards the book and its author:  I thank you! Day after day the source of
love among us is running more and more dry, at least in the world of bookme:n:
and so, I thank you once again!  I am overcome by an ineffable sadness when I
see all this spite directed at a beloved writer who has given us so much tearful
happiness, a spite felt because he has ceased entertaining us and, with a feeling
of sorrow and conviction, is making a confession before us and is attempting, as
far as this is in his power, to tell us something instructive and good.
   Much of what I wanted to say on this subject you have already said, and
incomparably better than I should have been able to say, particularly in a
langl1age of which I have such an indifferent command; but it seems to me that
you lost sight of one point, to which I alluded in my first lines, namely:  the
arrogant tone of these letters.  I have already explained to whose influence I
ascri~e it; but nevertheless, Gogol himself cannot be completely acquitted of it,
partkularly in view of the spiritual strivings manifested in his book.  I believe
this is a matter of great importance. We have always been quiet of mind and
humble of character; this is how we were raised by our Church, our only
teacher. Woe to us if we prove ourselves unfaithful to her wise teaching! We
owe this teaching our best national traits, our grandeur and everything that
distinguishes us from other peoples and is shaping our destiny. Unfortunately,
the new orientation of our best minds inclines precisely in that direction, and one
cannot but admit that our dear Gogol, the very one who had shown us our sinful
side in such harsh language, has succumbed to this influence. Our path is not

--- PDF page 219 / 323 ---

216                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


the one along which other peoples march; eventually we shall of course achieve
the fountainhead of good, the source of humanity; and perhaps, guided by our
holy faith, we shall be the first to glimpse the goal that God has foreordained for
mankind; but until now we have contributed little to t~e general cause of
humanity and the meaning of our purpose in the world still lies so deeply hidden
in the bosom of Providence that it would be madness for us to praise ourselves
before our elder brothers.  They are not better than we; but they are more
experienced. Your busy life in Petersburg has deafened you; you don't hear
everything that is taking place in the land of Russia. Listen to our voices: they
will reveal marvelous things to you. In the first half of your article you said a
few wise words about our newly invented national spirit; but you said nothing
about how we unwittingly strive to pervert our national character. Think about
that. You will not believe the extent to which people in our country have
changed since they shrouded themselves in this national pride, which was
unknown to our God-loving fathers.  This is what struck me most in Gogol's
book and what I think you didn't notice.  In everything else I'm in agreement
with you. Remember me to Tiutchev,7 and my heart-felt compliments to the
Princess; a good handshake to your son.

Basmannaia. 29 Apri11847

To F. 1. T. (Fedor Ivanovich Tiutchev) May 10, (1847)1

     I am delighted, dear Sir, to know that you are content with my portrait.2  It
was supposed to be lithographed in Moscow, but as a good lithographer could
not be found here, it was sent to Petersburg, and I think that you would accept
as gladly the original instead of the copy. If you would please make the effort of
informing yourself about the most reputable lithographer of Petersburg, as well
as his price, and tell me in a few words, I would be infinitely obliged to you.
You know that more than once people have requested me for my poor
countenance; so I must try to satisfy this obliging friendship.  I was going to
write to Viazemskii about it at the moment when I received your letter.  I am
going to write to him at this hour to compliment him on his article about Gogol,3
which I find excellent in contrast to the opinion of practically all our literary
people, whose anger against this poor man of genius could not be voiced.
Khomiakov is the only one who remained faithful to him or rather to himself.
   As you are undoubtedly curious about our domestic affairs, I think you will
also be curious to learn that this latter is now engaged in a very serious polemic
with Granovsky concerning the Burgundians and the Franks. You see that we
do not waste our time in trifling things and that the questions of the day
preoccupy us like the rest of the world.4 We don't have time, it is true, to
concern ourselves too much with all the silly things which happen in Europe,
such as Prussian affairs,5 for example, and other similar things, but it remains
for us, as you see, more than enough to do in order to prepare ourselves

--- PDF page 220 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                              217


worthily, in our quality as God's new people, for the lofty mission which has
been imposed upon us, for the direction of the intellectual and social movement
of the human species.
   What renders the grave debate of which I speak to you even graver still is
the fact that it comes, they say, from the moral position of our friend Khomiakov
among people of his color and your know what that position is.6 Moreover, no
matter what happens to him he will always, in my view, have the part of esteem
which belongs to him, for very fortunately in men there is always something
which matters more than their knowledge.
   What shall I tell you about me and my miserable health? We are always
balancing between a good that I do not find being good and the evil which, they
say, is not evil.  I thus vegetate in the fibbing of mind and of body. All that, you
know well, does not at all render me amusing, except for some misguided
friendship as obstinate as yours, but one must really experience the pain of the
fibbing existence which one has made.  Despite the space which separates us
your friendship forms one of my sweetest consolations and at this hour when
your forthcoming arrival has been promised in our latitudes, I will add that it
also forms my most delightful hope. So come now and you will learn what the
serious congenial feelings can do from an intelligent being upon or at least what
passed for such.  But hurry up, for the more I think about it the more I am
persuaded that it is time for me to depart from this world in one way or another,
by flight or by the tomb.  Every day I see surging around me some insolent
news or other which are taken for new powers, old lies taken for old truths, all
sorts of comic ideas taken for serious things - and all that poses as authority by
the power of the tribunal, or condemns you or absolves you, removes you or
grants you permission to speak.  It's like living in a correctional institution of the
police every day of one's life. What do you expect me to do in this new world
when nothing makes me smile, where nothing lends me a hand, and nothing
helps me to live?  After all I prefer to perish from the ennui caused by the
sadnesses of solitude rather than from the hand of men whom I loved so much,
whom I served based upon my strengths and men whom I would sti11like to
serve. Good day, dear friend. Please believe in my profound affections and my
very impatience to enjoy your amiable society.

Pierre Tschadayef

To Michael Zhikharev (1847)1

    Here, my dear friend, are the lines which you deigned to approve.2 Would
you please make a copy of it, as you promised me and send it to me.  I don't
need to repeat to you that I don't have the intention of sending it .on to their
address. Since circulation of my words is not forbidden, I would quite like that
they become known in that way.  I embrace you with all my heart.

To Michael Zhikharev, Aug. 7, (1847 or probably 1848)1

   My dear friend, I thank you for your letter. Your remembrance-gave me all
the more pleasure in that I did not anticipate it at all. You broke me off a little by
your congenial feelings. They say voyages form youth, in any case I like to
believe that the voyage only succeeded in awakening in you a sentiment which
slumbered a moment in the sweetnesses of domestic life.2 Amid my existence
ravaged by all kinds of miseries, this tender breeze come from so far away,
agreeably refreshed me.  I don't know where these lines will find you, but I
would like them to come to you before you have rendered yourself over to the
delights of Kachira,3 otherwise they run a great risk of not making your
sympathetic fiber vibrate at all. Perhaps you will not share my opinion about the
place of the Kachirian delights at this hour when you have tasted of it in all ways
in different places more or less unknown to geographers. But I don't think any
less that the true delights, so fruitful in all sorts of benefits for young men such
as yourself are to be found in our country in the village, under the paternal roof,
at the heart of this kindly tolerance which permits everything, allows one to do
anything, never condemns anything without limitations, finally, just like the
Russian horizon.
   The laughable tableau that you make of my existence -is not, I believe, very
exact, but you are perfectly correct in finding that there are beings more
miserable than I out there in the world.  I know many of them, even among
those who do not pass for unfortunate, those whose lot I am far from envying,
despite the apparent happiness or the veritable splendors which surround them.
However, a few days from now you will no longer be able to accuse me of faint-
heartedness.  The catastrophe approaches with giant steps.  I make the very
sincere wishes that this catastrophe, whatever it can be, may not blacken too
much the existences of some persons probably destined to survive me. The
other day I received a note4 which, by the time that it made, could be considered
with much exaggeration -as a veritable .decree _of assassination. My friend,sllch
are the beautiful days which I pass through. God grant that the person who
addressed this not to me does not ever know the meaning of those lines and the
fatal result which they could have. Fortunately human nature is made in such a
way that people who commit evil practically never realize the evil which they
commit. As for you, my dear friend, who for so long has spread such balm on
my life, whose affection is still for me one of the sweetest consolations, I hope
that the example of this life and the wreck that must inevitably terminate it does
not remain fruitless for you, when my memory should burnish your days with
some colors of melancholy.  It is the best part of my heritage and it rightly goes
to you in return for your friendship, without prejudice in my case against my
library which also goes to you as you know.  I just wrote to my brother in
charging him the execution of my last wishes. At the moment when I write to
you under my eyes I have his touching response in which he promises me 10
fulfill all my wishes exactly.

--- PDF page 222 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                              219

   You ask me, I believe, some news about cholera.  Just a few days ago it
devastated us horribly; at this moment it affects no more than forty patients per
day and the temporary hospitals are beginningtoelose. This'unfortunatecholera
did not want me; may God pardon it! But do you understand why it chose to let
me live? As for me, I don't understand anything it at all.
   You were wrong in writing nothing of your brother and .of .the various
charms which await him in the career which he is going to pursue. You know
that I cannot not be interested in a lively way in people who hold me from near
or from far.  I am, moreover, charmed that you have mentioned by homonym,
as you call him, for one could, I believe, become interested in favor of
Matthew.5
   Good day, my dear friend; I embrace you with all my heart.  Continue to
think about me a little if you have some leisure time; that will not be bad for you.
Moreover, one never wastes one's time by nourishing a good feeling.

Pierre Tschaadaef

To the Princess D. (Catherine Dolgorukov), January 22 (1848)1
First Letter

    Yes, Madame, "avoid the stormy question," avoid even, if possible, all
serious discussion, live as our fortunate fathers in the silence of thought and of
word.  However, what was  I  telling you.   That people have wholly
misunderstood, in my opinion, these words of the Lord, "my kingdom is not of
this world, "2 that the s.enseattributed to those words very often does not
correspond at all to the prayer which we address to God in the Lord's Prayer,
when we ask "that his reign come on earth as in heaven," and that consequently
we should not reproach the Roman 'Church for having seen in ·the words nothing
that would impede  it from according to its visible chief the prestige and
assistance of the temporal scepter.
    Note that the debate about the temporal power of the popes was not raised at
all by the Greek Church, but by the modern Protestants, children of the
eight~enth century and precursors of the sages of our day.  It was they who were
the first to discover that Jesus Christ meant heaven with these words, that the
kingdpm of God was an invisible kingdom like his church, that God claimed to
reign only in the hearts of men and other similar things. The Greek Fathers from
the council of Florence said nothing about all that. What concerned them most
was the famous filioque, this fatal exaggeration of Christian feeling which
attributed to Jesus Christ an honor which did not belong to him. As for the pope
it was his primacy which they disputed not his sovereignty, a thing which
everyone found to be very natural, since everyone found it very useful and
assuredly more than everyone, the illustrious guest of Eugene IV, the orthodox
emperor, come to Italy to implore his help against the Turks, who were camped
at the gates of Byzantium.3

--- PDF page 223 / 323 ---

220                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


   The kingdom of God will undoubtedly not be made like those of this world,
especially like some of these kingdoms and certainly that is precisely what Jesus
Christ wanted to say. But it is also not at all as kings of the earth that the pope
were destined to contribute their part in its realization on earth, it is as spiritual
chiefs of Christianity, as a living expression of religious unity, as hierarchical
symbol the the spiritual principle, hovering over all the other principles of this
world.  Similarly it is not at all as successors to the apostles, as vicars of Jesus
Christ that they reign in the city of the Caesars but very simply because history
wished it so in the interest of the Church of society and of civilization. An error
perhaps on the part of history, but in any case an error which is not lacking in a
certain appearance of truth and which could, I believe, cause a serious mind
familiar with the annals of western people and with those of our own country to
smile in pity. That is, I know, the point of view which people call human, but I
don't understand at all, I admit, how another point of view could find its
application to this world, if per chance society had been destroyed in the Middle
Ages, a thing which a more spiritual church, one more submissive to the powers
of earth, finally more perfect, might perhaps not have succeeded in preventing.
   Too often people forget what Christianity had to work with in the West,
with what forces it had to struggle. No attention is paid to the fact .that it is .not
dogma, nor the ambition of some old priests which constituted the church there,
but the impervious necessities of an entire world being born, those of these
victorious races who sat upon the smoking ruins of Rome, of those ruins
themselves, all powerful still in their dust, in their ashes so that if the church was
organized there like a kingdom of this world4 that is because it-could not do
otherwise, because its great affair in that hemisphere of the Christian world was
to save society, menaced by barbarism, as its great affair in the other hemisphere
was to save the dogma, menaced by the deleterious breath of the East and the
subtle Greek mind.  It is not the pope who went to Aix-Ia-Chapelle to place the
imperial crown on the head of Charlemagne, but it is the latter who went to
Rome to receive it from his hands.  Finally, it is not the papacy which made
western history, as our friend Tiutchev seems to believe, but it is completely on
the contrary that history there which made the papacy.
    Bless heaven for having placed the eastern church in the most favorable
conditions in the world for practicing and teaching Christian humility, but do not
accuse the western church too much of having been ambitious, for who knows
what would have come to the former, if it had been found in analogous
circumstances - our famous Nikon is a witness.s
   Those who consider temporal sovereignty as being incompatible with the
sacred character of the priest would thus do well, I find, to prove it to us in some
other way than in unceasingly repeating the words of Jesus Christ,without
taking well into account their sense, without seeking to reconcile them with the
other texts of the Bible, in which the-ideaof the Kingdom ufGod:isexpressed-in
a more precise manner.6  Besides, this incompatibility, as you know, was so
little felt otherwise that popes were far from being the only sovereign princes,

--- PDF page 224 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                                  221


bearing the miter and crown. And it is a singular thing that it is another church
which today offers us the bizarre spectacle of a priest exercising sovereignty
over a tribe of savage soldiers, and that is happening without the most fervent
defenders of the separation of the priest from political power being offended by
 it. Even more, it is from the hands of an entire holy collegiate church, glorious
inheritor of these superb patriarchs, from whom the odd politics of a great state
demanded the seat one day in order to break it the next day and from one of the
radiant lights of the eastern church that the warrior priest receives his sacred
investiture and precious ornaments, distinctive signs of his dignity.7
    This isolated factor, as you observed very well, forms,  it is true, an
exception in the bosom of this church, but unfortunately an exception which is
the result of a complacency without a serious motive on her part and for which it
is very doubtful that humanity, as well as the church herself, will ever gain any
advantage at all.
   The temporal power of the Roman pontiffs is thus basically only an
historical result which they fIrstly endured, which they subsequently accepted,
and which they fInally abused in accordance with the eternal nature of the human
 heart. But the temporal power has nothing in common with the very principle of
the Roman church, for which history bears all the responsibility:  an external
veneer for their spiritual primacy, that's all, and one which the most eminent and
more religious minds of the West believed necessary for the supreme authority
of the successors of Saint Peter, for the influence which they were called to
exercise in this world.
   The same error still, if you wish, and, one should add, an error for which
no people is better equipped than we are to appreciate the fatal consequences,
since it is in order not to fall into this error that we are destined to enjoy such
benefits denied to other Christian people, but always an excusable error since it
is to it that we also owe a thousand pieces of useful knowledge, preserved by it,
and even in a certain way all the others which were born from those there, of
which humanity was endowed by the people who experienced it.
    Note in passing that these people were far from finding themselves in the
same logical conditions as the people of antiquity, inventors of human science.
These latter ones did not know the truth. On the contrary, modem western
people misunderstood, repulsed an essential part of it, the most essential of all
perhaps, that which regulates Christian society, i.e., the very milieu in which the
Christian idea was supposed to be realized and achieve its complete bloom. A
deadly blindness which necessarily had to falsify their reason and strike them
withpo~erlessness.  Fortunately it did not turn out completely that way. The
blindness of modern western people did not render them completely sterile. The
forgiving heavens permitted  this blind race to envisage certain truths
undoubtedly in favor of other less culpable races.  The fortunate people,
possessors of the complete truth are able to preserve all the energy, all the
activity of their intellects like their erring brothers .and to render to them one day

--- PDF page 225 / 323 ---

222                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


with interest the enlightenment which the fortunate people received from their
erring western brothers.
    Finally the point of view which you defend and which we combat is
evidently the Protestant point of view, not at all that of the Greek church which
is perfectly indifferent to the matter. The storms of the question are hence not to
be found there. But here, in effect, is that which could raise them if the sweet
breezes which blow around us were not to have been destined to provide an
eternal and charming serenity in our heavens as well as in our hearts.  For
example, that would be like claiming that we suppose that a contradiction exists
in the very words of the Savior, whereas, on the contrary, the contradiction is in
their false interpretation which we pointed out with sorrow. That would be like
not accepting all the conditions for a free, frank, courteous discussion.  That
would be, in a way, like misunderstanding the very spirit of our church, so
lenient towards all opinions which don't directly attack the divine dogma, whose
guardianship was entrusted to it; that would be finally like being more orthodox
than the orthodox church herself.
    There undoubtedly is what could render the debate irritating and what could
put it on this muddy terrain of personality, on which mud does not fail to
bespatter the poor people who let themselves be led into it.  An -effect
unfortunately all too frequent in our slight usage of serious discussions, but
which I like to believe, neither you nor I, Madame, will ever experience.

To Sergei Poltoratskii, May 30, (1848)1

   A thousand thanks my dear friend, for your care and for your friendship.  I
cannot fmd from you the fact that people are little satisfied with the portrait, but
the fault lies evidently in the proof which you sent me; such is the opinion of the
artists which I share completely.2 Beyond that, as the lithograph was, as I was
assured, one of the most reputable in Paris, I have once again only to thank you
for your kindly pains.  Let's talk about other things. How, tell me, have you
resisted the temptation to say a word about the events of the day?3 That is a tour
de force which I admire more than I approve. Some impressions, some crayon
marks in your own way would have given me, I admit, great pleasure. By
chance could it not be some progress?  If it is so, then  I give you my
compliments with all my heart. By the time which runs along one must progress
as best one can, each in his own way and it is sometimes progress when one
goes backwards. For the moment I don't practice anything else, I march only by
going backwards.  I am doing admirably;  it's a locomotive process made all the
more agreeable in the certain latitudes of the globe as one is in this way certain to
travel in numerous company here. May absolutely all of humanity soon be able
to follow my example: that is my most sincere and most serious wish.
   What shall I say to you about our very fortunate, a thousand times happy
capital. They say that we always have a little of Asiatic cholera apparently to
console us for the political cholera which we lack and which European people

--- PDF page 226 / 323 ---

enjoy.4 Then, we are not at all stripped of a certain emotion, the natural effect of
our surprise at finding the world so stupid.  Inhabiting another planet, a new
planet, pulled out of nothingness only some one hundred and fifty years ago by
a crowned Leverrier,5 we have no reason undoubtedly to get mixed up in that
which is happening on the old planet which is called Europe. But how to remain
indifferent when one sees this poor humanity fall again into barbarity, sink into
anarchy, swim in its blood.
    However, here is a palpitating bit of news of our own making: we have a
new governor general. Can you guess who is the illustrious personage who is

making prose without knowing it.?
    Meanwhile, my friend, also let us cry a little and more seriously for this
poor, dear Madame Muraviev.8 You could not imagine how much I miss her
figure, all petrified with sorrow and with charity as it was. A singular matter!
This so pure, so useful woman was never loved, even by the people who had
the most motives for cherishing her, however, she had only the faults of her
virtues.  But do you know what especially hurt in her?   It was her truth.
Nothing is more insolent in effect than the true. So, my dear friend, by it is the
road to heaven.
    Perhaps you will be curious to learn what has become of her grand
daughter, Sophia, the child of exile.9 She is marrying the son of our friend
BibikovlO and renders him rich by a million. People are not too unfortunate, you
see, for having been born in Botany-Bay.ll What more shall I tell you? That I
am always sick, that I do not understand this fantasy of nature which persists for
some unknown reason in prolonging my impossible existence. People salute
you from all parties.  I am very impatient to see you again and to embrace you
provided in any case that you arrive for us in the company of a holy and just
revulsion against all the odious, abominable, horrible things of which you are
the witness.
     If you pass by Berlin see Iakushkin12 there, a funny fellow who studies law
there, muffled up in a little lady whom he just married between two barricades.
Do you conceive of that? He is the son of people whom I have loved a lot; 13
hence that is why I am interested in him.  Also go and see myoId friend
Schelling14 a little and know please what is happening amid this general
disp~acement of things and of men. His philosophic royalty, I suppose, must be
considerably compromised in the presence of so many unsettling royalties or on
the point of being unsettled.  I do not speak to you about the Circourts but you
will speak to me about them, I hope, when you return.  Besides, I don't know
why Circourt has not been writing to me, no matter how republican he is.15
    My friend, recommend me to the congenial feelings of Madame
Poltoratskii and  also  to  those  of  a  certain young  person  of your

acquaintanceship. No need, I believe, to assure you of my congenial feelings
