Letter to an Unknown (Fedor Tiutchev) July (1848)1

     I just read, my dear Sir, your interesting memoire on the events of the day.2
Permit me, before all, to express to you the pleasure which I experienced in
reading it. Then perhaps I shall still find something to tell you about it. As you
noted very well, the struggle is in effect only between the Revolution and
Russia.3 One could not characterize the question of the day any better. But I
admit that what astonishes me is not that the minds of Europe, pushed by needs
without names, by instincts without brakes, do not conceive of this so simple
fact, rather what astonishes me is that we others, in tranquil possession of the
sacred idea of which we are the depository, could not decipher  it.   It is,
moreover, quite a long time that we possess this idea. So how is it that we still
at this very hour misunderstand our mission in the world? Could it not be, by
chance, caused by the very fact of this spirit of renunciation which you point
out, correctly as the distinctive trait of our national character?4 That, I find, is
what is especially suitable for us to meditate about well.
   One could not state too often the fact that the social drama at which we assist
is only the continuation of the religious drama of the sixteenth century, this
superb protestation of human reason against the authority of tradition and of the
spiritual principle, aspiring towards the domination of society. Hence one soon
sees this protestation, which seemed so legitimate to the most serious intellects
of the epoch and which was in effect so, but which nonetheless contains within
itself the entire future of European people,  as bringing anarchy into religious
ideas first and then attacking the very foundations of the society, by denying the
divine origin of sovereign power. We were witnesses to the immense event
without taking part in it. We could and we should profit from the teaching
which it contained, but we did nothing of the sort.  The catastrophe was
completed at our gates without our understanding anything. And one moment
later we ourselves went to seek in its sources the ideas to which it gave birth, the
things which it created. Notice that the day when we undertook this pilgrimage
to the holy places of the foreign civilization in the person of the prodigious man
who expressed then the heart and soul of the nation,S as the man who bears his
title today expresses them,6 that day came immediately after that which saw the
complete development of our religious idea, for we were at the era after the
patriarchate.7
   Thanks be to heaven, we imported into our country only some externals of
this evil civilization, as well as some products of this fatal science. Civilization
and science itself remained foreign to us. But it is always so that we learned to
know European countries sufficiently in order to be able to judge about the

--- PDF page 228 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                               225


profound difference which exists between the nature of their society and the
nature of the society in which we live.  In reflecting upon this difference we
should have naturally come to hold a high opinion of our institutions, to attach
ourselves to them more profoundly  still, to convince ourselves of their
excellence, as well as of the power of the elements on which our social order is
based. We should have found in our traditions, in our customs, in our beliefs,
the form of our intimate life, in that of our public life, in our very prejudices, in a
word, in all that which makes up our national factor, all the necessary conditions
for an admirable development, all the sources for an infinite act of perfection, all
the gems for an immense future. But it was not so. For from the moment when
we came into contact with foreign ideas, we hastened to abjure our old
indigenous ideas, we very quickly renounced our ancient customs, we forgot
our venerable traditions, we tranquilly let our secular institutions fall apart one
by one; we abdicated almost our entire past, we only preserved our religious
beliefs.
      It is true that these beliefs which constitute the basis of our social state were
enough to safeguard us from the invasion of the worst principles of the foreign
civilization, against the breath of its most deletorious emanations, but the beliefs
were powerless to develop in us a feeling for the role which we were called upon
to fulfill among the people of earth.  So, in submitting to the yoke .of this
civilization, we retained the virtues of our fathers, their spirit of submission,
their love for the prince, their taste for abnegation and for renunciation. But at
the same time the idea deposited in our bosom by the hand of Providence did not
mature.  Misunderstanding completely this great idea each day we allowed
ourselves to be permeated more and more by new ideas. And when the new
catastrophe which struck the world for a second time occurred, we did not profit
from it at all.
  We were again not capable of discerning the advantages of the social state
which we had the good fortune to enjoy, nor the privileges which Providence
accorded us in favor of the purity of our beliefs and of the profound faith with
which our hearts were imbued. A singular thing!  The more the events
unfolding under our eyes explain, so to say, the problem of the world and show
us the lofty destiny reserved for us, the less we are capable of conceiving it.  It is
evident that all the revolutions at which we assisted during a half a century, far
from clarifying the state of the countries which experienced them, far from
clarifying the state of our own country for us, only served to cloud our intellects.
And.if at the tpoment we are now there is a certain awakening of the national
prinpiple, a ce:r;tain return to the ancient traditions which were the good fortune of
our fathers and which were the source of their virtues,becoming manifest
among us in a more or less evident manner, then one must admit that that is -a
very recent phenomenon and one which up to this hour still bears only the
character of an historical idea, a literary tendency perfectly unknown to the
country.

--- PDF page 229 / 323 ---

226                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


Letter to Princess D (Catherine Dolgorukov) Second Letter October 6, 1850
(Second Letter)1

   Madame, at this hour you will have read, I think, the protestation of Mr. de
la Rochejaquelin, of which I spoke to you the other day.2  I told you that this
piece appeared to me to be of particular interest to us other Russians and to raise
questions very often debated among us. Very well, permit me now to tell you
the reflections which it suggested to me. We are going to find ourselves on a
burning terrain, I warn you, but I like to believe that we will traverse it without
burning our shoes.
    Mr. de la Rochejaquelin supports himself with a celebrated passage from
Massillon.  I am going to transcribe the piece completely; it merits well being re-
read the way that it came from the Christian orator. Here in what terms the
humble bishop of Clermont expresses himself at Versailles in the presence of the
successor of Louis XIV, of that king who had said, "the state is me":

    "But, Sire, a prince, a great man is not made for himself alone, he owes
    himself to his subejct. In elevating him, the people confided power in him
   and reserved for themselves his cares, his time and vigilance.  It is the
    people who make the kings, just as they are. Yes, Sire, it is the choice of
    the nation which first put the scepter into the hands of your ancestors, it is
    the nation which elevated them on the military shield and proclaimed them
    sovereigns. Henceforth the kingdom became the heritage of their
    successors, but they ordinarily continue with the free consent of the nation.
    Their birth alone places them in possession of the throne, but it was the
    public vote which first attached this prerogative to their birth. In a word,
    as the initial source of their authority comes from us, kings should make
    use of it only for us. "3

   What do you say about that, Madam? Don't you find that it might be useful,
that it might be necessary that kings hear this language sometimes amid the
flatterers, courtesans, and valets who surround them. Do you think that God in
truth, in instituting his ministers, would have wished them to cause his word to
re-echo in all its authority not only in the ears of the people but also in those of
their masters?  I am pleased to believe that, sincere and serious, your beliefs will
not allow you to refuse to admit it. Very well, do you know what is necessary
for the priest to be able to speak in that way? . The church must be free; she
should be obedient externally and internally only to herself, i.e., to Jesus Christ
always present in her midst. But, what must be done for things to be that way?
Do you know of any other means than that which western people found, aided
by history, by the prestige of the name of Rome, by the heritage of the prince of
the apostles, by the logical spirit which distinguishes them? Do you know of
another solution to this great problem other than that of a visible chief,
independent of all human power, a sovereign himself, a living expression of the

--- PDF page 230 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                             227


unity of the church and of her authority in the necessary intervals when she
cannot make her voice understood in order to proclaim her sacred and infallible
will.  As for me, I don't know of another way.  Don't all the theological,
dogmatic, canonical and other considerations become obliterated before this
supreme consideration of the independence of the church.  Is not the entire
authority of the seven ecumenical obliged to bow before that authority, the first
of all, since it implies the very existence of the church, otherwise impossible to
understand, if this light did enjoy all its liberty?
    There you have the entire question of the filioque, reduced to its simplest
expression. As if it all boiled down to filioque. The church, or if you prefer,
this fraction of the·church where the idea·ef.her·universality and·ef.her·uni~y was
the most profoundly felt, where the necessity of this idea was seen to be
accepted most sincerely, wished to be obeyed in its chief, that's alL That part of
the church wished to be independent, sovereign, free from all human fetters in
the interests of the truth, religion, and humanity, confided to her solicitude by
Jesus Christ himself. She did not want to be tossed about by the winds of each
given power,of each man who proclaimed himself the master of his peers. That
is what is termed the ambition, the usurpation of Rome, but basically it was,as
one Sees, only the natural effect of the serious interests of European people,
applied to the most serious matter on earth:  that matter is the necessary
development of the religious ideal in society, i.e., in the very milieu in which the
religious ideal meant to achieve all its results, all its consequences. Undoubtedly
this ~xtraordinary destiny which the nature of things, history and religion had
bequeathed to the Roman Church had to give her the desire and the means of
abusing it, and no one ever denied that.  It is only too true that the human too
often got mixed up with the principle and that the earthly interests got confused
more than one time with heavenly interests. But it is no less true that in enduring
the inconveniences of the destiny imposed on the church in one part of .the
Chri$tian world, humanity extracted in general great advantages, in prodigious
exer~ise of intelligence, a lofty esteem for the power of thought, the germ of a
prof~1ind disdain for all the brutal powers of society, an extraordinary elan
towatds those regions from which fall all the truths, all the goods, all the things
whicl1 would not have taken place, if the spiritual power had not "been
estab~ished as an independent and sovereign power at the very summit of
society, if it had remained in the antichambers of temporal power.
    ~in:ally;whether one conceives of the church ·a-ccordingto the viewpoint of
the ~rqtesta:~ts, simply as an assembly of the faithful, or according to the
viewIi'oiat of .the Roman Church as represented by her clergy, the fact remains
that she must ibefree to think, to act and especially to preach as she wishes. But
that i~ prec;~s~ly what is impossible without a constitution guaranteeing her
against the encroachments of the powers of this world.
    :aut perhl\.ps, Madame, you would have preferred that the church silent,
enclosed in: its· rituals, bowed before the political powers, might never have
dared to instruct the great and the earthly masters that her words had been
accorded or forbidden following the whim of these men, who are always so

--- PDF page 231 / 323 ---

228                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


delighted, as you know, to hear the truth.  Perhaps you also would have
preferred that the most eloquent, the most illustrious, the most holy ministers
had been obliged to become stunted according to the caprices, the incapacities,
and the interests of these same men. Perhaps you find that it is not necessary
that God's word should enjoy its complete liberty, that it should be muzzled by
human hands. Perhaps you think that preaching is not a necessary attribute of
the successors of the apostles, that the Church of Jesus Christ, built by the
word, has no more need of it today, and that the church can henceforth perfectly
disp(!nse with it for the greatest good of the faithful and the greatest glory of the
Savior. Finally perhaps you consider that the church can live her complete life,
fulfill her august functions, achieve all her results with her tongue tied, her
inspirations choked off,  her authority mutilated.  If by chance that was your
opinion, then I would have nothing more to say to you, and it would only
remain for me to invoke in silence this Spirit whom Jesus Christ sent to earth,
"even though He does not come from me," so that He may deign to enlighten
Y01lf heart and place your misguided intelligence ·backupon the road·of the truth.
    But I hope that such is not the case, and that you are also as convinced as I
am that, if unfortunately there exists in some unknown corner of the globe, a
Christian community reduced to this deplorable state, such could never be the lot
of the true church of Jesus Christ, the universal and orthodox church to which
we have the good fortune to belong.
  A word more.  The Bible, they say, ·has pronounced that the Spirit
proceeded from the Father. Yes, but then where did it declare that the Spirit did
not proceed from the Son? Why, I ask you, does one preclude the other? Does
not Jesus Christ share with the Father all His titles, except that of paternity?
Why then would he not spare with Him that one too in virtue of which the Holy
Spirit proceeds from the Father?  But, people still say to me, you have then
forgotten that before the eighth century the Roman Church herself had not
professed that the Spirit proceeded from the Son. Undoubtedly no, but as for
you, have you forgotten in your turn that the Roman Church had not professed
either that He proceeded from the Father before the council of Constantinople,
i.e., up until the fourth century.4 The fact is that the creeds were never drawn up
except in view of some heresy or other.  So, as the part of the Son in the
procession of the Holy Spirit had never been challenged before the eighth
century it was perfectly useless to make mention of it in the creed, that's all.
   Do you want to know the entire history of the filioque in two words or less?
Here it is: Spanish sectarians, inheritors of these Aryans who denied the divinity
of Jesus Christ, got it into their heads one day to deny also Jesus Christ's part in
the procession of the Holy Spirit which no one, as we have just seen, had either
affirmed or denied up until then. The Church, as she had to do, firstly hesitated
about pronouncing in favor of a novelty, rendered necessary by these heretics,
and Leo III suspended the famous silver tablets with the old creed in ·Greek and
in Latin in the church of St. Peter.5 But jealous of rendering to Jesus Christ the
honor due Him, the Roman Church did not delay in introducing the filioque into

--- PDF page 232 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                             229

the creed, convinced as she was firstly that she could not lose the esteem of God
by furnishing to the faithful a new subject for blessing the divine name of His
Son. The text was supposed to have in the eyes of the Roman church only a
conditional importance, given the fact that all its canonical value was owed to the
Roman church alone.   Finally, the universal church could not lose her rights,
that she would enjoy all the prerogatives, all the inspirations, all the lights of the
primitive church even unto the end of time.  Meanwhile, the two principles
which since time immemorial divided the world, namely, the idea and the form,
the thought and its more or less incomplete expression, came to meet on the new
terrain, where all the serious interests of humanity had given themselves a
rendezvous since the coming of the Savior. And finally when the fatal moment
arrived in which they were in contact, the idea, having once been manifested
according to the regular procedure of reason, could only accord certain
concessions in favor·of·unity, which the Roman·churchdid not fail to·do, all the
while knowing in advance that she would be accused of being inconsistent for
this act of fraternal charity by those very ones whom the Roman church desired
to be able to profit by it. In its turn the wording, also once having seen the light
of day, could no longer be abjured.
   The two Christian families hence ·stayed ·each in it-s ·respective ·role. One
audaciously took off from the wording and did not fear to attribute to Jesus
Christ a title which had not yet been generally attributed to Him, but which the
one CIn:istian family believed belonged to him in accordance with the spirit of the
sacred text. More timid, more faithful to the wording, the other Christian family
refused this title to him and treated as schismatics those who had, made,
according to that Christian family, the part of the Lord too great,  those who
dared to add a new grandeur to His grandeurs, yet another reason to adore Him.
Such is the march of history. What must one conclude from that? Evidently that
the exaggerated love of the Lord had led the faith of European people astray and
that we stayed with the literal truth of the biblical doctrine, thanks to our docility
towards the sacred text. Bless heaven for the fact that it accorded to us a more
sober faith than to our western brothers, and let us hope that God will deign to
pardon them for their misguidedness in favor of their ardent love for His well-
beloved Son.                                                            .
   , ,Madame, I submit these reflections to your kindly intelligence and in
advance I subscribe to your judgment. May it be dictated by this pious tolerance
whicrhs~i1Jidistinguishes us from all the other nations of the world, but which, I
greatly fear, will soon become among us only an amiable appanage of some elite
m\tUres :suah as you.

Letterto V. A.Zhukovskii of May 27 (1851)1

    Perhaps many, like me have not yet thanked you, my dear Vasilii
Andreevich, for sending us your latest work-s, ·but .few I ·think ·have the ·same
excuse as I. You probably remember that some ten years ago you left me in a

--- PDF page 233 / 323 ---

230                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


house that even then was collapsing from delapidation and which, in your
words, was supported not by pillars but solely by the spirit.  Since then it has
continued to decay steadily and to frighten me and my visitors by its oblique
aspect. Such is one of the ridiculous trials of my inane existence, of which there
are many, and willy-nilly they sometimes distract me from discharging the
pleasantest of obligations; but still I confess my guilt to you in order, however,
that you should listen to what I have to say about another matter, which I hope
you will see as a token of my belated gratitude.
  A few days ago Bulgakov2 showed me your short missive about your
adventures with Fanny Esler.3 Do you know the impression these few lines
made on me? They were a sad reminder of the losses I have suffered; they
reminded me that no longer is there anyone among us who could at least laugh at
us with a certain authority, that is, with some benefit. 'Some are far distant,
some are dead.' So now we enjoy complete impunity, tell whatever lies we like
both in conversation and on paper, in friendly discussions and before the public.
It can't be said that we have become less intelligent than before, but nor can it be
said that we have grown wiser.  It goes without saying that we have learned
much about which we had no inkling before, but what's the point of that if all
this newly acquired knowledge is either barren or is expressed in some obscure
dialect that bores the reader to tears. Censorship is not a teacher, it can teach us
nothing, and taste least of all.  It's time you visited us:  this is the drift of my
letter. You promise to be here in the month of September, but will it be for long,
that is the question.  If you come only to look at us and enjoy the sight of us,
what would be the use of that! No, come to live with us and to instruct us. You
have lived too long in your foreign back of beyond; this is truly a sin. Who
knows, perhaps God will reward you for this good deed and restore your wife's
health in the land of the Orthodox? You won't believe how spoilt we have
become since we began to live without our mentors. The lack of authority is
destroying us.  Neither in the world of the bookmen nor in that of the
conversationalists is there anyone left from that group of honored individuals
who until recently were the leaders in society and guided it; and if a few
survivors are to be found, they are decaying somewhere in mental and emotional
solitude. Everyone is now speaking about orientatio~s; it is not orientation but
administration that we need.  You can't teach reading and writing without
teachers.
  We have many self-made authorities, but individuals with true standing are
yet to appear in the enlightened stratum of society. Of course, when we did have
authorities, among whom were you, they were not always obeyed:  this has
always been the case; but still, the presence of people who were universally
respected not only for their intellectual accomplishments but for the traits ·of their
character was beneficial and taught newcomers modesty. That word is absent
from our most recent pocket dictionary. Please come at least to help us find it.
It's strange! Never has there been less humility in evidence here than since some
among us began to expatiate about that Christian code which more than any other

--- PDF page 234 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                                231


Christian code teaches humility, which is itself nothing but humility. Here is an
example. Last year one of the most fervent exponents of the retrograde tendency
wrote a play.4 Whether it was good or bad is not important; the play was written
in praise of that way of life which was allegedly destroyed by the arbitrary will
of a great man5 (who was, nevertheless, a product of this very way of life); in
the opinion of our friends, that is the author's sympathizers, that was all that was
required. But then it is performed in the local theatre; and what happens? On the
day of the performance there appears in the Gazette an article by the author
himself in which he artlessly points to his creation as an example of a truly
Russian play.6  Note that no one found this amazing, no one even paid any
attention to this, for it seemed natural to everyone. And it's no surprise; how
can the unquestioning worship of a single idea not lead to the worship of the
mind to which it owes its existence, be that mind your own or that of your
friends. Well, that is where we are at. Still, the author is an intelligent, kind and
honorable man; but he had to pay tribute to the age. For we are livirrg-inanage
of our own, although one that is not as dissolute as your foreign epoch.
     I don't know whether Bulgakov showed your letter to our countess;  I
believe he only copied out for her those lines that could flatter her author's
vanity. I should have liked to borrow it from Bulgakov in order to show her the
whole letter according to myoid habit of loving my friends, not only for their
sake bur for mine, but I didn't know the address of her house.  It must however
be said that her encomium to the elderly dancing girl outraged all decent people
both· here and in Petersburg.  I called all this nonsense Ie culte du jarret and
asked Mme. Rostophchin to translate this expression into Russian, but she was
unable to.7
   At parting I once again repeat my most humble request to you to return to
your native country.  It's bad for minors to be without a tutor. This is the reason
that prompted me to pick up my pen, to which, as you can see, I have grown
somewhat unaccustomed, otherwise I should not have been as loquacious. Pray
treat my banter with indulgence and not as a teacher.  I embrace you with all my
soul and with all my heart.  I have your manuscripts here, but I haven't had time
to see them, althought they are at the Red Gates. Farewell. Your truly devoted
Petr Chaadaev.

Article concerning the coup d'etat of Napoleon III (1851)1

     It would be a great mistake,  I believe, if one were to think that the
extraordinary events taking place in France were accomplished for the benefit of
the prin~iple of order.  It is a democracy, constituted democracy, organized as a
permanent factor which alone will profit from these events.  I do not say that
democracy is necessarily incompatible with order, but I think that for the
moment it is not the principle of order at all which triumphs but democracy all by
itself; later we shall see. Whether the official monarchists rejoice about it or not,
that is their affair. As for us, faithful subjects of a legitimate prince, of a serious

--- PDF page 235 / 323 ---

232                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


prince, inheriter of a race brought to the throne by the unanimous and sincere
wish of the country, we are only capable of deploring profoundly this brutal
success of force against law.
   The hero of this drama, part buffoon, part sinister, will never be anothing
thing in our eyes but a clever and fortunate adventurer, and nothing more. A
power acclaimed by the armed forces having filched a foolish wish from a
country at bay, could never contain within itself anything of the conservative, no
guarantee for the security of the future. There is nothing in common between
that power and the legitimate powers, dynastic or otherwise, founded upon the
traditions of the people and upon political morality.  If, by chance, he might
happen to do some good, to restore a little calm on the agitated sea of society,
this could come about only to the detriment of the actual welfare, the real
prosperity of the country.  Dictatorship, good or bad, necessary or useless, can
never be but a regretable provisionary measure, whose continuation could only
be desired by those who lack the simplest notions of morality.
    Napoleon was an exception to the general rule; he was a great man, an
isolated fact in the contemporary history of the world; his grandeur excused his
seizure of power.  His nephew, on the contrary, is only an impudent and
insolent adventurer, propelled just by chance to the head of a great nation. He is
one who prevailed over the real or presumed anarchy in which he found the
nation in order to impose upon her a praetorian anarchy all the more destable
because it has its source not in the ignorant masses but in the elite of society.
    Moreover it represents an anarchy which could also spread around the world
as much as the other, it is said, failed to do so. All men of the measure of Louis
Napoleon, and his ant-hill world, placed in the same circumstances will
henceforth wish to repeat his adventure.  It is claimed that the cabinets, calling
themselves conservatives, approved of it. Too bad then for these cabinets if
they have the clumsiness to applaud this acrobatic leap, accompanied with tears
and grimacing teeth. One can find it to be amusing, if you wish, to see France
transformed into an Olympic circus, but one must not forget that in assisting .at
these kinds of spectacles one must pay the price.  Besides, all the acts of
usurpation have always felt the need to make their crime pardonable by flattering
national vanity. But is there a way of satisfying national vanity in a bellicose
nation other than at the costs of other nations! Hence, advice to the neighbors.
   The fact is that wise minds, true men of order could still never count upon
the support of the armed forces during bad days; this illusion no longer exists.  It
remains henceforth proven that this force, just as it was in the days of the Roman
Caesars and just as it was in more recent epochs, will never be anything, despite
the progress of Christian enlightenment, other than)a docile instrument in the
hands of the first given power which will have something to offer the army or to
promise to it. So it is the disgrace of the army in the moral opinion of the
civilized world which is just being accomplished in this day on the soil which is
classic for social experiments of the human species. Such is, according to me,
the most important result concerning the fact of the weapons of Louis Napoleon.

--- PDF page 236 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                               233


Let Mr. Cobden2 and company congratulate themselves, that they triumph over
the fall of the army, it's admirably done, but I don't see too much, I admit, that
honest people, who are not going to the peace conference but who very simply
desire peace in the world, could rejoice about.
   From this time forward all honest powers, all government not degraded by
the abuse of it,s authority, will retreat before the necessity of serving this machine
of high passion called the army.  And then, what can one oppose to the
formidable march of the idea of the century, socialism, demagoguery or by
whatever other horrible name you may call it? For my part, I don't know "at-all.
Perhaps one could with some chance of success oppose it with a new idea, a
fresh idea, more powerful and truer than all those which in recent times traversed
the world by devastating it. But who will find this very fortunate idea? Will it
be one of these poor brains wandering misguided amid the rubbish of old
regimes and with the new regimes in order to re-establish the old ones by
exaggerating their "accomplishments?
   Do you think that the more or less silly devotees of order are nonetheless
going to pull it out one of these days form the bottom of old cast-off clothes or
from the heap of armour soiled by their Gothic arsenals.  Don't dream  it.
Should the idea itself come to offer itself to their astonished regards, they would
take it to be the statue of the commandant returned from hell and, more cowardly
than Don Juan, they would refrain, believe me, from inviting it to their feudal
banquet.
    But, people say, the coup d'etat of Louis Napoleon saved France from the
fatal crisis of 1852.  Firstly it would have to be proven that this crisis was "as
imminent as people say. Undoubtedly Mr. Carlier3 may know much more at
length about this matter and if he affirms that the catastrophe was inevitable, one
must well believe him at his word, but there is one thing that everyone ignores
and that is that, invested with the right to declare all of France in a state of siege,
the executive power thereby found itself deprived of all the possible means of
repressing evil passions, of crushing mutiny of killing socialism without
departing from legality, without killing the constitution. So, what was the good
of the coup d'etat?  But, people say, it was still necessary that the president
remain in power, for he alone could ward off the storm, and for that it was
necessary to re-establish the universal suffrage which the national assembly did
not want. Good luck! Well, here we are returned to the pure democracy of Mr.
Proudhon.4
     1:)0 not, my dear friend, confuse the sinister events of our day with previous
revolutions. The troubled European minds can misinterpret the true character of
the cataclysm which they are experiencing, carried away as they are by the
general torment, menaced as they are or as they feel they are when it comes to
their dearest interest and their most previous goals. But as for us others, happy
and calm, regurgitating with all kinds of felicites, let us evaluate things better, let
us learn how to distinguish the appearance from the truth.  It is our privilege to
have nothing to do with the miseries of the rest of the world, to have only to

--- PDF page 237 / 323 ---

234                                 FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


regard what other people do and to profit from their faults and from their
success. Totally disinterest in their cause, unmoved jurors, let us judge it with
the serenity of minds disengaged from.all passion and from all prejudice.
   What did the first revolution want? The abolition of certain odious and
oppressive privileges of certain absurd distinctions, which those who profitted
by them did not themselves want anymore. Also were not the true authors of
this revolution gentlemen, members of the clergy, men of letters belonging to
these classes or living among them? Now that is no longer the matter; it now is
only a matter of a more equal repartitioning of the national riches, a more
equitable distribution of the goods of the earth. A question of social economy,
posed by the natural march ofl things, by the logical development of the
economic idea in society, which ~lreOCCUPied the spirit in all times, but for which
the solution has become urgent n our day on the soil of France, thanks to an
egotistical and shabby governm nt which only knows how to come to terms
with the needs of its time, witho  t ever forthrightly thinking of ever satisfying
them, thanks toa blinded family which imagines that it can revive again for an
epoch of civilization that which has never been seen except in times of
ignorance, namely the establishment of a dynasty which has not understood that
our century could very well endure and preserve some thrones but not to
establish any new ones again.
  A singular thing: People finished by finding quite just the revolt against the
privileges of birth, qut, birth after all is only a law of nature as well known as
gravity or chemical affinity. And today one finds unjust the revolt against the
insolence of capital, which is a thousand times more oppressive, more brutal
than the pretensions of birth ever were. Birth obliges, it is said.  I am not aware
that today it is said "capital obliges."
   But history will be merciless towards the man who, holding in his hands the
destiny of several millions of his fellow men, played with them because his
ambition, his whim, or his madness and of whom Europe will be able to say
after the ruination of forty years of peace that "it was he who began it."
   The reaction in European countries, where peace has just been upset by the
recent events, has as its purpose the strengthening of the power shaken by these
events. Hence it is not a matter of re-establishing in these countries the ancient
order of things, borne away by the hurricane of the century, but very simply to
render to governments the force which they lack.  But what do the peevish
children who speak about reaction in our country really want? Power in our
country is not lacking any force.   It is found invested here with sufficient
authority. Public security is not menaced in our country by indifferent masses of
proletariats. Finally, we enjoy all the benefits of a government functioning with
all the regularity and all the liberty imaginable. Against what then do you wish
to react in our country?  It is evident that it can only be directed against the
invasion of certain ideas and certain principles which were never foreign to us
and which a revolution of a particular nature, unknown to other countries of the
world, introduced into our country.  But how were these ideas and these
principles manifested, where were they produced?  Apparently in the new

--- PDF page 238 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                              235


institutions which ruled us. And where would the ancient ideas and ancient
principles be found characterized?  Isn't it in the institutions which no longer
exist?  It is true that the new institutions were imposed upon us by a power
arisen from our soil, from the bowels of our society, such as it was formed by
history, which in its turn is merely the result of the particular character of the
Slavic race, of the nature of our religious beliefs and especially of the
geographical and economic conditions of the country which we inhabit.
Nonetheless it is in the ancient social forms that the primitive elements of society
resided and were preserved. These forms were nothing but the expression of
our national spirit, of our institutions, of our total being. So if one wishes to
generate a truly national impulse upon our country, it is evident that there is
nothing else to do but to revert to the ancient forms, erased by the system
inaugurated at the outset of the past century, and which, one must admit, had
obtained the universal vote of the country either by the effect of an adroit
juggling act, or by that of an irresistible seduction or finally by that of a violence
without any example in the history of the world. Such is in effect the thought of
our reactionaries, if they still exist, moreover inoffensive types, as one sees,
since the wishes which they form tend only to assure to the political power even
more authority, if that is even possible, and to enclose it in even more prestige,
to inspire the people to even more veneration for it than ever.

To Michael Zhikharev, Nov. 1 (1853)1

     Finally, my dear friend, you come to give me a sign of life. When I say
"you come" I mean that your letter reached me a month ago, but, as you know,
the measure of time has no longer existed for me for a long time. In this case, as
you see, I am partially taking the part of this poor century, so brutally scoffed at
by you, but which in -any -case is still -not -agitated any less within the -narrow
limits of time and space, despite those noxious vapors from it, an apt symbol of
the rapidity of its course, which so justly upset our patriotic heart. The fact is
that the look of the civilized world had in effect to change its face a little since I
had the honor of seeing it for the last time some thirty years ago.2 But it is also
true that the picturesqueness of Europe, that of the Rhine river, for example,
were not exactly like the picturesqueness of Kachira and of whatever river flows
over there. You must do it, my friend, and content yourself a little that you will
find it in the rest of Europe.  I don't even know if your urbane existence at the
amiable Chamovniky3 did not offer more nourishment for your rustic tastes than
the most desolate Alpine peaks will be able to offer you, should you deign to
visit them, where, after all, some traces of civilization are seen. No matter what,
if you have gone to search in Europe, beyond an increase in health, nothing but
country sweetness, clumsy pilgrim, I fear that in the final analysis you may find
that you have quite badly set up your program.  So decide to request from
western civilization, as people say in the Arbat, something else other than the
steppe with its numerous and varied delights. Who knows perhaps you will end

--- PDF page 239 / 323 ---

236                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


up by finding in this civilization something which may compense you for your
deprivation up  to a certain point.    I would hope  that the demon of
picturesqueness would push you to England; you would find there, at least I
hope, that the ravages of civlization have not completely inveiled the delightful
figure of nature at least so long as the steam engine, gas and electricity have not
come to overturn completely this country so privileged by its landscape.
     It was in the year of our Lord 1823, I believe, I was living in a cottage of
Sussex, covered with ivy and vines.4 My house faced out upon one of those
walls of a classic parsonage; on the other side I saw the field of my rustic
civilized proprietor who, upon finishing the works-oithe-day, played the ·sonata
of Pleyel on the fluteS and other pieces of no less entertaining music. Before me
a little castle, no longer the sojourn of the Queen Charlotte of scandalous fame. 6
Far away the sea always scintillated, despite the eternal fog hovering over the
entire country . Well, all that appeared very picturesque to me, I admit, without
speaking about Miss Alice with whom I sometimes rode in my tilbury.7 Go and
see that, my friend.

Article for L'Univers, Jan. 15 (1854)1

   The Russians do not cease reproaching us for our ignorance of all that
concerns their country.  It is very true, and we readily admit, that we do not
know their vast empire better than that of the Birmans, even though it is situated
at our gates. We shall even agree that, if you wish, there exists no people on
earth who are less known than the Russian people. But, I ask you, how can one
come to know well a people, whether they still remain on the world scene or
whether they've left it, if it makes no difference. Does one not come to know a
people by studying their monuments and their writers and by asking the people
themselves about what constitutes their particular nature, and what sets them
apart from the other nations of the world? But, where are the monuments of the
Russian people, where are writers who will reveal to us the distinctive traits of
this people established between East and West - a people who, they say,
threatens East and West by its ambitious aspirations? Do Russians, by chance,
imagine that the immense expanse of a country is. enough to make  it an
interesting branch of human knowledge and enough to give us the desire to
know the language, laws and social state of the tribes which inhabit it? That is a
pure illusion of national vanity which science can take into account only to
register it among the aberrations of people destined to serve for the teaching of
generations to come.
   So what is Russia then for us? A fact, nothing but that, a completely bare
fact which tends to roll over the map of the globe in more gigantic proportions
day by day and hence which must be stopped from being able to grow out of
control and from encroaching upon the old civilized world, inheritor, depository
and guardian of all previous civilizations including the civilization from which

--- PDF page 240 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                              237


Russian once took her first bits of culture and the pompous and sterile form of
worship in which she remains enclosed.
   What could you wish to be studied there? A page of geography, that's -all,
in order to be able to hold onto something perhaps more imaginary than real
concerning this power. Moreover, doesn't everyone know that Russia owes a
great portion of her force to European civilization, to that civilization from which
she profited quite precisely because she did not possess any other?  But, this
civilization, its forms, its procedures and its results are perfectly known, so there
is nothing there to learn.  Everyone also knows that, deprived of western
enlightenment, Russian would have become booty for one or other of her
warlike neighbors who were more advanced than she in the art of warfare.  If
she had achieved her actual state by means of her internal development, if she
had gained her political importance from her own nature, it would have been an
entirely different matter. Then everyone, including the entire civilized world,
would have undoubtedly inquired about this fruitful and powerful nature, the
elements which compose it, the character which she imprints upon -numerous
and intelligent populations, the different phases which she underwent - but it
was not at all like that. As is known, one fine day Russia herself shook up all
that constituted her particular physionomy, apparently recognizing the
insufficiency of her national element, and then Russian clothed herself in the
forms of modem civlization. And it is only from that day that she became
pow¢rful and that Europe turned its uneasy regards towards her not as a new
object for study or for meditation but very simply as a political fact which
Europe had to observe in order not to become absorbed by Russia.
    There are, I know, a good number of Russians today, some of whom we
are acquainted with in Paris, who claim that Russian underwent the reform of
Peter the Great despite herself. But in attributing to the energetic will of one sole
man, be he the greatest of mortals, a revolution which by their own admission
transformed the country from top to bottom, what are the clumsy patriots doing
if not dishonoring their nation rather than exonerating it?  But such averse
reaction is far from being the opinion of the majority of Russians who have not
been made fanatics by the retrospective utopias of their new national school.
Whatwould you think, in effect, about a nation which would firstly allow the
fruit of its own history to be ravaged by the fantastic whim of one. of its
sovereigns and then when providence itself appeared to be charged with the care
of facilitating the nation's return to the venerable ways of its fathers by accprding
the nation four successive reigns by women - and, my God, what kind of
women, a disgrace to their sex - and betterthan that,after-an entire -century of
praetorian revolutions, such a nation would remain unmoved in the new mold in
which it was cast despite itself? Very well, such is however the idea of their
nation formed by these good Russians of the new school so jealous for the glory
of Russia.
    Fortunately for the honor of the human species, things proceeded in a
completely different manner.  Peter the Great only continued a revolution the
origins of which, in a manner of speaking, we discover already in the first pages

--- PDF page 241 / 323 ---

238                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


of Russian history. He reformed only that which existed only nominally. He
abolished only that which could no longer endure. He established only what
was in the process of being established by itself. He acccomplished only what
his predecessors had already attempted to accomplish before him.  In my
opinion, that is the only rational way for us to take into account his famous
reform program and the acquiescence which the Russian people gave to it. But
if one is just aware that the entire history of his people is basically composed
only of a series of successive abdications in favor of their princes, that the nation
began its historical career by giving itself over to a handful of Scandinavian
adventurers invited in by the nation itself to come and govern it, that the nation
then went to seek a religion among foreign people, that the nation later borrowed
more odious customs from the conquering savages of their country, and finally
that the nation has not stopped undergoing all kinds of exterior influences - then
undoubtedly one will find the grand act of submission, which initiated our
civilization and introduced it into our political system, to be even more natural.
And hence one should no longer be surprised to see the ancient possessors of
this civilization and of this political system attaching so little interest to the study
of its social state.
    In any case, the moment has come when our ignorance about the place of
Russia could no longer continue without causing detriment to our own security.
Finally we must seriously seek to take into account the principle which pushes
this immense empire beyond its limits and causes it to exert a formidable
pressure on the rest of the world, a pressure which we considered for too long a
time as the effect of an ambitious politics of a profoundly contrived system, but
which is, as we are going to see soon, only the very natural effect of several
causes of a completely different character. But no matter what it is, a political,
moral or geographical phenomenon, it doesn't matter, we can no longer afford
avoiding plunging ourselves into a profound study of this phenomenon no
matter what it is. We must try to prove to Russia herself, if possible, that she
marches to her own doom every time that she directly opposes to the old
civilized races, who are more powerful a thousand times than she, due to the
long and laborious work of their intellects and due to the continuous,
perservering exerciseoftheir faculties rather than ·their material forces. We must
also try to prove to Russia that Russia ,hers.elf owes everything tothat,and.even
this feeling of exclusive nationalism which she arms herself with today against
us and boasts with pride.
    Moreover, one must not imagine that this is a task fraught with .extraordinary
difficulties:   it is not at all.   It is enough to have passed some years among
Russians to become convinced that, given the conditions which the government,
which they have provided themselves with, created for them, an enlightened and
impartial foreigner, despite the insufficiency of materials offered to his
investigations, possesses in fact not only more means of evaluating their social
state than the Russians themselves have and that he still can, without too much
presumption, charge himself with the task of enlightening them about the
processes in which they are engaged.

--- PDF page 242 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                              239


     Firstly two elements appear in Russian history:  the geographical element
already pointed out by us and the religious element. Then one just adds to it a
third element, the servitude of the rural population.  We shall concentrate
principally upon the latter, because it results, it seems to us, from the two others
and completely embodies them. But, before going any further, we·note that up
until the days of Peter the Great's reforms, Russians knew no other teacher than
their church and hence consequently the entire moral development of this great
people before that epoch, whatever the development was, rightly belonged to the
church alone.
    Everyone knows that serfdom exists in Russia, but people are very far from
realizing its true nature, its role, and its importance in the social order of this
country. For example, one would commit a great error should one proceed to
assume that its influence is exerted only upon this unfortunate .partofthenation
which endures its oppressive weight. On the contrary, its influence upon the
classes which profit from it must be studied in a special way if one wishes to
comprehend its most deplorable effects due to his eminently ascetic beliefs, due
to the temperament of his condition, finally due to the space which very often
separate him from his master, the Russian serf, one must say, has not so much
to complain about as one might think. His actual situation, moreover, is only a
natural consequence of his previous situation.  It is not violence which reduced
him to servitude; it is the logical march of things, taking place at the basis of his
intimate life, his religious feelings and his nature. Do you want proof of that?
Look at the free man in Russia: no apparent difference between him 'and the
serf.  There is, I find in the resigned look of this latter one something more
dignified, more calm than the vague and worried look of the other one.
    In effect, byits origins as well as by its particular character Russian slavery
offers a unique factor in the world without precedent in history, without
anything analogous in the current state of human society. Constituted as it was
among the people of antiquity as it is in our day in the United States of America,
it has had no effect other than one which results naturally from this detestable
institution,namely misery of the slave, corruption of the master.  But the
influence of slavery in Russia is vast in another way: We just observed that no
matter whatkind of a slave the Russian serf is in the complete meaning of the
term, the Russian serf does not, however, bear a mark of it on his person. He is
not at all separated from the other classes of society either by customs, or public
opinion, ~r ~acial difference. In the house of his master the Russian serf shares
the daily work of the free man, in the fields he lives mixed up together with the
peasants of the free communes.  Everywhere he is mixed in with the free
subjects of~e;empire without any apparent distinction. Very well, it is precisely
in this biza~e9onfusion of the most opposite instincts of human nature that, in
our opinion, the source of the general degradation of the Russian people is to be
found.   It. is because of that that everything in Russia bears the brand of
servitude, customs, tendencies, instruction and yes, even liberty itself, insofar as
any liberty at all can exist in that country.2

Four Fragments1 [1854]

One must not forget one thing which is that, compared with Russia, all of
Europe is liberal - kings, cabinets and people. So how could you think that
Europe could experience real congenial feelings for Russia?   It's the natural
antagonism of light against darkness. At the present moment what adds to the
animosity of European people against Russia is that Russia is not content with
becoming part of the European political system; on the basis of her power, today
Russia claims to pose among civilized people as a country more civilized than the
others, by taking advantage of the calmness which she preserved during the
recent crisis which passed through Europe.
    Note that it is no longer just the Russian government alone which ex,resses
these claims, but the complete country.  Instead of the docile and submissive
disciples which we were before, look, we have now become masters of those
who were yesterday still our masters. An occasion was offered and Europe
seized it by the hair in order to humiliate us, that's all.
    In speaking about Russia people always believe that they are speaking about
a political power like others. But that is not so at all. Russia is an entire world
obeying the will, the caprice, the fantasy of only one man, whether he is called
Peter or Ivan, no matter, it's always an incarnation of arbitrariness. Russia is a
country which, contrary to all the laws of human societies, advances only
towards its own enslavement and those of the people who are neighbors. Hence
it is as much in its own interest and that of other nations that it would be useful
for Russia to pursue a new path.
  My intimate relations with the departed Archbishop gave me many occasions
to talk with him. He always spoke with respect about the eastern church and
hoped in the future to see it effect a rapprochement with the Anglican Church.
     I know, "health is not contagious, it is sickness which is, just as in the case
of truth and error." An orthodox Christian responds to me with "And hence
Christianity." Well, never would a serious Christian make this reply. Forthe
true Christian, Christianity is a separate thing, a purely divine thing which has
nothing in common with the order of human ideas. For the true Christian the
establishment as well as the propagation of Christianity is a perpetual miracle,
especially the propagation.  Even more so for the philosopher himself
Christianity is a separate thing, and he is obliged, if he wishes to define its
history, to explain a combination of unique events in the history of the world.

To Adolphe de Circourt, Jan. 15 (1854)1

My dear Sir,
   Soon two years will have passed since you addressed to me a letter full of
very interesting things.   It was at the time of your famous coup d'hat.2
Everyone wanted to read it, so much so that the letter ended by wandering into

--- PDF page 244 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                                241


the hands of persons who were curious to know your opinion about this
extraordinary event. Meanwhile the reaction ran its course; the world entered
into a new phase. Reflection was necessarily suspended in the presence of the
general defeat of all the ideas and all the principles which once had directed the
people.
   What could you want me to see about all that? Perhaps you find that the
world is still far from being returned to its normal state, that the actual moment is
no more propitious for a rational exchange of thought than the previous epoch
was. But no matter what, I finally must write to you if only in order not to pass
for an insolvent debtor in your eyes. Moreover, I recall very well that basis·of
your ideas.  I could thus pefectly rebind the broken thread of our conversation.
But I prefer to speak with you about something other than your country, whose
situation remains completely unintelligible to me since the day when the
burlesque parody of a great glory ravaged your liberties under the pretext of
rendering tranquility to you.  It will, however, always refer a little to you, in as
much as we find ourselves faced with the questions of the day; for it is the
privilege of your country to cause other people on earth to participate in your
domestic affairs. Hold on, you know the profound calmness which we enjoy,
our traditional devotion to the order of things established in our land. Well, we
have no less felt to a certain extent the contre-coup of your recent exploits. Our
patriotism has grown. Since time immemorial we were wholly satisfied with the
regime imposed upon our country by its history, by its geographical conditions
and by its religious beliefs. We are today a thousand times more satisfied. Our
submission to this tutelary regime has never been manifested with such a
complete confidence. The natural effect of all that is that those who govern us
have at this hour even more latitude than they had previollslyin following the
principles which always guided them. Hence they profit from it with a bit of
rare wisdom.  For example, you could figure out what wise and vigorous
methods we saw appear recently designed to preserve us from the fatal influence
of your ideas and your revolutions.
   The new limits imposed upon literary journalism by a hierarchical
censorship, coming from the very source of power, safeguards us from the fatal
license. of the press, one of the principal causes of your disasters.  The
pernicious extension of false knowledge, another source of evil, is paralyzed by
the new rule which reduces the number of students to be admitted to our schools
of higher ¢ucation to the number strictly necessary for service to the State. One
of the vastest institutions of public instruction in our capital has been
suppressM;3 it was an institution which parenthetically had furnished to the
country mor¢ than one eminent man, and, among others, the well known teacher
of the august heir to the throne, the dear poet whom we just 10st.4 The avid
curiosity of our young people to know a lot of useless things for the welfare of
the country is further repressed either by the very program of scholarly studies
or else by the difficulty of obtaining a permit to travel abroad. The measures
which were still in the past designed to prepare for the gradual emancipation of

--- PDF page 245 / 323 ---

242                                  FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS


our rural population and which nourished in the bosoms of this population some
dangerous fermentations, have definitively been abandoned.  Finally, and this I
find most important, all the upper administrative posts of the empire are filled
today with men most capable of preventing us from deviating from the good
road.
    The new nominations distinguish themselves especially by the most
fortunate choices. The minister of interior has just been conferred upon a man
who administered the interesting province, the cradle of Russia, where he was
for a long time the governor general with admirable energy, facing evil passion
to come out into the open in order to be delivered once to prosecution -by the
public prosecutor, placing the zeal of the dominant clergy in the service of the
State, and finally bringing about the coalescence for the great national unity, a
very dear wish of our new patriots, with complete success.5 The ministry of
public education,6 taken away from the man who more than any other sought to
develop in our young people the taste for a vain and ambitious science, has been
restored to the hands of a man of profound piety, an enemy of all knowledge,
who offers the surest guarantee to make our youth henceforth march in the
somber and submissive pathway from which they were misguided for too long a
time. Of the three ministries which control in our country the highest direction
of public mind, only the ministry of police or what replaces  it, has not
undergone any modification either in its chief or in the remainder of its excellent
personnel, but in return his sphere of activity has been considerably augmented.7
He has been invested with new prerogatives which are in tune with him: beyond
the factual surveillance of his natural jurisdiction, he has also been intrusted with
all the intelligence factors and they confirmed upon him the most efficacious
powers for suppressing all disorderly swings of thought.
   Among the high state functionaries newly called. to the special trust of the
government, one must also point out the eminent man ,charged with representing
power in our ancient city.s You are acquainted with Moscow as well as any of
us; you can appreciate his importance as a monument of the past, an expression
of the parent and a flag for the future; you will understand completely the
significance of this choice.  In effect, at the moment when all the European
democracies have just been shaken by the signal from P1lris, where can you find
a man more appropriate to this important past than the officer of fortune coming
out of this ranks of the lesser nobility from the provinces, placed very close to
the people, someone who speaks only their -language -and who judges things
only from their point of view, and who finally sees only with a jealous eye the
nobility which is mainly policed as the only social class to which European ideas
can find access. Hence the effect was instantaneous. You know that the old
liberalism of the previous regime, an absurd anamoly in a country piously
devoted to its princes, whose idiom expresses by the ~ame term altar and throne,
thanks be to God, has not existed among us for a long time, but unfortunately
something of it has remained in -the manners and in -the language of the people

--- PDF page 246 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                              243


who make up what is called good company. But in times like these even that
could present some trouble in the eyes of a foresighted minister.
    Hence it is soon seen that the salons of the new .governor .general, .once the
meeting place of a select society, are being emptied of their old habitues and are
being opened to a new society, very foreign to the other one, since the new
society is docile to the prudent exigencies of the day.  From that moment
onwards no other suitable liberty was known other than that which bears with it
a sweet quality of customs stripped of all formal prudery, a fond heritage of a
celebrated epoch in the modern history of France.9 I cannot tell you about all the
good which our young people extract from the new regime inaugurated in the
mayor's house. Nothing is more dangerous in the time in which we live than to
allow young minds to be handed over to those tastes which are too serious, in
which the sterile work of thought is fed all sorts of fragments of the imagination.
Very well, the kindly hospitality of the family of our governor general provides a
delightful remedy for this evil. The jovial familiarity of the mother and the
seductive manners of the daughter are carrying out a veritable revolution to the
profit of the good cause of changing the habits of our youth.

Letter to M. Zhikharev, (1854)1

      It is a great error, in my opinion, to imagine that the present crisis is only the
effect of an imprudent policy, on the one hand, and the jealousy of the great
powers and of the revolutionary spirit, on the other. Never was a regime less
ambitious than that of the emperor Nicholas, and if one can reproach the Russian
cabinet for anything it is rather for having been too prudent.  The personal
character of our emperor enters undoubtedly very much in the events which are
taking place today, but this character is not a political system, and the Russian
cabinet, in submitting t.o it, was n.ot making p.olitics; it was .obeying, that's all.2

Letter .of an Unkn.own  t.o an Unkn.own (Female) (between Oct.ober and
December, 1854)1

     N.o, a th.ousand times n.o, this is n.ot the way that we l.oved .our ·c.ountry in
.our youth. We wished her well-being, we desired s.ome go.od institutions f.or
her, we even s.ometimes went so far as to wish her a little liberty if that were
p.ossible. We th.ought .of her as great and p.owerful, full .of future, but we did n.ot
think either that she was the most powerful c.ountry in the w.orld, or that she was
the most fortunate. We were far from imagining that Russia might stand for
some sort of abstract principle containing the definitive solution of the social
problem or that Russia constituted in herself an entire separate world, a direct
and legitimllte inheritor of the glorious eastern empire with its titles and virtues.2
Nor did we believe that Russia had a special mission to absorb in herself all
Slavic people and thereby to bring about the regeneration of the human species.
We especially did not think that Europe was on the point of receeding into

--- PDF page 247 / 323 ---

244                                 FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS

barbarism and that we had been charged with saving civilization with some
scraps of that same civlization which had served once to snatch us from our
ancient torpor.
  We treated Europe with civility, even sometimes with respect; ·forwe ·know
that Europe had taught us many .things, .among .others, .our .own .history. When it
happened by chance for us to triumph over her, as Peter the Great did, we said,
it is to you Gentlemen that we owe it. And so then one fine day it happened that
we arrived in Paris and that we were received kindly, forgetting for a moment
that we were basically only young newcomers and that we had yet contributed
nothing to the common fund of the nations, not even a poor little solar system as
the Poles our subjects had,3 not even a miserable algebra, as those Arab infidels
whose absurd and barbarous religion we combat today. We were treated well,
because we were found to have some manners of well educated people, because
we were polite and modest, as newcomers were supposed to be, without any
claim to public esteem other than our advantageous size. You have changed all
that, so be it, but please allow me to love my country in the manner of Peter the
Great, Catherine and Alexander. The time is not far off, I hope, when that
patriotism will perhaps be found to be worth more than another.
    Note that every government, aside from its particular tendencies, possesses
the instincts of its nature, as an animated and intelligent force, destined to live
and to act:  for example, the government senses or does not sense itself to be
supported by its governed. But the Russian government knew itself this time to
be in perfect accord with the general will of the country. Hence, in great part,
the fatal blunderings of its politics which took place during the present crises.
Who does not realize that the reaction which is claimed to be national has reached
the state of veritable monomania among our new scholars.  It was .no longer .a
matter, as before, of the prosperity of the country, nor of civilization, nor of any
kind of progress - it was enough just to be Russian. This title alone contained all
the goods imaginable, including salvation.  They believed that they had
discovered at the basis of our rich nature all kinds of marvelous elements,
previously ignored by the universe. They began to repudiate all the serious and
fruitful ideas that Europe had taught us. They claimed to inaugurate on the soil
of Russia an entire new moral order which relegated us to some Christian East or
other, invented for our particular usage, without realizing in the least that in
isolating overselves morally from European people we were also isolating
ourselves politically, that once our fraternity with the great European family was
broken, none of those people would lend us a hand at the hour of danger.
Finally the. most audacious of the devotees of the new national school did not
hesitate to salute in the fight in which we found ourselves engaged, the
realization of their retrospective utopias, the dawn of our return to the tutelary
regime abjured by our fathers in the person of Peter the Great.
   Too unlettered, too frivolous to evaluate or even to decipher these learned
hallucinations,  the. government scarcely approved of them at all, I know;
sometimes the goverruilent even haphazardly launched a brutal boot at the most

--- PDF page 248 / 323 ---

FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS                                              245


advanced and least prudent of the blessed gang,4 but the government was no less
convinced that on the day when it threw down the gauntlet before the godless
and decayed West, the congenial feelings of all the new patriots, who take their
unfinished studies, their inarticulate wishes and their vague hopes for the true
national policy, would not fail the government any more than the docile
enthusiasm of the crowd, always a party to all the patriotic dreams told in the
customary banal idiom during similar occurrences. And look how one fine day
the advance forces of Europe were in the Crimea.

Letter to Michael Zhikharev, November 3 (1855)1

   My dear friend, I was going to write to you at the moment when your letter
arrived.  Firstly I am going to respond to your lines, then you wi11leam of the
little posthumous service which  I am asking of you, posthumous with
relationship to me, of course.  I once again thank you for your friendship and I
once again admit that I scarcely anticipated it at all.  It is, I know, one of my
vices that I take certain things too seriously, but how can one find the means of
correcting oneself when one is in the midst of traversing the second half century
and when one approaches the end of the course for the most serious matter in the
world? In any case I am delighted to find you also such a good child.  It seems
to me that this title is better for you than that of having been aborted, which you
may like, I believe, to adorn yourself with. What do you think -about it? Note
that these abortions are almost always in our country the effect of the social
conditions in which we are enclosed in such a way that the most honest
aspirations of the most legitimate ambition are basically only gaucheries. All that
you say, moreover, is perfect. There is only one thing in your letter that I do not
understand, it is your illusion concerning my position.  In any case I can only
rejoice about it, since that evades for you the disappointment of sensing that you
are assisting at this dismal catastrophe which, after all, could not be a matter·of
indifference to you.  I do not have any desire to repeat to you what I was saying
in this context the other day, but I cannot refrain from warning you that it is
completely useless for you to delude yourself with the hope of seeing things take
up their ordinary course again.
       {\. new disaster has just taken away yet another sheet anchor from me.
Tomprrow or after tomorrow all will be said, and I will have but to uncover my
heaq and await the lightening which is destined to annihilate me. But since
neit4er you nor I possess the lightening rod which could deter it, let's not talk
aboqt it any more and allow me to let you know what I was going to tell you in
takirig up my pen.
    Yet one more word about your letter. My dear friend, it seems that among
your "kachirian riches", such as, for example, the brilliant hoarfrost of your
forest of high trees which you look forward to with such impatience, and among
other similar things, you lack some thing which is very useful in certain
circumstances of life, namely a dictionary, for you would have directly

--- PDF page 249 / 323 ---

246                                 FRAGMENTS AND DIVERSE THOUGHTS

discovered after the word "delices" the words S. F., which is to say feminine
substantive. More familiarized with the French writing you would besides have,
without any other indication, found it impossible to couple the masculine with
this word, which is moreover only written in the plural.  I tell you all this
because you seem to attach a kind of importance to that which you may regard, I
suppose, as a fault of language, and I smile at the idea that after my death you
will find yourself furnished with an entire collection -of -dictionaries -of all types
and formats, which will naturally give you the means of rectifying some of your
grammatical beliefs.2
   So here is what this is all about: At this hour I am occupying myself with
putting a little order in my papers, and I hasten as fast as I can to fulfill my task
before the supreme day which will put an end to all my earthly preoccupations.
   God knows if I shall succeed, for I just discovered that I have scribbled on
more paper than I thought. But, in any case, I beg you, my friend, to come to
Moscow at my first appeal to take possession of my poor scraps of paper before
xxx3 lays hands on -them. -0n the -edge of the -blessed abyss -which is destined to
swallow me up, I naturally cannot nourish myself on vain illusions, but it is
impossible for me not to admit that among this jumble there are some pages
which merit to be preserved, if only as pieces justifying a little chapter in the
history of our time. You have given me, my friend, so many proofs of your
affection and you yourself have always had such an interest in my writings that I
could not doubt your eagerness to fulfill my wish. Nonetheless I have need of a
word from you to put my mind at ease about this matter, and I beg you not to
refuse me.
   You asked me, I believe, if I have seen xxx.4 I have seen him a couple of
times at his place, and !found him as interesting and as good to see as before,
but I have not assisted, even though he invited me there, at those famous student
exercises of which the journal rendered so a grotesque account.
     I am completely touched by the kindly memory on your father's part. Tell
him please that I also preserve the memory of his frank and cordial friendship,
so different from this official friendship which, far from doing good to one's
heart, brings it some hard blows, a kind of torture which is all the more cruel as
one must suffer it in silence. So may he accept with my last farewells, the
expression of my lively gratitude for his past and present congenial feelings as
well as his wishes, however ineffectual they are, for an impossible future.  I am
certain that God guards for him yet a few days of serene happiness and pure joy,
for his heart tells me that he merits them.
   Good day, my dear friend. And you too undoubtedly will see more than
one prosperous and radiant day.  Satisfaction of the heart and of the mind will
not be lacking to you. I am certain of it, because you too have what merits them.
But that will only come at the price of some lessons which -experience will
charge itself to give to you. God grant that those lessons will not be too severe -
this is one of my dearest wishes.
   Remember me please to my cousin.5
