   COMMENTARIES AND NOTES TO THE APOLOGIA OF A MADMAN

1.   I Corinthians 13:4-7:  "Charity is patient; charity does not envy, is not
pretentious, is not puffed up, is not ambitious, is not self-seeking, is not provoked;
thinks no evil, does not rejoice over wickedness, but rejoices with the truth; bears
all things, believes all things, endures all things."
2. This refers to the publication of "The First Philosophical Letter: and the reaction
against it.
3. The religious and political writer Hugues Felicite Robert de Lamennais (1782-
1854). His works Essai sur l'indifjerence en matiere de religion and Defense de
I'Essai sur l'indifference en matiere de religion were both found in Chaadaev's
private library; see Shakhovskoi, Literaturnoe Nasledstvo, Vol. 22-24, (Moscow,
1935), p. 71. Shakhovskoi also states that in a text of the Apologia of a Madman
which was in the hands of Chernyshevskii the phrase "a great writer of our time"
was footnoted specifically as referring to Lamennais.
      The first two parts of Lamennais's Essai sur l'indifference en matiere de
religion are important to an understanding of Chaadaev's own position. Chaadaev
had underlined several words in the book and had made marginal notes, as well.
The final note is dated December 1, 1829, and that is the very date at the end of
"The First Philosophical Letter."  Shakhovskoi holds that the influence ·of
Lamennais upon Chaadaev was much stronger than that of Ballanche, de Bonald,
or de Maistre (p. 71).
      Lammennais wrote several essays in Chateaubriand's Conservateur from
1817 to 1823, but the monarchical tendencies of Villele finally alienated him from
that group. During 1830 and 1831 Lamennais published his liberal Catholic journal
L'Avenir with the dedication Dieu and Liberte. In 1831 he was called to Rome by
Gregory XVI.  In this case the pope was strongly influenced by temporal
authorities; he suppressed Lamennais and his movement because of the dangerously
"liberal" tendencies within L'Avenir. Much to Lamennais's own surprise, he
received an extremely cold reception in Rome. In 1832 the encyclical Mirari Vos
denounced Lamennais's L'Avenir. Lamennais replied with his Les Paroles d'un
croyant which definitely marked his break with Rome. Gregory answered with
another encyclical, Singulari Nos, in 1834.  Lamennais continued to write
numerous articles and books.  He actively supported the revolution in 1848.
Chaadaev wrote the Apologia of a Madman in 1837; but .by .that time .hehad
apparently become disenchanted with Lamennais's tendency to identify God's voice
with the popular, democratic movement in France.
4. Chaadaev detected a Russian tendency to have contact with western Europe and
to submit to that cultural influence as early as the middle of the sixteenth century.
5. Peter the Great.
6. This is aimed not only at some of those later intellectuals known as Slavophiles
but also at the group known as the "Official Nationalists," especially the coterie
around Pogodin, Polevoi, Gretch, Weltmann, Burachek, and Shevyrev.  In
general, Chaadaev is attacking all the nationalistic, isolationist groups in Russia.
7. See the above note.
8.  Bayer, Schiller, Miller, and especially Schloezer.  Gerhard Friederic Muller
(1705-1783), the successor to Gotlieb Bayer, was a prodigious researcher in
Russia. He ransacked archives in Siberia, found source materials on the Time of

                                       275

--- PDF page 279 / 323 ---

276                                                APOLOGIA OF A MADMAN


the Troubles, for example, charters issued by Boris Godunov and Vasili Shuiskii.
Muller's book Sammlung russischer Geschichte (St. Petersburg, 1732) aroused the
curiosity of the famous German historian August-Ludwig von Schloezer (1735-
1790).  Schloezer came to St. Petersburg in 1761 as a professor and became a
member of the Academy of Sciences. There he worked on Russian chronicles. He
left Russia in 1776 to become professor of history at the University of Gottingen.
His famous Probe russischer Annalen (Bremen and Gottingen, 1768) was the first
effort at applying a critical method to the study of Russian chronicles.
9. The noted Russian historian Nikolai Karamzin (1776-1826), whom Pushkin
called "the Columbus of Russian history," made Russian history popular and
appealing to his literary generation  with his famous work, The History of the
Russian State (S1. Petersburg, 1816-1826).  Chaadaev had known Karamzin
personally since his days in St. Petersburg in 1816.
10. Probably the writings of Nikolai Polevoi (1796-1846) and Mikhail Pogodin
(1800-1875).  Polevoi attempted to write a national history, A History of the
Russian People in six volumes from 1830 to 1833 (an obvious antithesis to
Karamzin's History of the Russian State).  Although Polevoi had -considerable
journalistic talent, he possessed none of the literary talents or refinements of a
Karamzin.  For this reason, Chaadaev considered Polevoi's work to be second-
rate.  Polevoi's task appears in reality to have been beyond his basic intellectual
capabilities. Mikhail Pogodin became a lecturer at Moscow University in 1835 in
place of his teacher, M. T. Kachenovski, who had taught Chaadaev.  Pogodin
edited the Moscow Messenger from 1827 to 1830. In general, his popular writings
contained a highly nationalistic interpretation of Russian history and later he himself
became one of the staunchest supporters of tsarist imperialism.
11. The Time ofthe Troubles, from 1605 to 1613. The last male heir of the original
Riurik dynasty, Fedor Ivanovich, died in 1598. Boris Godunov became tsar upon
election by the Zemskii Sobor, but he was faced with years of famine and pestilence
on the land, plus a contender for the throne, the so-called False Dmitri.  This
pretender to the throne claimed that one of the sons of Ivan IV, Dmitri, had not died
in 1591 and that he was the true Dmitri, rightful heir to the throne. With the help of
Polish soldiers Dmitri seized the throne in 1605 to reign until 1606. The boyar,
Vasili Shuisky, then seized the throne and held onto it until 1610. 1611 was the
nadir of Russian power; the Poles occupied the Kremlin, the Swedes invaded
northern Russia, and several other pretenders to the throne arose throughout
Russia. A nationalist movement, inspired by the orthodox monks at Trinity
monastery and supported by the lesser gentry and lower classes, led by Pozharski
and Minin, threw out the foreign intruders. In 1613 Mikhail Romanov was elected
tsar to begin the long Romanov dynasty in Russia. Chaadaev considered the Time
of the Troubles to be an extremely important period in Russian history; in his mind,
it was one of the rare times in which the "people," tile Russian nation, acted as a
real force in history.
12. The Philosophical Letters Addressed to a Lady.
13. "The First Philosophical Letter."
14.  "The First Philosophical Letter."
15. Nikolai Gogo1's play Revizor (The Inspector General). In 1824, scenes 7, 8,
9, and 10 were presented in St. Petersburg; on June 16/28, 1830, parts of it were
played again, then the third act was performed in Moscow. Finally most of the play
with a few lines cut out was performed in Moscow.  Here Chaadaev might be

--- PDF page 280 / 323 ---

COMMENTARIES AND NOTES                                                            277


referring specifically to the performance in Moscow on December 2/14, 1835,
which he could have attended. The "almost tragic emotion" resulted from Gogol's
satire of the corruption and bribery which existed in Russian provincial life.

COMMENTARIES AND NOTES TO FRAGMENTS, ARTICLES, AND
OTHER LETTERS

   Most of the following translations are based on manuscripts from the Pypin
Archives (no. 250) or from the Shakhovskoi Archives (no. 334), both of which
are housed in the Manuscript Division of the Institute of Russian Literature
(Pushkin House), Academy of Sciences, Leningrad. Other manuscripts come
from the Manuscript Division of the Lenin Library, Moscow, the Section of the
Central Archives of the Government at Leningrad; the Military and Historical
Archives, and the Historical Museum of Moscow; the originals of the letters to
Ivan Gagarin are at the Bibliotheque Slave, Centre des Etudes Russes at
Meudon, France.  The latest scholarly edition of these is  by Raymond T.
McNally, Francois Rouleau, and Richard Tempest entitled Pierre Tchaadaev,
Oeuvres inedites ou rares. Centre d'Etudes Russes, France, 1990.

    Abbreviations
    V. E. =Vestnik Evropy
    R. A. =Russkii Archiv
    Gershenzon edition = M. O. Gershenzon, Sochinenii i Pis'ma Chaadaeva, 2
Vols. Moscow, 1913-1914.
    Gagarin edition =I. S. Gagarin, ed. Oeuvres choisies de Pierre Tchaadaev,
Paris-Leipzig, 1962.
  MRT edition = Raymond McNally, Fran~ois Rouleau, and Richard
Tempest, eds. Oeuvres inedites ou rares de Pierre Tchaadaev, Centre des Etudes
Russes, Meudon, France,  1990.
    Rouleau edition = ·Fran~ois Rouleau, ed., Pierre Tchaadaev Lettres
philosophiques, Paris, 1970.
    Tarasov edition = Boris Tarasov, ed., P. la. Chaadaev, StatU i Pis'ma,
Moscow, 1987.

COMMENTARIES AND NOTES                                                        279


Fragments and Diverse Thoughts [1828-1856]

 1. Copy by Michael Zhikharev, Pypin Archive 250-549 (old no. 3491) and 250-
293 (old nos. 3489-3491) published by Tsuguo Togawa in Slavic Studies
XXXIX, University of Toronto 1978, republished in Slavic Studies no. 23,
University of Hokkaido, 1979, 1-52. A Russian translation was published in
Voprosy filosofii, no. I, 1986; six of the Fragments were published -in Russian
translation at the end of an article by Chaadaev in "Something from the
Correspondence of XXX" Telescope, no. 11 [1832], reproduced in the
Gershenzon edition, vol. II, 24-25, MRT edition 23-65.
    In his first volume Gershenzon published a portion of the "Fragments and
Diverse Thoughts" including fragments from the Telescope under the title
"Otryvki" in yet another Russian translation from a copy by A. P. Elagina
(Gershenzon edition I, 143-160). At the end of his publication of Chaadaev's
article for L'Univers, Shakhovskoy placed his translation of three fragments
(Zveni'ia, vol. III, M-L 1935,387-389).
    Recently Shakhovskoy's translation was only partially published in Voprosy
filosofii,  I, January 1986, 125-140 along with his translation of Chaadaev's
"Aphorisms and Remarks" (121-124).  In his book Stat'i i pis'ma Chaadaeva
(Moscow, 1987) Tarasov only republished the Elagin translation from the
Gershenzon edition and Shakhovskoy's translation which had already appeared
in Voprosy filosofii(Jan. 1986) including "Aphorisms and Remarks" under the
title "Otryvki i Aforizmi." The Zhikharev translation which includes the entire
"Fragments and Diverse Thoughts" was published in the journal Simvol, no. 15,
1986, 215-273. The "Fragments" represent a kind of intellectual diary which
Chaadaev kept from his return from his European trip up to his death.  It can be
compared  in importance,  as Zhikharev  states with  the works of "La
Rochefoucault, Pascal, and the first Napoleon" (Zhikharev "Chaadaev" V. E. II,
38).
    The Zhikharev copy is divided into thirteen sections which are not in
chronological order. Sections 1 and 2 deal with a critique of the philosophies of
Schelling and Fichte. Section 9 contains a fragment in which Chaadaev refers to
his meeting with the Methodist missionary Charles Cook "five years ago," i.e.,
in January of the year 1825 when Chaadaev met Cook in Florence (See the
Gershertzon edition,  I, 69-70).  All of section no. 12 is a translation of
paragraphs in section 9, aside from the final paragraph, so we include only that
final paragraph here. Section 13 consists of two aphorisms in Russian; the first
about the iminent war with France and England over the Eastern Question.
Sections 4 and 5 from a unity of thought with ideas analogms to those in the
First Philosopnical Letter, the Apologia of a Madman, the letter to Count Circout
dated 1846 (bershenzon edition  I, 268-275) and Chaadaev's article for
L'Univers.  Section 6 contains a French translation of a critique of the Polish
Hegelian Ciedzkowski Prolegomena to Historiosophy, written by the German
left Hegelian K. L. Michelet. We have not translated that passage here.  In
section 7 Chaadaev refers to the Crimean War; this section and also section 8

--- PDF page 283 / 323 ---

 280                               FRAGMENTS, ARTICLES AND OTHER LETTERS


contain passages which recall that of "A letter from an unknown (male) to an
unknown (female)" written in 1854. Sections 10 and 11 from a whole with an
analysis of the philosophical systems of Fichte and Hegel, as well as two
fragments about the Crimean War. Section 3 contains a fragment repeated in a
paragraph in Chaadaev's article for L'Univers (See Cahiers du monde russe et
sovietique .. XV, 3-4, 413). The fragments from the Pypin Archive 250-549
were written in 1853; the one before the penulimate one is at the end of section
 12; the penullimate one and the last one form section 13.
    In preparing the Fragments for publication Shakhovskoi tried to number
them and put them in chronological order and that is the way they were recently
published in Voprosy filosofii.  But, though one can date some passages with
great accuracy, dating of all of them accurately is impossible. Such an attempt
also confuses the way in which Zhikharev preserves the Fragments.  (For a
recent provocative study of Chaadaev's general thought see Andrzej Walicki,
The Slavophile Controversy, Oxford University Press, 1975,83-117 and by the
same author, A History of Russian Thought, Stanford University Press 1979,
81-91.)
2. Friedrich Wilhelm Joseph von Schelling (1775-1854), German philosopher,
idealist and "father of existentialism."  Chaadaev regarded Schelling as the
greatest philosopher of his day and Schelling expressed admiration for Chaadaev
as "one of the most remarkable men not only in Russia but in Europe." They
had met in 1825 in Karlsbad. Chaadaev wrote to Schelling in 1833 and again in
1842. In his Fifth Philosophical Letter Chaadaev referred to Schellingism as "a
kind of subtle platonism."
    Schelling'S first period known as subjective idealism (1795-1796) is when
he slowly freed himself from the notions of Fichte. The second period (1797-
1803) was the one of Naturphilosophie when he worked out his most well-
known and influential views.  The third period (1803-1809) the so-called
philosophy of "triumph" when Naturphilosophie and epistomology were joined
together.  The fourth and final period (1809-1854) was that of "positive
philosophy" an attempt to join historical mythology and religion, a kind of
historical philosophy of revelation. Chaadaev initial discussions with Schelling
at Karlsbad were about Schelling's then new "positive philosophy."
3.  Benedict Spinoza (1632-1679) Dutch philosopher of Sephardic Portugese
Jewish origins who saw God as iminent in the universe. He was accused of
being a pantheist and excommunicated from the Jewish community. He rejected
personal immortality and any transcendental difference between good and evil.
4.  Chaadaev characterized pantheism in his Fifth Philosophical Letter as a
"deadly doctrine."                                                         ,
5. Johann Gotlieb Fichte (1762-1814) German idealist philosopher. In his Fifth
Philosophical Letter Chaadaev called Fichte's system "the philosophy of the
omnipotence of the human ego," a precursor of Schelling's idealism.
6.  Friedrich Heinrich Jacobi (1743-1819), German philospher of "feeling and
faith" who attacked the Kantian tradition and Spinoza's -rationalism,and the

--- PDF page 284 / 323 ---

COMMENTARIES AND NOTES                                                            281


system of Fichte, Schelling and Hegel for their tendencies towards -pantheism
and "nihilism."
7. Jacobi - See previous note no. 6 above.
8. Peter Abelard (1079-1142) medieval philosopher and poet, lover of Heloise,
attacked nominalism in favor of conceptualism.
9. A1cuin (735-804) born in York, England, theologian and man ofletters who
was an important teacher during the "Carolingian Renaissance" under
Charlemagne.
10.  Cicero (106-44 B. C.) Roman orator and politician. Chaadaev often cited
his words.
11. A Russian translation of this paragraph appeared in Zven'ia, ill-N, 388.
12. M. V., perhaps a reference to the infamous homosexual Philip Vigel, a
particular enemy of Chaadaev's and a chauvinist. Vigel was the administrator of
the Department of Foreign Religions.
 13. Compare with the words in the Gershenzon edition, I, 200 and I, 230.
 14. See the Gershenzon edition, I, pp. 197 and 220.
15. Peter the Great. See the Gershenzon edition 1,87.

25. Epistle to the Romans, 13.1. One of Chaadaev's main ideas was the lack of
any tradition of rule by law in Russia.
26. See the Letter to Circourt, Gershenzon edition, I, 272-273.
27. See Chaadaev's article for L'Univers for similar words.
28. The Time of Troubles 1605-1613 in Russia, Civil war, famine and foreign
intervention by the Poles and the Swedes, dynastic crisis with five claimants to

29. See Apologia of a Madman (Rouleau edition, 205) and Chaadaev;'s article for

Rouleau edition 52).
31. Zhikharev records that in discussions with the Slavophiles Chaadaev held
that Peter the Great did not "steal away" the Russian nationality. No individual
is powerful enough to be able to overturn the character of the Russian people

especially within one generation,  See V.E. The Messenger of Europe.. IX,

32. Chaadaev's idea was that "it is in expanding abroad ... that Christianity was
developed in the West.
33. Protestantism. In the Sixth Philosophical Letter Chaadaev condemned the
Reformation as "this deadly event" which had ruptured European unity (Rouleau

34. See Apology of a Madman (Rouleau edition, 204).
35.  Patriarch Nikon who was dismissed and exiled by Tsar Alexis after the

36. See "The First Philosophical Letter" (Rouleau edition, 52).
37. Victor Cousin (1792-1867) French founder of the spiritual or eclectic school,

38.  Theodore Simon Jouffroy (1796-1842) French philosopher of common

39. Count August Cieszkowski (1814-1894) Polish Hegelian philosopher. See
Walicki, The Slavophile Controversy, 97. "Michelet of Berlin" is Karl-Ludwig

45. Georg Wilhelm Hegel (1770-1831) famous German philosopher of absolute
idealism and the dialectical system of thought. See Gershenzon I, 245.
46. A play on words: in Russian "the third act" and "the third section," i.e., the
secret police are written the same way.
47. Perhaps Innokentii (1800-1857) theologian and preacher named archbishop
of Kherson and Taurus in 1848.

55.   See, for example, Chaadaev's ideas about Moses in "The Seventh

56. John VI.46. The Gospel of St. John, especially the section known as the

57. See Chaadaev's "Eighth Philosophical Letter."
58. Epicurus (341-270) B. D.) Athenian philospher who considered pleasure as
the greatest good, i.e., the practice of virtue.  In his "Seventh Philosophical
Letter" Chaadaev evaluates Epicureanism positively. (See Rouleau edition 170-
171.)
59. Zeno of Citium (333-262 B. C.) founder of the Stoic school. Chaadaev in
his "Seventh Philosophical Letter" judged stoicism as "arrogant, hard and
impractical." (See Rouleau edition 170.)
60. Matthew VI.33. The theme "the kingdom of God on earth" is at the core of
Chaadaev's thought.  (See Rouleau edition 45-46, 58, 62, 89, 148, 186, 199,
232.)
61.  St. Luke II, 40 and 46.
62.  Bathold Georg Niebuhr (1776-1831) German historian, author of The
History of Rome published during 1827-1828 in which he treated the sources
scientifically and analyzed the legends critically.
 63. Fran90is Guizot (1787-1874) French Romantic historian and conservative
politician.  Almost all of Guizot's works were in Chaadaev's library.  In his
"Sixth Philosophical Letter"Chaadaev ·praised ·Guizot'scontributions to the
philosophy of history. (See Rouleau edition, 150.)
 64. Nicholas Mihailovich Karamzin (1766-1826) Russian historian and man of
letters representing the so-called sentimental schoo1.Chaadaevfirst met the poet
Pushkin in Karamzin's home.
 65. "Many are the poets who have never penn'd
        Their inspiration, and perchance the best:
       They felt, and loved, and died, and would not lend
        Their thoughts to meaner beings ... "
      From The Prophecy of Dante Canto IV.
 66. Emanuel Swedenborg (1688-1772) Swedish mystic who considered that he
had made contact with spirits and angels. In .his works he described the .cities,
marriages, and customs of angels.  See  Quenet, 52; and Raymond McNally

68. See "The Third Philosophical Letter" Rouleau edition, 91.
69. Charles Bonnet (1720-1793) Swiss naturalist and religious cosmologist who
thought that all possible beings had been created at the same time; they all existed
in germinal form and came to life upon the death of other organisms.  See
Quenet, 172-174 and McNally Chaadayev and His Friends, 189.

70. William Paley (1743-1805) English theologian and moral philosopher who
considered that activity is right if it has a useful goal. He was a forerunner of

78. Rene Descartes (1596-1650) famous French philosopher and mathematician,

84. Gaspard de Coligny (1519-1572) French admiral, leader of the Huguenots.

86. Albrecht von Wallenstein (1583-1634) Friedlandian hero, commander of the

87.  Fran<;ois Michel Louvois (1639-1691) French Minister of War under Louis

the Huguenots.
88.  In 1689 Peter the Great hanged 200 of the treacherous strel'tsy (palace
guards) from wood scaffolding around the Kremlin walls.

Letter to Alexander Pushkin, September 18 (1831)
 1. Published first in Russkii Arkhiv 1881, Vol. I, 431-435, republished in the
Gershenzon edition (1,162-166).
2. Chaadaev gave a version of "The Philosophical Letters," probably Letter VI
which Alexander Pushkin, Russia's greatest poet, took with him to Moscow in
May 1831 to convince French publisher Bellizard to publish it.  Evidently,
Pushkin wrote on July 6, 1831 to Chaadaev, the poet saw neither Bludov nor
Bellizard because of the cholera quarantine and asked whether Chaadaev wanted
the manuscript back.  In August Pushkin tried unsuccessfully to send the
manuscript back to Chaadaev by mail.
3. The July revolution of 1830 in France when Louis Philippe replaced Charles
X as king.
4. The poet Vasilii Zhukovskii.
5. King Charles X of France who tried unsuccessfully to roll back the wheel of
history and restore royal absolutism.
6. The movement led by Lamennais.
7. Count [M. Lamarck] one of the radical leaders of the opposition in 1830-31.
8. Pushkin's poem "To the Slanderers of Russia."  The other poem which
Chaadaev refers to is "The Anniversary of Borodino."  Both poems were
published with Zhukhovskii's "Old Song" at the end of August and beginning of
September in 1831 in a separate brochure under the title Upon the Seizure of
Warsaw. Three Poems oiV. Zhukhovskii and A. Pushkin.

Letter to Schelling, 1832
1. First published by M. M. Longinov in Russkii Vestnik, November 1862, 157
ff. republished in the Gershenzon edition (I, 167-170). The letter was sent to
Schelling, the famous German philosopher via A. I. Turgenev, who was then
abroad. Chaadaev usually worked for a long time on his letters.  In this case
though the letter is dated 1832, he actually sent the letter only in April, 1833.
2. Chaadaev did meet Schelling at Karlsbad in 1825 and evidently made a very
positive impression on that German philosopher.
3. Victor Cousin (1792-1867) French writer and philosopher ofthe "spiritualist"
school based on the ideas of the Scottish philosopher Thomas Reid (1710-1796)
whose "common sense" philosophy accepted the external world as existing by
intuition.

Letter to A.  I. Turgenev, April 20 (1833)
1. Turgenev archive 2680, first published in the Gershenzon edition (I, 170-
173).  Alexander Ivanovich Turgenev, born in 1784 into the family of Ivan
Petrovich Turgenev.  Studied at Moscow University boarding school and in
1800 went to work for the archives of Foreign Affairs in Moscow, attended
G6ttingen University for two years; he held various government jobs.
Alexander was a friend of Pushkin, Viazemskii, and Chaadaev. He spent the
years from 1825 to 1845 mostly in Europe with some short return trips to his
homeland.  He knew  Goethe, Humboldt, Walter  Scott,  Lamartine,

--- PDF page 290 / 323 ---

COMMENTARIES AND NOTES                                                        287


Chateaubriand, Hugo, and Schelling. Chaadaev enjoyed writing to Alexander
Turgenev about philosophical topics.
2. Turgenev's cousin - A. I. Nefedeva (1782-1857).
3. Madame Bravoura - Italian-born beauty living in Moscow, a friend of A. I.
Turgenev, Chaadayev, Viazemskii.
4.  Pashkovy - General A. V. Pashkov and his wife E. P., born Kindiakova.
Both General A. V. Pashkov and General P. V. Kindiakov were friends whom
both Chaadaev and Turgenevoften -visited.
5. Turgenev showed this letter to the French philosopher Ballanche.

Letter to A.  I. Turgenev, May 1 (1835)
 1. Turgenev archive 2684, first published in the Gargarin edition Oeuvres
Choisies (ParisI862), 170-174, republished in the Gershenzon edition (I, 179-
182).
2. "The writing about which you speak" - probably an early version of some of
Chaadaev's "Philosophical Letters" which he was in the midst of revising and
which A. I. Turgenev undertook to insert in one of the French reviews.
3. Countess Rozaliia Aleksandrovna Rzhevskaia (1791-1865) a very beautiful,
cultured; and zealous Catholic, from1831 onwards a widow.
4. Madame Ekaterina Nikolaevna Orlov, wife of Michael Orlov and daughter of
N. N. Raevskaia.
5. Concerning Bravoura, see Letter of April 20, (1833) to A. I. Turgenev.
6. Avdotiia Petrovna Elagina - mother of the Kireevskii brothers; in her noted
salon Chaadaev was a constant guest.
7.  Sophia Sergeevna Meshcherskaia (1775-1848), poetess known for her
projects to disseminate the Bible among the common Russian people.
8. Three Tales by N. Ph. Pavlov came out at the end of 1834 or at the beginning
of 1835.
9. Kukolnik - author of the play Prince Skopin Shuiskii, a drama in five acts.
10. The article and the Zhukovskii letter appeared in the "Bibliotheka dlia
chteniia" (Vol. 8, Mar.-Apr. 1835), the article entitled "German Philosophy"
was not signed.
I1.Philarete Chasles (1799-1873) - French writer.
12. Abbot Lacordaire (1801-1870) well-known Paris church preacher in the
30's.
13.  Sophia Petrovna Svechin (1782-1857) - a noted Russian Catholic, then
living in Paris, one of the pillars of French ultramontanism; A. I. Turgenev was
a close friend of hers.
14. On Meshcherskaia, supra.

Letter to A.  I. Turgenev (1835)
1.  Turgenev archive 2684, first published in the Gagarin edition, 174-187,
republished in the Gershenzon edition (I, 183-190).
2. Chaadaev speaks about the second volume of "Der Salon" by Heinrich Heine,
published in January, 1835. Chaadaev must have known this book already in its

original form:  the main part was published in Revue des Deux Mondes 1834 in
the form of three articles, I. The Religious Revolution and Martin Luther, II.
Les Precursers of the Philosophical Revolution, Spinoza and Precursors
Lessing,  III.  The Philosophical Revolution, Kant, Fichte,Schelling.  See
Heinrich Heine's Siimtliche Werke herausg. von Prof. Ernst Elster. Leipzig and
Wien. Bibliograph.Institut Band IV, Einleitung. 145.
3. The play of Zagoskin "The Dissatisfied" was first presented in Moscow on
December 2,1835.
4. Michael Orlov (1788-1842) friend of Chaadaev and of Pushkin, leader in the
Union of Welfare and the Decembrist uprising.
5. Uvarov, Count Sergei Semonovich, Minister of National Education.
6.  Saint Marc Girardin (1801-1873) French writer, later political activist,
professor of literature at the Sorbonne.
7. Antoine Cesar Becquerel (1788-1878) French physicist, noted for his own
excellent works on elyctrical theory and magnetics; his new book, about which
Chaadaev writes was - Traite de l'electricite et du magnetisme, the flrst volume
came out in 1834, and thtp 7th, the last - in 1840.
8.  Kupfer - Adolph Yakolevich Kupfer (1799-1865) physicist, since 1828 a
member of the Russian Academy of Science, Chaadaev probably has in mind
Kupfer's work The Manuel for Magnetic  Activity and Meteorlogical
Observations, 'Saint Petersburg, 1835.
9. Simonov, Ivan Mikhailovich (1794-1855) professor of astronomy at Kazan,
who worked on the study of magnetic declinations.  In 1823-24 Simonov and
Kupfer lived in Paris, where they were ordered to buy physics instruments;
Chaadaev might have met them there.
10. Spurzheim, Johann-Kaspar (1776-1832) noted phrenologist.
11. Jouffroi, Theodor Simon (1796-1842) a student of Cousin, member of the
"spiritualist" school of the Scottish philosopher Reid, professor of philosophy in
the College de France.
12. Pallas, Peter Simon (1741-1811) well-known traveller and naturalist.
13. Fischer, Johann-Eberhard (1697-1771) former Rector of the Academic High
School in Petersburg in 1739, he was ordered to go to Siberia to do research.
14. Young Gagarin- Prince Ivan Sergeevich Gagarin (1814-1882) then 21 years
of age. From 1833 to1835 he had served at the Russian mission in Munich,
where Schelling lived. Later in his own preface to Oeuvres choisies de Pierre
TchadaaiefGagarin wrote:  "In 1833 at Munich, the celebrated Schelling spoke
to me of him (Chaadaev), as of one of the most remarkable men whom he had
met. When I was in Moscow in 1835, I hurried to put myself in contact with
him ... "
15. Pierre-Simon Ballanche (1776-1847) French poet and thinker, along with
DeMaistre, Bonald, and others one of creators of a Catholic traditionalist
philosophy of history. He wrote An Essay Concerning Social Palingenesis in
1828.

papers is an extract of a letter from Baron d'Eckstein who sent it along with his
brochure Analysis of the Kachaka-Upanischat dated "22 December, 1835."

Letter to Ivan Iakushkin, May 2 (1836)
1. From the Dashkov collection 93-3-1361 manuscript section Institute of
Russian Literature (Pushkin House), Leningrad, published by Dmitrii
Shakhovskoi in Dekabristy i ikh Vrem'ia (The Decembrists and their Time) Vol.
II, Moscow, 1932, 183-185; MRT edition, 69-70. Gershenzon wrongly dated
this letter October 19, 1837. The letter evidently never reached Iakushkin who
was living in exile in Siberia.  Iakushkin was ordered transferred from
Petrovsky to Ialutorovsk on April 16, 1836.  His mother-in-law Nadezhda
Sheremeteva took care of his correspondence.   It appears that she did not
forward Chaadaev's letter to Iakushkin, probably because she didn't know
exactly where he was.  Ivan Dmitrievich Iakushkin (1793-1857) founding
member of the Union of Salvation, leader of the Decembrist uprising, university
friend of Chaadaev, had been exiled to Siberia in 1825.
2. The first volume of Antoine Cesar Becquerel's Traite de l'electricite et du
Magnetisme published in 1834.
3. Catherine Gavrilovna Levashev, born Rechetova, died in 1839, Chaadaev's
cousin. Towards the end of 1833 Chaadaev moved into a wing of the Levashev
mansion on Novaia Basmannaia St. in Moscow and resided there all his life.
She and Chaadaev were good friends. They wrote letters to one another even
though they lived in the same house.
4. Antoine Cesar Becquerel (1788-1878) French physicist and Georges Leopold
Chretien Frederic Dagobert, Baron of Cuvier (1796-1832) a comparative
zoologist and paleontologist who believed in the liberal interpretation of Genesis.
On the relations between Chaadaev and Cuvier see V. E. V, 1871, 12. See also
Quenet, 212-213.
5. Cuvier (1769-1832) zoologist and paleontologist who adhered to a literal
interpretation of Genesis.
6.  Chaadaev referred to the Decembrist Uprising of 1825 as "an immense
calamity which set us back half a century" -in -his "First Philosophical Letter"
(Rouleau edition, 56).

Letter to Myself in the manner of Michael Orlov (1836)
 1. Shakhovskoi Archive 334-418. Another copy is in the Dashkov Collection
93-3-1358. Textual differences are minimal; published in MTR, 72. Michael
Fedorovich Orlov (1788-1842) veteran of the Napoleonic Wars, friend of
Pushkin, active member of the Union of Public Welfare. After the Decembrist
uprising tsar Nicholas I declared, "Orlov should be hanged first." But Orlov
escaped that judgment due to the influence of his brother Count Alexis Orlov.
2.  Chaadaev's "First Philosophical Letter" appeared in Russian translation
towards the end of September 1836 in the journal Telescope. On October 24
Alexander Turgenev wrote to Peter Viazemskii that Chaadaev was in the midst of
writing against himself in accordance with the ideas of Orlov (Ostaf efski Arkhiv
kniiazei Viazemskikh, Vol. III, St. Petersburg, 1899,336). This letter remained
unfinished.

Letter to Michael Orlov, 1837
1.  Pypin archive 250-535, another copy in the Manuscript Section, Lenin
Library, Moscow, no. 1032; published in MRT, 74-75. Concerning Orlov see
Letter to Myself, October (1836).
2. Chaadaev noted that one of the most remarkable characteristics of Christianity
was "the elevation of hope to the rank of virtue and its placement on a par with
faith and love"  (See The Journal of Herzen dated March 5, 1844 in Polnoe
Sobranie (Complete Works) Il, 339).
3. See above in The Fragments, "Christian immortality is life with the least
death; not at all a life after death, as people imagine." In his letter of November
15,1846 to Samarin Chaadaev wrote "Fortunately life does not end on the day
of death; it begins again the day after." (Gershenzon edition I, 277).

Letter to A.  I. Turgenev, 1837
1. Turgenev archive 2684, published in the Gershenzon edition, I, (213-217).
This letter was apparently not mailed to Turgenev.
2. Ekaterina Alexandrovna here K[aterina] A[leksandrovna] Sverbeeva (1808-
1892), born princess Shcherbatova, intimate friend of Chaadaev. Her literary
salon in Moscow was one of the main intellectual centers. Alexander Turgenev
called her "Recamier-Sverbeeva" (0. A. IV, 195,206) thereby comparing her to
the famous Parisian literary salon of Madame Recamier.
3. L'Af!aire de Rome by Felicite Robert de Lamennais  (Paris,  1836).
Lamennais (1782-1854) was a famous French Catholic apologist.  His best-
seller Essai sur !'indifference en matiere de religion had a strong influence on
Chaadaev.  But during the 1830's when Lamennais drifted into liberalism,
Chaadaev became disenchanted with his views. Lamennais edited the liberal
Catholic journal L'Avenir from October 16, 1830 to November 15, 1831. He
went to Rome to plead his case but the Pope condemned some of his views in
Mirari Vos a papal encyclical of August 15, 1832. Lamennais published his
"radical" Paroles d'un Croyant in 1834 in which he pleaded for democracy. The
papal encyclical Singulari Nos condemned the Paroles. Lamennais drifted out of
the official church. Liberal Catholicism was crushed.
4. To whom it may concern.
5. What does the people have to do with reason?
6. the golden mean
7. An article by Peter Viazemskii in French about a fire in the Winter Palace, first
published in Gazette de France at the end of 1837 or beginning of 1838 and then
printed in a separate brochure anonymously "/ncendie duPalaisd'HiveraSaint-
Petersboug."  (Paris, 1838).
8.  [I mean prose, the poet is exceptional anywhere.]
9. The efficacy of eloquence lies in its effect upon the listener.
10. Peter Viazemskii.

Letter to Ivan Iakushkin, October 30 (1838)
 1. Published in Gershenzon edition (I, 208-213) as a letter to A. 1. Turgenev.
But Dmitrii Shakhovskoi proved that it was in reality a letter of October 30,1838
to Iakushkin.  (See Shakhovskoi "Iakushkin i Chaadaev" in Dekabristy i ikh
Vrem'ia, 193-194).
2. Iakushkin's article "Shto Takoe Zhizn?" (What is Life?).
3. Chaadaev criticizes Iakushkin's views about the essential difference between
church teaching and science. He attributes Iakushkin's mistakes to the fatal
influence of the 18th century Enlightenment.
4.  For example, that of Pierre Ballanche (1776-1846), the French religious
thinker.

Gagarin of 1840 in our book). Concerning Meshcherskaia see Letter of May 1,
1835 to A. 1. Turgenev.

Letter to Ivan Gagarin, October 1 (1840)
1.  Gagarin archive, Box A, Bibliotheque Slave, Centre d'Etudes Russes,
Meudon, France.  Copy in the Shakhovskoi archive 334-322, published in
MRT, 36.  Prince Ivan Sergeevich Gagarin (1814-1882), diplomat at the
Russian Mission in Munich from 1833 to 1835. Chaadaev sent Pushkin a copy
of his "First Philosophical Letter" via Gagarin.(See Gershenzonedition, I,
198). Gagarin left Russia in 1843 to become a Catholic and a Jesuit. In writing
to Iurii Samarin about his own conversion to Catholicism Gagarin revealed: "The
foundation of it was laid by Peter Iakovlevich Chaadaev on Basmannaia Street in
1835 or 1836 ... " Gagarin Archive, Letter to Samarin dated January 18-30,
1866, Slavic Library, Center of Russian Studies, Meudon, France.  Gagarin
later became the first editor of Chaadaev's works when he published Oeuvres
Choisies de Pierre Tchaadafev (Paris-Leipzig, 1862).
2.  Ivan Pavlovich Galakhov (1809-1849) friend of Herzen, Ogarev and
Granovsldi.
3. Countess Anastasia de Circourt, born Khliustina, wife of Count Adolphe de
Circourt. She wrote articles in French about Russian literature.
4.  Part of the western provinces of the Russian empire suffered a famine in
1840.
5. Shakhovskoi suggests that this is a veiled reference to the forcible conversion
of the Uniates to the Russian Orthodox Church.

COMMENTARIES AND NOTES                                                         293

Letter to Ivan Gagarin, October 1 (1842)
1. Gagarin archives, Box X, Meudon. Published in the Oeuvres Choisies (Paris
1862), 207-208.  Gagarin's own commentaries on this letter are "This note is
not without importance. The totality of Chaadaev's ideas led him not to admit
that Russia might have had a Catholic past. When I spent my last sojourn in
Moscow, I spoke to him about the marriage of Princes Anna, daughter of
Iaroslav the Great to Henry I, king of France, without indicating any supposed
change of religion or the factor of a mixed marriage. From this followed the
necessity of recognizing that Russia and France belonged to the same church at
that time. Chaadaev did not immediately react, but the next day or the one after it
he wrote me the letter which one has just read." (Oeuvres Choisies 207-208).
2. Anna Iaroslavna, daughter of Iaroslav the Wise (or the Great), wife of Henry
I, king of France and mother of king Philip I. She died in 1075.
3. Iaroslav the Wise (978-1054), Grand Prince of Kiev between 1019 and 1054.
He married Irene-Ingegerde, daughter of the Swedish king.  His sons sought
western European women for marriage; only his third son married a Byzantine
princess. His three daughters became queens, one of Norway, another of
France, as noted above, and a third  daughter married the king of Hungary.
Maris, a sister, became the wife of the Polish king.
4. Nestor (c.1056-after 1113) author of the "Chronicle of Past Times" according
to tradition, but today  is considered a compiler.
s. Xsiaslav Iaroslavich (1024-1078) Grand Prince of Kiev, eldest son ofIaroslav
the Wise, married the daughter of Boleslas II, king of Poland. After the death of
Iaroslav he ruled in Kiev. Chased out of Kiev by his own brothers, he appealed
to Pope Gregory VII (Hildebrand) to mediate his dispute with his brother
Sviatoslav (1073). In 1075 Iasiaslav went to Rome and put the Russian lands
under the protection of St. Peter. Isiaslav,in .seeking to .secure his .throne,
appealed several times to the Pope, the Polish king and the German emperor for
help; finally in 1077 Isiaslav secured the throne only to die in 1078.
6. Antonia Rimlianina was Saint Anthony the Roman (1067-1147). He left his
rich Roman parents and friends in order to take refuge in the desert, according to
tradition.  Navigating to Novgorod supposedly  '.'on a rock," he founded a
monastery on the shores of the Volkov river. He died on August 13,1147. The
ruins of the monastery of Saint Anthony the Roman are still evident at Novgorod
today.
7. A letter written in Greek by John II, metropolitan of Kiev (1077-1088) to the
anti-pope Clement ill concerning doctrinal differences between the western and
eastern churches.
8. Sesviatskoie- a village near Moscow, situated near the Triumphal Gate of the
Peterburgian highway near Moscow. The name comes from the Church of All
Saints there.

 1.  First published in the Longinov article in Russkii Vestnik November, 18-62,

 3. The Prussian king Frederich-Wilhelm invited Schelling to occupy the chair of

Letter to Madame E. A. Sverbeeva, July 10 (1842)
 1. Shakhovskoi Archive 334-381, published in Russian translation by Golitsyn
in V. E. April 1918, 240-243; MRT edition, 84-85.
2.  Catherine  Aleksandrovna  Sverbeeva  (1808-1892)  -born  Princess
Shcherbatova, was an intimate friend of Chaadaev. Her salon was one of the
intellectual centers of Moscow; Chaadaev read two of his "Philosophical Letters"
there in 1836. (See Gershenzon edition, I, 196). Alexander Turgenev called her
"Recamier-Sverbeeva" after the famous Madame Recamier who organized one of
the leading salons in Paris.  (0. A. 195,206).
3.  Rozhestveno:  the Shcherbatov property in the Serpukhov region of the
Moscow district.
4. Sinaniary or Sinamary: Chaadaev called his aunt's property of Alekseevskoe
by this name.
5.  Countess Evdokiia Petrovna Rostopchina (1811-1858) born Sushkova, a
poetess, and wife of Andrew Fedorovich Rostopchina (1813-1892) son of the
famous governor of Moscow since 1833.
6. In his article "About Rural Conditions" which appeared in the sixth issue of
The Muscovite in 1842 Khomiakov referred to two -ancient Muscovite
institutions, the ordinary village commune (mir) and the repartitional commune
(polovnichestvo ).
7. Xavier Marmier (1809-1892) French writer who journeyed to Russia in 1842
and wrote a travel book Letters Concerning Russia, Finland and Poland (Paris
1843).
8. The quotation is Chaadaev's French translation. See A. Khomiakov Polnoe
Sobranie Sochinenii III, Moscow, 1911, 64 for the original Russian quoted
later in this letter.
9.  Catherine Nikolaevna Orlova (1797-1885) born Raevskaiia, a friend of
Pushkin and Chaadaev, wife of Michaael Orlov who died March 14, 1842.
10. Prince Alexander N. Golitsyn, patron of Alexander Turgenev.
11. Natal'ia Shakhovskaia.
12. Elizabeta Shcherbatova.
13.  Perhaps a daughter of Princess E. P. Dolgorukova (1800-1863) born
Golitsyna, wife of Prince Paul Petrovich Dolgorukov (1787-1845).

3. Reference to Khomiakov's article "On Rural Conditions."
4. Alexander Sergeevich Menshikov (1785-1868), admiral and diplomat, future

Letter to T. (Alexander Turgenev) (before 1843)
1. Pypin archive 250-538 (old no. 3475-1 and 3476-3). Copy in Shakhovskoi
archive 334-402; MRT edition, 87-92, the Russian translation by Shakhovskoi
was published by L. N. Chertkov in Russian Literature # III (1969) 118-123,
and republished in the Tarasov edition, 259-266; -the original French· text was
first published by Raymond McNally in Studies in Soviet Thought 1986, 332-
339. Alexander Ivanovich Turgenev (1784-1845), writer, literary intermediary
between the Russian intelligentsia and Europe.  The second son of Ivan
Petrovich Turgenev.  After studying at Moscow Univesity he entered the
Archives of Foreign Affairs in 1800. Towards the end of January 1802 he went
to Petersburg and in July left Russia to study in Germany at the University of
Gottingen for two years. Upon returning to Russia in 1806 he got a post at the
Ministry of Justice. From 1806 to 1824 he worked in various governmental
jobs.  He was a friend of Pushkin, Karamzin, Zhukhovskii, and P. A.
Viazemskii. July 8, 1825 upon his doctor's advice Alexander Turgenev left for
Europe where he lived all his life, aside from trips to Russia. He knew Goethe,
Humboldt, Walter Scott, Lamartine, Chateau briand, Victor Hugo, Balzac and
Schelling. Alexander knew Chaadaev since 1820.
2. The allusion is to Madame Catherine Sverbeeva, though Zhikharev transcribes
"Monsieur" rather than "Madame."
3. On March 23, 1843 Madame Sverbeeva wrote to Chaadaev that she had
received "a monstrous letter from our friend T." which she attributed to the
degenerate influence of his "Parisian existence." She sent a copy of the letter to
Chaadaev (Lenin Library, Manuscript Section,  Fond 102 - no. 1032-57.
Concerning the quarrel between' Turgenev and Chaadaev see Gershenzon, (I,
247).
4. Chaadaev's characterization of Alexander Turgenev is almost the same as that
written by Viazemskii, op. cit. 333-335.
5. Prince A. N. Golitsyn (1773-1844), Turgenev's patron, Procuror-in-Chiefof
the Holy Synod and then Minister of Public Education. He was forced to resign
in 1842 and so was Turgenev soon after that.
6. A reft<rence to Turgenev's philanthropic aid for prisoners which Viazemskii
and others ridiculed.
7. The death of E. A. Zubova, a dear friend of Madame .sverbeeva, took place
on August 17, 1843.
8. Like many of his contemporaries Turgenev often accused Chaadaev of being
vain.

--- PDF page 299 / 323 ---

296                               FRAGMENTS, ARTICLES AND OTHER LETTERS

 9. Chaadaev refers to his ideas expressed in "The First Philosophical Letter":  "If
we wish to provide ourselves with an attitude similar to that of other civilized
people, we must, in a way, repeat the entire education of humanity in our land."
(See Rouleau edition, 51).
 10. In his "Apologia of a Madman" Chaadaev declared that even before Peter the
Great wished to make Russia a part of Europe "over three hundred years ago
Russia aspired to become fused with the West." (See Rouleau edition, 201).
 11. The "historical school of jurisprudence" founded by Friedrich Karl von
Savigny (1779-1861) which identified the law of nations with their traditions
and their Volksgeist.  Slavophil ideas were close to those of this "historical
school."
 12. Chaadaev deplored the influence· of Hegelianism, "this-elastic philosophy"·on
the Slavophils.
 13.  In several of his articles and in his July 1848 letter to Tiutchev Chaadaev
referred to the Russian inclination towards submission and sacrifice.
 14. See Karamzin. History of the Russian State, Vol. I, St. Petersburg 1842,
 132.
 15. Compare these words with those in the October 19, 1836 letter of Pushkin to
Chaadaev in which he wrote:  "It is Russia, it is its immense expanse which
absorbed the Mongol conquest.  The Tatars did not dare cross our western
frontiers and turn their backs on us.  They retired into their deserts, and
Christian civilization was saved ... Are not the wars of Oleg and Sviatoslav and
even the appanage wars like this life of bubbling adventure and sharpened
aimless activity which is characteristic to the youth of all people? The Tatar
invasion is a great, sad story. The awakening of Russia, the development of her
power, her march toward unity (Russian unity, of course), the two Ivans, the
sublime drama begun at Uglich and terminated at the Ipatief monastery - what, is
all that not history but a pale, half-forgotten dream?,'Pushkin never sent this
letter of October 19th to Chaadaev but on December 15 he read it to Alexander
Turgenev.  It is very probable that Turgenev communicated the contents to
Chaadaev, since the words here sound like a reply not only to the Slavophils but
also to Pushkin (See Pushkin's Works Vol X, 866).  In the authoritative
academic edition Polnoe Sobranie Sochinenii in 16 volumes, Moscow,
Leningrad 1937~1949, letters 1267 and 1267a.  For commentary see V. E.
Vatsyro, Pushkin.Pis'ma iz Poslednnykh Let, (Pushkin. Letters of his Last
Years 1834-1837), Leningrad, 1969,486-87.
16. Ivan IV "the Awesome" was married seven times in violation of orthodox
religious practices. His illegitmate son, Dmitrii, killed at Uglich in 1591 was
born from his last wife, Maria Nagoi.
17.  In 1564 Ivan IV left Moscow for the Alexandrovskaia Sloboda, a village
about 60 miles from Moscow where there was a monastery. The boyars and the
people begged him to return to Moscow. He did but began in February 1565 the
Oprichnina, a kind of special part of his realm housing his personal guards.
This reforrn was accompanied by a reign of terror.
18. Chaadaev refers to Khomiakov's article "Concerning Rural Conditions."

19. The quotation is exact.
20. In his "Second Philosophical Letter" Chaadaev insisted upon the important
differences between Russian and European serfdom.  In a letter dated March
1820, Nicholas Turgenev recalled conversations with Chaadaev about serdom in
which Chaadaev blamed it on governmental actions. (R. A. 1872, 1205-1207).
21. As the great Russian historian Vasilii Kliuchevskii put it "the history of
Russia is the history of a country which colonized"  Kliuchevskii, Sobranie
Sochinenii Moscow, 1956, I, 31.
22.  Ironic reference to Ivan IV the Terrible who issued decrees limiting the
movement of Russian peasants.
23. Tsar Fedor Ivanovich, son of Ivan the Terrible became tsar in 1584 but real
power rested with the boyar Boris Godunov who was later elected tsar in 1598.
See Chaadaev's Letter of 1844 to Khomiakov.  (Gershenzon edition, I, 248-
249).

Letter to Count Adolphe de Circourt, Jan. 15 (1845)
1. Pypin archive 250-528 (old no. 3457-2) another version in Russian
translation in V. E. July 1874,91-95 and also there in 1900,472-476; MRT
edition, 102-106.  Gershenzon published a version close to the one here
(Gershenzon edition I, 253-258; his Russian translation appeared in his edition
n, 244-249, republished in the Tarasov edition 272-276. Adolphe Marie-Pierre,
Count of Circourt (1801-1879) French diplomat, writer.  Entered Ministry of
Interior in 1822 and in 1830 the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, but quit in that
same year after the July Revolution of Louis Philippe. He went to Geneva
where he met Madame Khliustina, born Tolstoia, and married her daughter
Anastasiia in September, 1830. They lived in Italy for three years and in 1835
undertook a long trip to Germany and to Russia.  They then set up fixed
residence in Paris by 1837 and had a salon frequented by strangers. Along with
Alexander Turgenev Circourt was the one to whom Chaadaev liked to write
about his philosophical views.  Circourt published "Tableau de la litterature
russe" in the Revue /ranraise et etrangere in 1838 and "Lettres sur un voyage en
Prusse et en Russie in Bibliotheque Universelle in 1844.
2.Chaadaev translated Filaret's sermon for the sanctification of a church in
Moscow given on December 23, 1843; part of it was published in the September
25, 1844 edition of the French Catholic periodical Le Semeur. (See the letter of
M.  I. Zhikharev to A. N. Pypin, January 25, 1876 in the N. K. Pisanov
collection 496-3-13, Institute of Russian Literature, Moscow).  Filaret, Vasilii
MikhailovichDrozdov (1782-1867), archbishop of Moscow after 1826.
3. During the winter and spring of 1844-1845, Prof. S. Shevyrev presented a
series of public lectures on Russian literature.
4. See Herzep's commentaries (Herzen, op. cit., n, 1118).
5. Alexis Khomiakov and Ivan Kireevskii supported the same idea.  (See I.
Kireevsky Sqchineniia, I, 113).
6. The Russian Primary Chronicle recounts the legendary coming of the Apostle
St. Andrew to Russia.

 2. Chaadaev translated Khomiakov's article "The Opinions of Foreigners about

 3.  Nicholas Aleksandrovich Melgunov (1804-1867) writer and intermediary
 between Russia and the West.
 4. Aleksandrovich Danilovich Menshikov (1673-1729), a favorite of Peter the
 Great, ruled de facto under Catherine I (1725-1727) the surviving wife of Peter
 the Great and also at the outset of the reign of Peter II. He was banished in

 5. Peter II, emperor of Russia (1727-1730) but the Dolgorukis, Menshikov's

Moscow, the capital under Peter II.
 6. Ernst-Johann Biron or Biren, lover and political favorite of Empress Anna
Ivanovna (1730-1740).
 7. Elizabeth I, daughter of Peter the Great, Empress of Russia (1741-1761).
 8. Catherine the Great (1762-1796)
 9. Socrates, church historian called The Scholastic, born in Constantinople about
 379, died in 440. He continued the Church History of Eusebius and recounted
 the period from 306 to 439 A.D.
 10.  St. Anthanasius, illustrious Church Father, born about 295 in Alexandria,
 di~d in 373. He wrote his Apologia to Emperor Constance as a reply to
 acC(usations made against himself. Constance II, Roman emperor, born in 317,
di6d in 361; he meddled in theological controversies and was essentially a
 caesaropapist.
 11. Theodosius the Great (c. 346-395), Roman general in Spain, became eastern
emperor in 379; he fortified the Christian religion -as the -official Roman state
 religion.
 12.  St. Ambrose, Latin Father of the Church, archbishop of Milan, born at
 Treres around 340, died in 397. Ambrose forbade Emperor Theodosius from
 entering Milan Catherdral after the emperor's massacre in Thessalonica in 390
 and admitted the emperor to communion only after a lengthy repentance.
 13.· [See what Metropolitan Platon says in his history of our church concerning

 tendency toward abdication."
 15.  During the reign of Alexis Romanov (1645-76) the Law Code of 1649
 formalized serfdom and Patriarch Nikon in effect shared the throne with the tsar.

Letter to Fedor Ivanovich Tiutchev, May 10 (1847)
1. Pypin archive 250-543 (old no. 3483-28), copy in Shakhovskoi archive 334-
404 published in Russian translation by Shakhovskoi in Literaturnoe Nasledstvo
XIX-XXI, 1935, 587-589 republished in the Tarasov edition, 290-292 in

--- PDF page 304 / 323 ---

COMMENTARIES AND NOTES                                                           301


French by Francois Rouleau in Plamia no. 68, Oct. 1985, Meudon, France, 43-
44; MRT edition, 121-122.  Fedor Ivanovich Tiutchev (1803-1873) famous
Russian poet was born on his family property at Ustug, about 200 miles from
Moscow.  After tutoring in French, Russian orthodox piety and patriotism at
home, he entered Moscow University in 1819. After completing his studies he
entered the diplomatic service in 1821. From 1822 to 1837 he worked at the
Russian embassy in Munich. He left diplomatic service in 1844 and returned to
Moscow.  Tiutchev and Chaadaev were friends especially after 1844.
Concerning Chaadaev Tiutchev wrote: "The man whom I contradict the most is
the one I love best" (Zhikharev V. E. 1871,40, no. 1). He and Chaadaev had
often heated discussions. Chaadaev loved to debate Tiutchev especially at the
English Club in Moscow, where once the debate got so heated that the head
waiter rushed to separate them, since he feared that they might come to blows.
Tiutchev was a perfect foil for Chaadaev, a Slavophile poet with philosophical
insights. Ivan Gagarin called Tiutchev "an orthodox Joseph de Maistre" (Kirill
Pigarev F. I. Tiutchev i ego vremiia Moscow, 1978,318). Since Tiutchev had
spent twenty years abroad, Ivan Aksakov called him "a Russian emigrant from
Europe."
    This letter reflects Chaadaev's severe psychological crisis which began in
1846. He fell into a deep depression, hypochondria and even contemplated
suicide.  See Richard Tempest "Chaadaev and Tiutchev" Studies in Soviet
Thought Vol. 32, no. 4, (1986) 383-395.  Chaadaev's letter circulated in
society, but in February, 1848 Viazemskii had still not received it himself and
asked Chaadaev for it. (See Starina i Novizna, I, 1897,210.
2. Tiutchev acknowledged receipt of Chaadaev's portrait in a letter dated April
13,1847 (R.A. X(1900), 411-412).
3. Viazemskii's article "Iazykov -- Gogol" appeared on April 24-25, 1847 in the
Sanktpetersburgskie Vedomosti, (See Gershenzon edition, I, 279-285).
4. An ironic reference to the polemic between Granovskii and Khomiakov about
the Burgundians and the Franks.  Chaadaev viewed the entire debate 'as -a
tempest in a teapot. See the note of Shakhovskoi in Literaturnoe Nasledstvo
XIX, 1935, 592.
5. The decision of Frederich Wilhelm, king of Prussia, to convene the Landtag.
6. Reference to Khomiakov's position as the head of the Slavophils.

Letter to Princess Catherine Aleksandrovna Dolgorukova, Jan. 22
(1848)
 1. (The first of "Two Letters Concerning Religion"). Pypin archive 250-522
(old no. 3445-7) and archives of S. T., K. S. and A. S. Aksakovykh 3-20-117;
MRT edition, 125-128. Russian translation published by Richard Tempest in
Voprosy filosofii XII (1983) and the French original in Studies  in Soviet
Thought 32-4 (1986). Shakhovskoi identified "Princes D" as being Ekaterina
A. Dolgorukova, (1803-1852) wife of the Decembrist Ileia Andrevich
Dolgoruky (1798-1848).  (See Shakhovskoi introduction to the 'Philosophical
Letters' in Literaturnoe Nasledstvo XXII-XIV, 1935, 9.)  The differences
between the Pypin copy and that of the Aksakov archive are minimal. The
Aksakov copy is signed "Michel Chirpounofsky," a mock reference to the fact
that Chaadaev's brother lived on the Khirpunovo property in the Nizhnii
Novgorod region.  Chaadaev's letter appears to be an answer to Tiutchev's
article "The Papacy and the Roman Question." Tiutchev referred to the events of
1849 in Rome when in January of that year under the leadership of Garibaldi and
Mazzini the Republic was proclaimed leading to the intervention of the French
expeditionary force in July.  Tiutchev's article was written in October of 1849
and published in the Revue des deux Mondes in January, 1850.  Chaadaev
probably read it in an earlier version.
2.  St. John 18.36.
3. At the council of Florence on July 6, 1439 the western Roman Catholic and
the eastern Byzantine Orthodox Churches were reunited. Among the signers
were the Pope Eugene IV, the Emperor of Constantinople John VII, the
Patriarch of Constantinople Joseph II, and the archbishop of Moscow, Isidore of
Kiev. But it had no practical effect.
4. This phrase refers to Tiutchev's article "The Roman Question" published in
the Revue des deux mondes in 1849. Chaadaev read the manuscript before it
was published.  In it Tiutchev wrote "Jesus Christ said 'My Kingdom is not of
this world;' well then, one must question why Rome, after becoming separated
from the unity, believed that it had the right to organize a kingdom of Christ like
a kingdom of the world in terms of an interest which it identified with the very
interest of Christianity as a whole."  (Bykov iedition, 354).  See Richard
Tempest "Chaadaev and Tiutchev" Studies 'in Soviet Thought Vol. 32, no. 32,
November 1986, 382-395.

--- PDF page 306 / 323 ---

COMMENTARIES AND NOTES                                                        303


S. Patriarch Nikon who asserted the superiority of the church over the state in
Russia during the reign of Alexis Romanov (1645-76).
6. The theme of "the kingdom of God on earth" is at the heart of Chaadaev's
thinking.  See Rouleau edition 45, 46, 58, 62, 89, 148, 186, 199, 273. The
phrase the "Thy Kingdom Come" (Adveniat regnum tuum) from the Lord's
Prayer is cited at the outset of "The First Philosophical Letter', 'The Apologia of
a Madman', and in many other of his works and letters.
7.  Peter II (Peter Petrovich) of Montenegro (1813-1851) prince-bishop was
consacrated at St. Petersburg in 1830.

 1. Pypin archive 250-536 (old numbering 3470 and 3472-21); MRT edition,

 2. Poltoratskii ordered ten lithograph portraits of Chaadaev in Paris and sent six

 S. Count A. A. Zakrevskii (1796-1865) See below Letter of January 15, 1854
to Circourt.
 6. The "Shcherbatovs" refers notably to A. G. Shcherbatov (1777-1848)

 7.  Monsieur Jourdain, the main character in Moliere's play Le bourgeois
gentilhomme, who ironically spoke in prose without heingconscious of .thatfact
 until he was told so.
 8.  Catherine Muravieva (1771-1848), mother of the Decembrists Nikita and

 9. :Sophia Muravieva (1826-1892) called familiarly "Nonushka."  After the
Russian government officially took away noble titles from her father Nikita, the
family name was changed to Nikitin.
 10. Elie Mikhailovich Bibikov (died in 1861).
 11. Famous penal colony in Australia; the reference here is to Siberian exile.
Sophia Muravieva was born in Chito, Siberia.
 12. E.!. Iakushkin (1826-1905), later became a jurist and ethnographer.
 13.  Especially his father Ivan D. Iakushkin (1793-1857) the Decembrist. See

Letter to an Unknown (Fedor Tiutchev) July (1848)
1.  Pypin archive 250-553; MRT edition, 131-133, published in Russian
translation by Shakhovskoi in Literaturnoe Nasledstvo, XIX-XXI, 1935,589-
590, republished in the Tarasov edition, 294-296.
2.  Tiutchev's article "Russia and the Revolution" dated April 12, 1848 was
published in Paris in 1849. Chaadaev read it during the summer of 1848 before
its publication.  It frrst circulated as a memorandum to Nicholas I; but the tsar did
not like it and would not allow it to be published in Russia.
3. "For a long time there have been only two real powers in Europe: Revolution
and Russia ... The life of one is the death of the other."  (P. Byrov edition, 1913,
344).
4. "The Russian people are Christian .. .It is this capacity for renunciation and for
sacrifice which forms the very basis of its moral nature." Ibid.
s. According to Chaadaev, Peter the -Great -had -the approbation -and support of
the Russian people.
6.  In "Russia and the Revolution," Tiutchev held that Nicholas I was the
sovereign in whom Russian thought was incarnated.
7. In 1589 under the reign of Fedor Ivanovich, Boris Godunov arranged that the
chief of the Russian Church receive the title of Patriarch.

Letter to Princess Catherine Dolgorukov, Oct. 6, 1850
1. Second Letter to Princess D. in the series of "Two Letters to Princess D.
concerning Religion." Pypin archive 250-522 (old no. 3445-2); MRT edition,
149-152, published in Russian translation in Simvol no. 20, 1988. An
anonymous criticism of this letter is in the Gershenzon editon, (I, 373-77).
2.  Henri-Auguste-Georges, marquis de la Rochjaquelin (1805-1867), French
politician. Elected deputy from Morbihan in 1842, he became one of the leaders
of the legitimist party who wanted to reconcile monarchy with the sovereignty of
the people. After the Revolution of 1848 he rallied to the cause of the Republic,
was re-elected deputy from Morbihan, sided with the legitimists and protested
against the coup d'etat by Louis Napoleon in 185l.
3. Famous passage from the sermon of "Petit Careme" by Massillon (1663-
1742) in 1718; Louis XIV was nine years old.
4. Reference to the Church Council of Constantinople held in 381 (the Second
Ecumenical) when the Creed became traditional in the Church, the so-called
Nicene Creed.
s. Pope Leo III (795-816) is the one who crowned Charlemagne emperor of the
West.

Alexander II, heir to the throne.

Article concerning the coup d'etat of Napoleon III (1851)
"1851"
1. Pypin archive contains several versions 250-286; 250-554 (old no. 3496-39)
and 250-555 (old no. 3494-40). Copies in the Shakhovskoi archive 334-266 and
334-269 (the first version is the longer one); MRT edition, 158-162.
Peter Scheibert published a Russian version of the  text, (translated by
Zhikharev) in "Neue Texte von Petr Iakovlevic Caadaev" Zeitschrift jur
slavische philologie XXVII, no. 1 (1959), 413-415.  The French text was
published by Fran~ois Rouleau in Plamia, no. 68, October 1985, 45-48.
Sc~eibert points out some convergences between the critical views of Chaadaev
and those expressed by Karl Marx in his article "The Eighteenth of Brumaire of
Louis Napoleon" published in May 1852 (See Scheibert, op. cit., 51). The
Russian text was republished in Voprosy filosofii, no. 6., 1988, 115-119.
The nephew of Napoleon I Louis-Napoleon Bonaparte (1808-1873) elected
President of the Republic, on February 10, 1848, led a coup d'etat on December
2, 1851. On January 14, 1852 he was appointed President of the Republic for a
decade. On December 2, 1852 Louis-Napoleon became Emperor of the French
with the name Napoleon ill. Since Chaadaev does not refer to this last fact, his
article was probably written at the end of 1851 or during 1852.
2. Richard Cobden (1804-1865) English economist and politician, advocate of
free trade who predicted the abolition of war through the use of juridical courts
of 'arbitration and warned England not to become politically involved in
international conflicts.
3.  Pierre Carlier (1792-1858) prefect of police in Paris after Louis Napoleon
because President in 1848.
4.  J. P. Proudhon (1809-1865) famous socialist and anarchist who for a short
time portrayed Louis Napoleon as the incarnation of the social revolution in his
"The Social Revolution Demonstrated by the Coup d'Etat of December 2"
published in Brussels in 1852.

Letter to Michael Zhikharev, Nov. 1 (1853)
1. Pypin archive 250-518 (old no. 3432-7); MRTedition, 169-170.

--- PDF page 309 / 323 ---

306                               FRAGMENTS, ARTICLES AND OTHER LETTERS


 2.  Reference here to Chaadaev's sojourn in England in August 1823. From
Yarmouth he went to London to spend 4 days and then to Brighton, the
fashionable spa where he took to the sea waters.
3. A place presumably in Moscow.
4. See the Fragments "Fallen in England, etc." In the English county of Sussex,
in which Brighton is located, Chaadaev stay.ed inacottage.there.
5. Ignace Pleyel (1757-1831) Austrian composer, naturalized French citizen,
student of Haydn.
6.  Caroline of Brunswick (1768-1821), wife of George IV, king of England,
who started unsuccessful divorce proceedings against her for adultery, after he
came to the throne. This led to a scandalous trial in 1820.
7. Tilbury - a light, open, one-horse carriage popular at the time.  Its name came
from that of its English inventor.

Article for L'Univers, Jan. 15 (1854)
1. Pypin archive 250-552 published in Russian translation by Shakhovskoy in
Zven'ia (III-IV), (1934) republished in the Tarasov edition, 318-323, and in
French by Francois Rouleau in Cahiers du monde russe et sovihique XV (3-4)
1974,409-413; MRT edition, 173-176.  Chaadaev wrote this article for the
French Catholic journal L'Univers. He liked expressing his critical opinions
about as if he were a foreigner. Furthermore, as Shakhovskoi suggests the very
title may refer to Chaadaev's views as "not nationalistic but worldwide, i.e.,
universal. "
    Russia's failures during the Crimean War severely affected Chaadaev. One
month after the fall of Sevastopol, according to D. N. Sverbeev in a letter to his
wife, "This is what Chaadaev says, and I don't think that he lied. As you know,
he is now in close relations with Zakrevskii and was once at his place with
(A.F.) Orlov. The latter asked him after a long conversation, "Well, what do
you think, Chaadaev, how will it end?"  Chaadaev answered, "Russia will
remain a second-rate power for a long time because of it." Orlov and Zakrevskii
in unison replied "Perhaps forever."  ("Letter of D. N. Sverbeev to E. A.
Sverbeeva, Sept. 29/0ct. 1, 1855," Bartenev Diary, 385, 390).
    Chaadaev placed a great deal of emphasis upon Russian serfdom, especially
in his "Second Philosophical Letter" and his letter of 1843 to Alexander
Turgenev. To Chaadaev the origins and the continuation of serfdom in Russia
were at the very core of Russia's problems. Russian expansionism was paid for
at the price of extending its form of slavery.
   Two weeks before his death, having seen Alexander II at Zakrevskii's place,
Chaadaev was in a black mood and did not look forward to anything good from
the new tsar.  "Look at him" Chaadaev said pointing to Alexander II,  "it's
basically horrible for Russia. That stupid expression, those wandering eyes!"
    Chaadaev's attitude in this article is as pessimistic as the "Philosophical
Letters."  His intellectual life had come full circle.  Here he again criticizes
Russia not only for being an intellectual vacuum, a "tabula rasa" as contrasted

--- PDF page 310 / 323 ---

COMMENTARIES AND NOTES                                                       307


with the rich history and culture of Europe, but also its leading principle of
slavery represents a threat to Europe.
2. This idea is very characteristic of Chaadaev (see his letter of May 20, 1842 to
Schelling, Gershenzon edition, I, 245, and his "Apologia of a Madman" Ibid, I,
228). Chaadaev refers to the "retrospective utopias "of the official nationalists
and of some of the Slavophils. Ballanche referred to Joseph de Maistre as "a
prophet of the past" (prophete du passe) and Viazemskii compared the
"Slavophils or Russophils" to "archeological liberals." Like Joseph de Maistre,
they belonged to the past. (Viazemskii Aesthetics and Literary Criticism,
Moscow, 1984, 343).

Four Fragments
1. Pypin archive 250-556 (old no. 3498); MRT edition, 177. Reference to the
Crimean War dates these fragments after September 20, 1854, the arrival of the
French army in the Crimea, and the defeat of the Russian army on the Alma
river.

Letter to Count Adolphe de Circourt, Jan. 15 (1854)
1. Pypin archive 250-528 (old no. 3457-13); MRT edition, 178-180. In this text
are ideas expressed in the Fragments and also in the January 15, 1854 article for
L'Univers. The work is a profound satire full of irony, concerning the politics
of tsar Nicholas I.
2. The coup d'etat:  that by Louis Napoleon on Dec. 2,1851.
3. The "Noble Boarding School" (blagorodniipansion).
4. From 1797 to 1801 Vasilii Zhukovskii (1783-1852) had been a student in that
boarding school, and he was a tutor to the future tsar Alexander II. He died in
April 12/24, 1852. Reference here is to the repressive measures taken by the
secret committee of April 21 and the rigorous censorship imposed after the
revolution of 1848.
5.  In 1853 former Kievan general governor, General D. G. Bibikov (1792-
1870) replaced Lev Alexandrovich Perovski (1792-1856) as minister of internal
affairs. The "coalescence for the great .national unity" is .an .allusion to the
forcible incorporation of the Uniates into the official state orthodox church,
6. Abraham Sergeevich Norov (1795-1869) named Minister of Public Education
in 1853.
7. Reference to the "Third Section" (the secret police) and its chief from 1844 to
1856, Alexis Fedorovich Orlov (1768-1861).
8.  Arsenii Andreevich Zakrevskii(1786-1865) son of an impoverished noble
inspector general from 1815 to 1823, governor general of Finland (1823-1831),
made count in 1830, military general governor of Moscow (1849-1859). Only
half-literate all his life, according to Delvig, Zakrevskii treated people like a
despot and tended to "get involved in family matters for the reconciliation of
husbands with their wives and children with their parents...  In a word,
Zakrevskii acted like a good proprietor ·on ·his ·estate." Delvig Polveka ·russkii
zhizn (A Half a Century of Russian Life) II, 11-14.

--- PDF page 311 / 323 ---

308                               FRAGMENTS, ARTICLES AND OTHER LETTERS

    Chaadaev exercises his usual irony here:  Zakrevskii was married to
Agratina Fedorovna, born Tolstoi (1799-1879). Their daughter, Countess Lydia
Nesselrode (1826-1884) was infamous for her innumerable love affairs,
according to Delvig op. cit IT, 94-95.
9. Reference to the Directory 1795-1799 between the French Revolution and
Napoleon: the epoch of the marvelous-and the unbelievable.

Letter to Michael Zhikharev (1854)
1. Pypin archive 250-518 (old no. 3435-1), (1854) copy Shakhovskoi archive
334-330; MRT edition, 181.
2. The crisis over the "Eastern Question" and the resultant Crimean War (1854-
1856).  It was Tsar Nicholas I who branded the Ottoman Empire as "a sick man"
(See the Parliamentary Papers. Accounts and Papers Vol. XXXI, pt. 5, Eastern
Papers, p. 2, Sir G. H. Seyman to Lord Russell, January 11, 1853, "We have
on our hands a sickman --a -gravely sickman" Oxford Dictionary of Quotations
3rd ed. (1980), 362.

Letter of an Unknown to an Unknown (Female)
1.  Pypin archive 250-556 (old no. 3498-41); MRT edition, 182-183.  The
original was found among the papers of Catherine Nikolaevna Orlova, widow of
Chaadaev's close friend Michael Odov. Gershenzon published another version
(I, 309-310) and his Russian translation (IT, 280-282) was republished in the
Tarasov edition, 305-307. Gershenzon claimed that this letter was part of an
essay entitled "Concerning Political Life in Russia" which circulated in St.
Petersburg in 1855 and was attributed to Chaadaev.  (See the Gershenzon
edition, (I, 424) and the memoire of Senator K. N. Lebedev in Russkii Arkhiv
(1893), no. 3, 285-286).
2. Reference to Tiutchev's idea that the Russian empire was the inheritor of the
Byzantine and Roman empires and hence had the mission of absorbing all the
Slavs.
3. Copernicus - discovery of "a poor little solar system."
4. Even though the Slavophils supported the ancient principle of autocracy and
wanted no participation of the Russian people in political decision making, the
goverment was suspicious of them.  After the publication in 1852 of the
Slavophil Collection by Ivan Aksakov, the government forbade any further
Slavophil publications. Furthermore, Aksakov, who had begun dressing like a
common Russian peasant, was forbidden from doing so any further. He was
also compelled to shave off his beard, since the government considered it
unsuitable for a nobleman.
5. The text was thus written after the debarkation of the allies in the Crimea; the
battle of Alma on Sept. 20, 1854.

Letter to Michael Zhikharev, Nov. 3 "(1855)
1. Written before September of 1855, Pypin archive 250-519 (old no. 3435-7),
copy in Shakhovskoi archive 334-330; MRT edition, 185-187.

COMMENTARIES AND NOTES                                                      309


2. Chaadaev liked to play the role of pedantic grammarian in some of his letters
to his nephew Michael Zhikharev.
3. The goverment or possibly Zakrevskii, governor-general of Moscow.
4. Shevyrev or Pogodin.
5. Zhikharev's mother.

Letter from Ardatov to Paris (between 1844 and 1855)
 1. Pypin archive 250-558 published in Voprosy filosofii ,no. 6, 1988, 112-115;
MRT edition, 188-190. The article is another example of Chaadaev's irony; his
brother Michael resided in the village of Khripunova in the Ardatov region -of
Nizhnii-Novgorod.
 2. [Ivan Vasil'ev.(ich) Kireevskii]. Ivan Kireevskii tried to obtain governmental
approval to accept the post of editor of the journal Moskovitianin (The
Muscovite) but without success.  Nonetheless, he did accept the function of
editor without the  title and edited the first three issues in 1845 until the
government forbade further publication.
 3. [Aleksei Step.(anovich) Khomiakov].
 4.  Citation from Khomiakov's article "Concerning the Peasant Condition"
published in 1842 in issue 6 of the Moskovitianin, which Chaadaev often -quoted
 in his letters.  (See A. Khomiakov Polnoe Sobranie Sochinenii (Compelte
 Edition), Vol, III, Moscow, 1911,64.
 5. Quotation from the Russian "Chronicle of Past Times" the so-called Nestor
 Chronicle.

Aphorisms and Diverse Remarks (1855)
 1.  Pypin archive 250-551 (old no. 3493-36) first published by Raymond
McNally in the Forschungen zur osteuropiiischen Geschichte, vol. 25, Berlin,
 1978,217-218 and partially by Fran~ois Rouleau in Plamia, vol. 65, 1984,21-
 30.  The Shakhovskoi translation was published in Voprosy Filosofii  1,
 January, 1986, 121-124 and republished in the Tarasov edition, 161-170. The
Zhikharev translation into Russian was published in Simvol no. 15, 1986,274-
 284. The Shakhovskoi translation appeared in Voprosy filosofii,  I, 1986, 121-
 124 and reappeared in the Tarasov edition, 161-170; MRT edition, 191-198.
 2.  Count Joseph de Maistre (1754-1821) Savoyard philosopher, a leading
 Ultramontanist. From 1806 to 1812 de Maistre was sent by the Sardinian king
 to St. Petersburg. He supported papal infallibility and advocated the use of the
pope as an arbitrator in the international affairs of Europe.
 3. Reference to David Strauss' Life of Jesus (Das Leben Jesu). See the letter to
Turgenev, Gershenzon edition, I, 194.
 4. According to Zhikharev these words refer to Chaadaev's arch enemy Philip
Vigel. (Zhikharev "Chaadaev." V. E. Article IT, 23, note 1.

Published Texts

       Gagarin, I. S. ed., Oeuvres choisies de Pierre Tchadai"ef, publiees pour la
premiere fois par Ie Pere Gagarine de la compagnie de Jesus. Libraire A. Franck,
Paris-Leipzig. Letters I, VI, and VII, a late version of the "Apologia of a Madman"
and a few private letters.

       Gershenzon, M. 0., ed., Sochineniia  i pis'ma P. Ia. Chaadaeva, 2 vols.,
Moscow 1913-1914. Letters I, VI and VII and a late version of the "Apologia of a
Madman" in vol. I with another version in vol. II, along with several private letters.
The most complete old edition in French and Russian.

      McNally, Raymond, ed., "Chaadaev's Philosophical Letter Written to a
Lady and his Apologia of a Madman" Forschungen  zur  osteuropiiischen
Geschichte, vol. XI, (Osteuropa-Institut an der Freien Universitat Berlin) Berlin,
1966,24-128. The first complete publication of all eight "Philosophical Letters"
and an early version of "The Apologia of a Madman" in the original French.

      McNally, Raymond, Rouleau Fran~ois, et Tempest Richard, Pierre
Tchaadaev. Lettres lnedites ou Rares, Centre d'Etudes Russes, Meudon, France,
1990. The most complete and up-to-date edition of Chaadaev's works inCluding
many of his hitherto unpublished articles and letters in both French and Russian.

      Rouleau Fran~ois, ed., Pierre Tchaadaev Lettres philosophiques Libraire
des Cinq Continents:  Paris, 1970. Up-to-date French edition of the 'Philosophical
Letters.'

       Tarasov, Boris, ed., P. la. Chaadaev.Statii i Pis'ma Moscow, 1987. Re-
edition of some of the main works by Chaadaev from the Gershenzon edition plus a
few unpublished letters in Russian; expanded, re-edited, and republished in
Moscow, 1989.

      Voprosy Filosofii Staff, ed. (prilozhenie k zhurnalu) P. la. Chaadaev
Sochineniia. Moscow, 1989.  Republications from the -Gershenzon -edition -plus
some other letters in Russian translation.

        Blinoff, Marthe Life and Thought in Old Russia Pennsylvania State Univ.,
1961. Short extracts.

       Edie, James M.; Scanlan, James P. and Zeldin, Mary-Barbara, with
collaboration of George L. Kline, eds., Russian Philosophy, an Historical
Anthology, 3 vols., Chicago, 1965. A complete translation of Chaadaev's "First
Philosophical Letter" is in vol. I (106-125) and excerpts from Letters III, V, VI and
VII.
